stories.what_happened: Williams confirmed Albon will make his 100th race start for the team at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, per the team's July 22 announcement. He'd already equalled, then broken, Nigel Mansell's record for most starts by a driver with Williams earlier this season (at Monaco and Barcelona respectively), and Hungary makes him the first driver in the team's history to reach triple figures.
key_points: Albon will start his 100th Grand Prix for Williams at this weekend's Hungarian GP
He's the first driver ever to reach a century of starts with the team
He already broke Nigel Mansell's Williams starts record earlier this season
Fewer than 30 drivers in F1 history have hit 100 starts with one constructor

depth.background: Alex Albon joined Williams — one of F1's most storied but recently struggling teams, with champions like Nigel Mansell, Damon Hill, Alain Prost, and Jacques Villeneuve in its history — in 2022, becoming a central figure in its ongoing effort to climb back toward the front of the grid.

stories.title: Tropical Storm Bertha makes landfall near Louisiana — but the damage looks limited so far
stories.what_happened: Tropical Storm Bertha weakened slightly to about 50 mph sustained winds as it crawled west along the northern Gulf Coast, making landfall or grazing southeast Louisiana on Wednesday afternoon. The storm is forecast to pass near New Orleans Wednesday night before continuing toward Houston and dissipating by the end of the week, dropping 1 to 4 inches of rain (isolated pockets up to 6 inches) and pushing storm surge as high as 4 feet in spots.
key_points: Bertha came ashore/grazed the Louisiana coast Wednesday with winds around 50 mph, down from 60 mph the night before
It's expected to track over New Orleans Wednesday night, then head toward Houston before dissipating
Forecasters warn of 1-4 inches of rain (isolated 6in) and storm surge up to 4 feet along the coast
Early reports suggest impacts are milder than initially feared, though flash flooding risk continues

depth.background: Bertha is the second named storm of the 2026 Atlantic hurricane season, which runs June through November. Forecasters had flagged El Niño — a Pacific Ocean warming pattern that tends to increase high-altitude wind shear over the Atlantic — as a factor that could suppress storm formation this year, making Bertha's slow crawl along the Gulf Coast a real-time test of that forecast.

stories.title: Sensex sheds 715 points, Nifty slips below 24,000 for a third straight day as oil spikes and Trump threatens pharma tariffs
stories.what_happened: Indian benchmark indices fell for a third straight session on Wednesday. The Sensex dropped 715 points (0.92%) to close at 76,755, while the Nifty 50 lost 191 points (0.79%) to settle at 23,996 — back below the psychologically important 24,000 level. Renewed US strikes on Iran pushed Brent crude above $92 a barrel, and pharmaceutical stocks were hit hard after President Trump said generic drug imports to the US will keep a zero-tariff rate for two years from August 1, 2026, before jumping to 100% for a year and then 200% after that.
key_points: Sensex down 0.92% to 76,755; Nifty down 0.79% to 23,996 — third losing session in a row
Brent crude above $92/barrel on fresh US strikes on Iran, raising India's import bill
Trump plan to raise tariffs on generic drugs sharply after a 2-year grace period hits pharma stocks hard
A weaker rupee and rising India VIX (a 'fear gauge') add to the risk-off mood

depth.background: India imports roughly 85% of the crude oil it uses, so global oil-price spikes hit its economy harder than most — pricier crude means costlier fuel and imports, a wider trade gap, and pressure on the rupee. India is also one of the world's largest suppliers of generic (off-patent, low-cost) medicines to the US, so US tariff policy on drug imports directly affects a major Indian export industry, including companies like Dr Reddy's and Sun Pharma.

stories.title: Haas benches Bearman for Friday practice — mandatory rookie rule hands his seat to Le Mans winner Hirakawa
stories.what_happened: Haas will run Ryo Hirakawa in place of Oliver Bearman for first practice at the Hungarian Grand Prix, satisfying F1's mandatory young-driver rule. Bearman returns to the car for second practice, qualifying, and the race itself. It's Hirakawa's second FP1 outing for Haas this season.
key_points: Ryo Hirakawa, the 2024 Le Mans 24 Hours co-winner, drives Haas's car in Hungarian GP FP1
Oliver Bearman sits out FP1 but returns for FP2 and the rest of the weekend
It's F1's mandatory rookie rule, not a sign Bearman's seat is in danger
Comes as speculation grows over whether Bearman keeps his Haas seat beyond this year

depth.background: F1 rules require every team to give an FP1 (first practice) session to a driver who has completed two or fewer F1 races over a season, to give young or non-F1 drivers track time in a real car. Ryo Hirakawa is a Japanese sportscar racer best known for co-driving Toyota's winning car at the 2024 Le Mans 24 Hours endurance race, and already has prior Haas FP1 outings this year. Oliver Bearman is Haas's 21-year-old regular driver, on loan from Ferrari's junior program, and is widely tipped to be racing for a third season at Haas in 2026.

stories.title: A ₹5,000 crore highway-investment fund opens for bidding — and investors aren't rushing in
stories.what_happened: Cube Highways Trust's InvIT opened for public subscription on July 22 with a price band of ₹151–152 per unit, aiming to raise ₹5,000 crore by selling 328.95 million units, entirely from existing unit-holders. The minimum bid is 95 units (~₹14,440). By evening on the first day, the issue was subscribed just 0.05 times — meaning only about 5% of the units on offer had bids so far. The issue closes July 24, with listing expected August 3.
key_points: Cube Highways Trust's InvIT IPO opened today, closes July 24
Price band ₹151–152 per unit; minimum investment ~₹14,440
Subscribed just 0.05x on day one — muted early demand
Entire ₹5,000 crore offer is existing owners selling down, not new money for the company

depth.background: An InvIT (Infrastructure Investment Trust) is like a mutual fund for big physical infrastructure — instead of buying shares in a company, investors buy units in a trust that owns income-generating assets like toll roads, and get paid out from the toll revenue those roads collect. Cube Highways Trust owns a portfolio of Indian highways and collects toll income from them. This IPO is an 'offer for sale', meaning the trust's current owners are selling some of their existing units to new investors — the trust itself doesn't receive any of the money raised.

stories.title: Modi launches ₹5,650 crore worth of energy and infrastructure projects in Tamil Nadu's Tiruchirappalli
stories.what_happened: Modi inaugurated and laid foundation stones for around ₹5,650 crore (about $680 million) in projects across Tamil Nadu. The centerpiece is a ₹3,700 crore City Gas Distribution network for the Nilgiris and Erode districts, which will pipe cooking and industrial gas to nearly 9 lakh (900,000) households and businesses. He also dedicated an Indian Oil lube-blending plant in Chennai to the nation, flagged off five new train services including two Amrit Bharat Express trains, inaugurated 370 km of rural roads under the PM Gram Sadak Yojana, and addressed an NDA political rally.
key_points: Modi inaugurated/launched projects worth about ₹5,650 crore in Trichy
Biggest piece: a ₹3,700 crore gas pipeline network for Nilgiris and Erode districts
Also flagged off 5 new trains and 370 km of rural roads
Visit doubled as an NDA political rally in a state where BJP is historically weak

depth.background: Tamil Nadu is run by the DMK, a party that has long kept the BJP at arm's length, making it a state where the central government's infrastructure spending carries an extra political charge ahead of state elections. Modi's government has repeatedly used project launches like this one to build a development narrative in states it doesn't govern.

stories.title: Lewis Hamilton says he 'won't stop' until an 8th title, as his Ferrari turnaround puts him 3rd in the standings
stories.what_happened: Hamilton gave what he called an 'encouraging' verdict on Ferrari's form while acknowledging he was to blame for issues at the recent Belgian Grand Prix weekend, according to Motorsport Week's July 22 report. At the season's halfway point he sits third in the drivers' championship on 147 points — ahead of teammate Leclerc — with one win, five podiums, and points scored in every single round this year, a sharp turnaround from his winless, podium-less 2025 campaign.
key_points: Hamilton sits 3rd in the drivers' championship with 147 points at midseason
He has a win, five podiums, and points in every race this year
That's a big swing from 2025, when he finished 6th with zero podiums
He took blame for recent issues at Spa heading into this weekend's Hungarian GP

depth.background: Hamilton joined Ferrari for 2025 hoping to add to his record-tying seven world titles, but his first season was a disappointment: sixth in the standings, no podiums, and 86 points behind teammate Charles Leclerc. That fueled widespread doubt about whether the pairing would work.

stories.title: Red Bull brings back its 'Macarena' wing after tracing it to Verstappen's crashes
stories.what_happened: Red Bull's technical director Pierre Wache confirmed the team traced Verstappen's two crashes to a mechanical design flaw in the wing's rotating mechanism — not a fundamentally unsafe aerodynamic concept — and has developed a fix. The revised wing is set to return for this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix.
key_points: Red Bull is reintroducing its rotating rear wing, nicknamed the 'Macarena', for Hungary
Engineers found a mechanical (not aerodynamic) fault caused Verstappen's crashes at Red Bull Ring and Silverstone
The wing was parked for the Belgian Grand Prix while the fix was developed
It's designed to boost straight-line speed

depth.background: F1 cars use movable aerodynamic parts sparingly and under strict rules; Red Bull's 'Macarena' wing is a rotating rear-wing design first used at the Miami Grand Prix that shifts to a low-drag mode on straights for extra top speed. After Max Verstappen crashed in qualifying at the Red Bull Ring and again in the race at Silverstone, the team pulled the wing for Belgium while investigating whether the part itself was to blame.

stories.title: Ferrari's Vasseur says Hungary should suit their car — but admits the team is 'at the limit' on engine power
stories.what_happened: Ahead of this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, Vasseur said the Hungaroring's characteristics should theoretically suit Ferrari's car, but cautioned that every team is bringing upgrades to this race, so the real competitive order is unknown until track running starts. He also conceded that Ferrari's engine is effectively 'at the limit' compared to Mercedes' power unit during qualifying, and said he doesn't expect a fix soon — a weakness that keeps hurting Hamilton and Leclerc specifically in one-lap qualifying pace, even if the chassis is competitive.
key_points: Vasseur: Hungaroring's tight, twisty layout should suit Ferrari's SF-26
But he warns everyone is bringing upgrades this weekend
Ferrari's engine is 'at the limit' vs Mercedes in qualifying
No quick fix expected — leaves Hamilton and Leclerc exposed on one-lap pace

depth.background: Ferrari's team principal Fred Vasseur has spent the 2026 season trying to close the gap to Mercedes and McLaren, with Lewis Hamilton and Charles Leclerc as his drivers. The Hungaroring is a tight, low-speed, high-downforce circuit — historically a track where car handling matters more than raw engine power, which in theory should favour a well-balanced chassis like Ferrari's SF-26 over rivals with a straight-line speed edge.

stories.title: Mercedes leads the championship but keeps losing points to its own mistakes — its boss wants that to stop in Hungary
stories.what_happened: Team principal Toto Wolff said Mercedes' W17 car has shown enough raw speed at every round this season, but 'self-inflicted' operational mistakes and technical problems have repeatedly cost the team points it should have banked. The most recent example: George Russell retired from the Belgian Grand Prix at Spa after opening-lap contact with teammate-rival Lewis Hamilton (now at Ferrari), while Antonelli went on to win. Wolff's stated goal for Hungary — the last race before the summer break — is simply 'a clean, reliable weekend.'
key_points: Mercedes leads both the drivers' and constructors' standings
But team boss Toto Wolff says 'self-inflicted' errors have cost them points all season
Russell retired at Spa after opening-lap contact with Hamilton
Wolff's demand: a clean, reliable weekend before the summer break

depth.background: Leading a championship on paper (having the fastest car) and actually converting that into race wins and points are two different things in Formula 1 — mistakes in pit stops, crashes, mechanical failures, or driver errors can hand points to rivals even from a dominant position. Mercedes, powered by rookie sensation Kimi Antonelli's strong form, currently tops both the drivers' and constructors' standings this season.

stories.title: McLaren brings another big upgrade package to Hungary — a new floor and more aero on top of its 'Macarena' wing
stories.what_happened: McLaren confirmed it will introduce what it calls 'a valuable package of upgrades' at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, including a new floor and additional aerodynamic components designed to improve performance at high-downforce circuits such as the Hungaroring (this weekend) and Zandvoort (later in the season). The team's reserve driver, Leonardo Fornaroli, will again drive in first practice (FP1) under F1's rule requiring teams to run rookie drivers in practice sessions.
key_points: McLaren is bringing a further upgrade package for the Hungarian GP: a new floor plus extra aerodynamic parts
It follows McLaren's 'Macarena' rear-wing upgrade already introduced this season
The parts target high-downforce circuits like the Hungaroring and Zandvoort specifically
Reserve driver Leonardo Fornaroli gets another FP1 outing as part of F1's rookie-practice rule

depth.background: McLaren has been the dominant constructor this season, but rivals — especially Ferrari and a resurgent Red Bull — have been closing the gap in recent races. Hungary is the last race before F1's month-long summer break, making it the final chance for teams to bank upgrades before an extended shutdown on car development.

thread.previously: McLaren had already confirmed it would bring its long-awaited upgrade package to Hungary, including an unusual 'Macarena' rear wing design.
thread.changed: McLaren has now detailed a further, separate package on top of that — a new floor and additional aero parts specifically aimed at high-downforce tracks like Hungaroring and Zandvoort.

prev_ref.title: McLaren finally brings its big upgrade — and its unusual 'Macarena' rear wing — to this weekend's Hungarian GP

stories.title: TMC's Kalyan Banerjee suspended from the Lok Sabha for the rest of the Monsoon Session over remarks against a woman MP
stories.what_happened: The Lok Sabha suspended Banerjee for the remainder of the Monsoon Session — effective until it ends on August 13 — after a complaint that he had used unparliamentary language, including calling a fellow MP 'chor' and making objectionable remarks directed at women members of the House. The move follows reports of an internal TMC rift, with Banerjee reportedly clashing publicly with party supremo Mamata Banerjee at a recent event.
key_points: Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla suspends TMC MP Kalyan Banerjee till the session ends on August 13
Suspension follows a complaint by an MP over Banerjee's 'unparliamentary language,' including calling a colleague 'chor' (thief)
Comes amid reports of friction between Banerjee and his own party chief, Mamata Banerjee
Adds to an already disrupted Monsoon Session already gripped by NEET-leak and Vande Mataram bill rows

depth.background: India's Parliament runs on a code of conduct enforced by the Speaker (in the Lok Sabha, the lower house) — MPs who use abusive or 'unparliamentary' language, especially targeting women colleagues, can be suspended for a period the Speaker sets. Kalyan Banerjee is a veteran Trinamool Congress (TMC) MP from West Bengal and one of the party's most vocal, combative speakers in the House. This Monsoon Session (India's Parliament sits roughly three times a year — Budget, Monsoon, Winter) has already been chaotic, repeatedly adjourned over the NEET medical-entrance paper leak and a bill on insulting the national song, Vande Mataram.

stories.title: Cadillac benches Bottas again for Hungary FP1 as rookie Colton Herta chases the team's first F1 point
stories.what_happened: Cadillac confirmed Colton Herta will again take over Valtteri Bottas's car for first practice (FP1) at the Hungarian Grand Prix on July 24 — his second FP1 outing of the season, after making his F1 weekend debut in Barcelona. It comes as Cadillac, in its debut F1 season, continues chasing its first-ever championship point.
key_points: Valtteri Bottas sits out first practice in Hungary; American IndyCar star Colton Herta drives his car
It's Herta's second FP1 outing of the season under F1's mandatory rookie-runs rule
Cadillac is F1's brand-new 11th team this season and still hasn't scored a point
Bottas has faced replacement rumors, though Cadillac's boss has publicly backed him

depth.background: Cadillac is a brand-new Formula 1 team that joined the grid in 2026 — the sport's first new entrant in years — and is still searching for its first championship point. F1 rules require every team to run a rookie (a driver with two or fewer prior F1 starts) in at least two Friday practice sessions per car each season, which is why experienced drivers like Bottas periodically make way. Colton Herta is a well-known American IndyCar driver signed by Cadillac partly to build the team's profile in the US.

stories.title: Congo's Ebola outbreak is now the fastest-spreading in history — nearly 1,000 dead in barely two months
stories.what_happened: Health authorities have recorded 2,473 confirmed Ebola cases and 999 deaths since the outbreak began on May 15, 2026 — a pace that makes it the fastest-killing Ebola outbreak ever documented, faster even than the historic 2013-2016 West Africa epidemic. The virus behind it, Bundibugyo ebolavirus, is a rarer strain for which there is no approved vaccine or treatment, unlike the Zaire strain vaccines used in recent years. Africa CDC says conflict in the region, resistance from local communities toward health workers, and an inconsistent response have let the virus spread largely unchecked — fewer than 9% of people who had contact with confirmed cases are being tracked and monitored, far below what's needed to contain an outbreak.
key_points: 2,473 confirmed cases and 999 deaths as of July 21, since the outbreak was declared on May 15
Killing people faster than any Ebola outbreak on record, including the 2013-2016 outbreak that killed 11,000+
The strain involved, Bundibugyo virus, has no approved vaccine or treatment — unlike the more common Ebola Zaire strain
More than 60% of deaths are happening in the community, before patients even reach a hospital

depth.background: Ebola is a severe viral disease that causes fever and internal bleeding and kills a large share of the people it infects. The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), in central Africa, has had more Ebola outbreaks than any other country, including the second-largest ever in 2018-2020. Vaccines exist for the most common strain (Zaire), which is why recent outbreaks were brought under control faster than this one.

stories.title: Tropical Storm Bertha bears down on Louisiana — landfall expected this evening
stories.what_happened: Tropical Storm Bertha, packing sustained winds of 60 mph, was tracked about 105 miles south-southeast of Mobile, Alabama, crawling northwest at only 6 mph. The National Hurricane Center expects Bertha to make its first landfall near southeastern Louisiana this evening as a tropical storm (not a hurricane), before drifting west along the coast and making a second landfall in Texas on Friday.
key_points: Bertha has 60 mph winds, moving northwest at just 6 mph
First landfall expected near southeast Louisiana this evening
Storm surge of 1-4 feet forecast from the Florida Panhandle to Louisiana
A second landfall in Texas is expected by Friday as the storm loops back

depth.background: The Gulf Coast — Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, the Florida Panhandle, and Texas — sits in one of the world's most storm-exposed regions, and is also home to a huge share of US oil refining and natural gas export capacity. Tropical storms here regularly force refineries and offshore platforms to shut down as a precaution, and slow-moving ones like Bertha are especially dangerous because they dump rain over the same area for longer.

stories.title: India call up Ravi Bishnoi and Prince Yadav after Varun Chakravarthy and Harshit Rana are ruled out injured
stories.what_happened: The BCCI (India's cricket board) confirmed Chakravarthy needs further recovery time and will report to its Centre of Excellence in Bengaluru for rehabilitation, while pacer Harshit Rana was also ruled out. Leg-spinner Ravi Bishnoi and seamer Prince Yadav have been added to the squad for the Zimbabwe series as their replacements. Bishnoi featured in England's first two T20Is but had a rough outing in the second match at Old Trafford, going for 60 runs in his four overs.
key_points: Leg-spinner Ravi Bishnoi and pacer Prince Yadav join India's Zimbabwe T20I squad
They replace Varun Chakravarthy (hamstring) and Harshit Rana, both injured in England
Chakravarthy heads to the BCCI's Centre of Excellence for rehab
Bishnoi struggled in his last England outing, conceding 60 runs in 4 overs

depth.background: India are touring Zimbabwe for a three-match T20I series starting July 23, led by Shreyas Iyer, just after wrapping up a difficult white-ball tour of England. Varun Chakravarthy had already missed India's last two T20Is in England with a hamstring injury.

stories.title: Amit Shah's bill to criminalise insulting Vande Mataram splits Parliament along party lines
stories.what_happened: Home Minister Amit Shah introduced the Prevention of Insults to National Honour (Amendment) Bill, 2026 in the Rajya Sabha, which would extend the existing 1971 law — which already criminalises disrespecting the flag, the Constitution, and the anthem — to also cover Vande Mataram. Passage has stalled as the Monsoon Session has been repeatedly disrupted by opposition protests over other issues.
key_points: Bill would give the national song the same legal protection as the flag and anthem
Insulting it, or blocking its singing, could mean up to 3 years in prison
Introduced by Home Minister Amit Shah in the Rajya Sabha on the Monsoon Session's opening day
Repeated House disruptions have so far stalled a vote

depth.background: Vande Mataram ('I bow to thee, Mother') is India's national song, written by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay in 1875 and a rallying cry during the independence movement — distinct from the national anthem, Jana Gana Mana. Some of its later verses have long been contested by parts of India's Muslim community for their religious imagery, making any move to formally elevate its legal status politically sensitive.

stories.title: The US and China will hold their first formal AI talks in September — trying to jointly manage frontier-model risk instead of just restricting each other
stories.what_happened: Reuters sources report the US and China have agreed to hold their first formal, AI-specific talks in September 2026, led on the US side by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. The meeting follows the Trump-Xi summit in May and is expected to happen before Xi's planned visit to the US on September 24. The agenda and venue are still being finalised, but the focus will be on 'frontier' AI models — the most advanced systems either country builds — including how each side controls release of future models and whether the US will restrict Chinese open-weight (freely downloadable) models.
key_points: First-ever dedicated US-China AI dialogue, planned for September
Grew out of the Trump-Xi summit held in May 2026
Timed ahead of Xi's planned US visit on September 24
Topics: powerful 'frontier' AI models, open-source model risks, IP theft

depth.background: For the past few years, the US and China have mostly dealt with each other's AI industries through one-sided tools: US export bans on advanced chips, and Chinese restrictions on rare materials the US tech industry needs. There has been no direct, dedicated channel where the two governments sit down specifically to discuss the risks of powerful AI systems — until now. The two countries' most powerful AI labs (American firms like Anthropic and OpenAI, Chinese firms like Moonshot AI) are racing to build ever more capable models, and both governments are increasingly worried about what happens if that race outpaces anyone's ability to control the technology.

stories.title: Sri Lanka's Kusal Mendis ruled out with a hamstring injury, weeks before his side's Test series against India
stories.what_happened: Mendis has sustained a hamstring injury and been ruled out of the rest of LPL 2026, playing for Colombo Kaps. The injury raises doubts about his fitness and availability for Sri Lanka's upcoming two-Test home series against India, which begins August 15.
key_points: Mendis out of LPL 2026 with a hamstring injury
Doubt now hangs over Sri Lanka's Test series vs India
That series starts August 15
Blow to both his franchise, Colombo Kaps, and the national side

depth.background: Kusal Mendis is one of Sri Lanka's key senior batters across formats and a regular feature of the national Test side. The Lanka Premier League (LPL) is Sri Lanka's domestic T20 franchise tournament, which players use partly as match fitness and form practice in the lead-up to bigger international assignments — in this case, a home Test series against India starting August 15.

stories.title: New Zealand eye a series sweep as West Indies fight for pride in the ODI decider
stories.what_happened: The two sides play the fifth and final ODI of the series today at Kensington Oval in Bridgetown, Barbados — a dead rubber with the series already decided, but with West Indies looking to avoid a complete sweep after being bowled out cheaply in three consecutive matches.
key_points: New Zealand have already sealed the 5-match series 3-1 after West Indies collapsed for 138, 140 and 188 in three straight defeats
Left-arm spinner Jayden Lennox has been the standout, taking 11 wickets in 4 matches in a series where spinners have taken a West-Indies-record 42 wickets
West Indies' Keacy Carty (174 runs) is the lone batting bright spot as the hosts try to avoid a full series sweep at Kensington Oval, Bridgetown
It's a dead rubber for the series outcome, but a chance for West Indies to salvage pride before hosting Pakistan for a marquee Test series

depth.background: New Zealand toured the West Indies for a five-match ODI series. West Indies won the opener in Providence, but New Zealand hit back with three straight wins, clinching the series 3-1 with one match to go, exposing a West Indies batting order that has repeatedly struggled against spin.

stories.title: Mercedes explains the car-to-car software gap that's been costing Russell pace since Silverstone
stories.what_happened: Mercedes technical staff, including engineer Simone Resta, said that deployment (energy-management) differences between Antonelli's and Russell's cars have been present since the British Grand Prix at Silverstone, contributing to a pace gap between the two even when both cars are otherwise similar. Team principal Toto Wolff, previewing the Hungarian Grand Prix — the last race before F1's summer break — said the team needs to 'execute a clean, reliable weekend' after a difficult recent stretch.
key_points: Mercedes engineer says deployment differences between the two cars began at Silverstone
Antonelli leads the title; team-mate Russell has fallen 50 points back
Boss Toto Wolff wants a 'clean, reliable' Hungarian GP to reset momentum
Comes right before F1's last race before the summer break

depth.background: In hybrid F1 cars, 'deployment' is how the car manages and releases energy from its battery/hybrid system through a lap — even small software differences in how two identical cars deploy that energy can create a real pace gap between team-mates. Kimi Antonelli has been Mercedes' form driver this season, winning at Spa, while George Russell has had a rockier run, including a first-lap retirement at the Belgian Grand Prix.

stories.title: Rubio says the US is open to an Iran deal, but warns Iran's Hormuz toll demand would 'threaten the world economy'
stories.what_happened: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio told Asian leaders on July 22 that Iran's demand to control and toll traffic through Hormuz would 'threaten the world's economy' and set a precedent other chokepoint states could copy. He said Washington remains open to negotiating a permanent deal but that Iran 'doesn't seem to be serious' about one, pointing to Tehran allegedly violating the June truce within two weeks of signing it.
key_points: Rubio says Washington is open to talks, but Iran 'right now doesn't seem serious'
He says Iran broke a June truce commitment within two weeks
Iran wants to charge tolls and manage traffic through the Strait of Hormuz
Rubio calls that a dangerous global precedent, warns Asian leaders

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman that roughly a fifth of the world's oil and a big share of its gas physically must sail through — there's no other practical sea route out of the Gulf. On June 17, the US and Iran signed an interim truce (a memorandum of understanding) that paused fighting, reopened the strait, and set a 60-day window to negotiate something permanent. But the deal never settled who actually controls shipping through it, and Iran read it as recognizing its right to manage — and charge for — traffic there.

stories.title: Zimbabwe name their T20I squad for the India series — Sikandar Raza to lead, uncapped Tsiga gets his shot
stories.what_happened: Zimbabwe Cricket named a 15-player squad for the series, led by Sikandar Raza. All-rounder Wessly Madhevere and pace bowler Newman Nyamhuri return to the squad, while wicketkeeper Tafadzwa Tsiga has been given his first-ever India call-up. The three matches will all be played at the Harare Sports Club on July 23, 25, and 26.
key_points: 15-member squad announced for the 3-match T20I series vs India
Sikandar Raza captains; Wessly Madhevere and Newman Nyamhuri recalled
Tafadzwa Tsiga earns his maiden T20I call-up
Matches at Harare Sports Club on July 23, 25 and 26

depth.background: Zimbabwe are hosting a new-look Indian side, led by Shreyas Iyer, for a three-match T20 International series starting this week. Sikandar Raza is Zimbabwe's most experienced white-ball all-rounder and long-time captain, and the squad announcement is Zimbabwe Cricket's opening move in a series where the home side will hope home conditions offset India's talent gap.

stories.title: Russell shuts down his own Mercedes-exit rumors as Red Bull admits rivals are circling Verstappen's seat
stories.what_happened: George Russell publicly rejected suggestions he could be squeezed out of Mercedes to make room for Verstappen, saying he has a contract for next year that 'settles it.' Separately, Red Bull team principal Laurent Mekies told Sky Sports that several drivers have already approached the team about the possibility of replacing Verstappen, calling it 'completely normal' given Verstappen is 'the best driver in the world.'
key_points: George Russell: 'I have a contract for next year. That settles it.'
Red Bull boss Laurent Mekies admits other drivers have asked about Verstappen's seat
Comes right after Verstappen publicly confirmed he's staying at Red Bull
Verstappen's Red Bull deal still has an exit clause tied to his championship position

depth.background: Max Verstappen has spent much of the 2026 season fending off speculation about a possible Mercedes move, fueled by an exit clause in his Red Bull contract that can be triggered if he's outside the top two in the standings at the start of the summer break. Rival team boss chatter and paddock rumor have repeatedly linked Russell's Mercedes seat to a possible Verstappen arrival for 2027.

stories.title: RBI says India's economy is holding up despite the oil-price shock from the US-Iran war
stories.what_happened: In its July 2026 Bulletin, the RBI said India's economic activity has stayed resilient even as global uncertainty — geopolitical tension and supply-chain disruption — has intensified. It pointed to healthy domestic consumer demand and strong industrial and services-sector output as the main supports, and said improving liquidity conditions (banks having more spare cash to lend) is helping sustain credit growth.
key_points: RBI's July 2026 Bulletin says the economy 'remains resilient' despite global uncertainty
Credits strong domestic demand plus industrial and services-sector growth
Says improving liquidity (how easily banks can lend) is supporting credit growth
Comes the same day Sensex fell for a third straight session on Iran-war oil fears

depth.background: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is India's central bank — it sets interest rates, manages the rupee, and publishes a monthly Bulletin that gives its own read on how the economy is doing. It's meant as a factual, technical assessment, not a political statement, though markets and the government both watch it closely for the central bank's tone. This assessment lands at a tense moment: the US-Iran war has pushed oil prices to a one-month high, which matters enormously for India because it imports roughly 85% of its crude oil, so pricier oil widens India's import bill, pressures the rupee, and stokes inflation.

stories.title: Adani Power's profit jumps 47% as the company plans a ₹15,000 crore fundraise
stories.what_happened: Adani Power reported that its consolidated net profit for the quarter ended June 30, 2026 rose 47.2% year-on-year to ₹4,866.6 crore, with total income up 32.6% to ₹19,322.3 crore. Alongside the results, the company's board approved a plan to raise up to ₹15,000 crore by issuing new equity shares to institutional investors.
key_points: Q1 FY27 net profit rose 47% year-on-year to ₹4,867 crore
Total income climbed 33% to ₹19,322 crore
The board approved raising up to ₹15,000 crore via a QIP share sale
Comes on India's biggest earnings day of the season, with ~63 firms reporting

depth.background: Adani Power, part of the Adani Group, is India's largest private thermal power producer, running coal-fired plants that supply electricity across several states. A QIP — Qualified Institutional Placement — is a fast, low-hassle way for a listed Indian company to raise fresh capital by selling new shares directly to large institutional investors (mutual funds, insurers, foreign funds) rather than the public, skipping the lengthier process of a full public offering.

stories.title: Audi arrives in Hungary on a two-race points streak — team boss wants one more push before the summer break
stories.what_happened: Heading into the Hungarian Grand Prix weekend, Audi is on a run of consecutive points-scoring finishes, highlighted by Gabriel Bortoleto's eighth place at the Belgian Grand Prix in Spa. McNish says the Hungaroring — the final race before F1's summer break — is another chance to build on that momentum and end the first half of the season on a high note. Both Hulkenberg and Bortoleto say the recent results have lifted spirits across the team, though Hulkenberg cautioned that execution, not just car pace, has been the difference between scoring and missing out in recent races.
key_points: Audi (formerly Sauber) has scored points in back-to-back races, including at Spa
Racing boss Allan McNish wants a strong Hungary result before the mid-season break
Drivers Hulkenberg and Bortoleto both say morale has lifted after recent results
Hungary is the last race before F1's three-week summer shutdown

depth.background: Audi took over the Sauber F1 team for the 2026 season as its own factory-backed entry, pairing experienced driver Nico Hulkenberg with rising Brazilian talent Gabriel Bortoleto. Racing chief Allan McNish previously ran Audi's motorsport programmes; the team has spent the season trying to establish itself as a genuine midfield player rather than a backmarker.

stories.title: Verstappen ends exit speculation, confirms he's staying at Red Bull
stories.what_happened: Verstappen publicly stated he is staying at Red Bull, directly addressing swirling exit rumors. The statement follows Red Bull's own moves to reassure him — including turning down a $3 billion offer for its junior team, Racing Bulls, and picking his next race engineer after a prior partnership ended.
key_points: Verstappen said 'it's time to basically stop all the rumours' about a possible move away from Red Bull
Comes despite a rough season — he currently sits 7th in the drivers' championship
Red Bull had already moved to keep him happy: picking his next race engineer and rejecting a reported $3 billion bid for junior team Racing Bulls
Ends months of speculation tied to a contract 'exit clause' triggered by the team's underperformance

depth.background: Verstappen's Red Bull contract reportedly includes a performance-linked exit clause that lets him leave if the team falls below a competitiveness threshold — one that a difficult 2026 car had pushed close to triggering. That fueled months of speculation about a possible move to McLaren or elsewhere, especially after Lewis Hamilton's high-profile switch to Ferrari raised the idea that another champion could switch teams mid-career.

thread.previously: Pundits had said yesterday that Verstappen's routes out of Red Bull were closing and expected him to stay for 2027.
thread.changed: Verstappen confirmed it himself today, publicly ending the speculation rather than leaving it to analysts.

prev_ref.title: Verstappen's escape routes from Red Bull are closing — pundits now expect him to stay put for 2027

stories.title: Japan's yen crashes through a 40-year low — and its central bank now hints at hiking rates faster to fight back
stories.what_happened: The yen slid past 163 per dollar this week, its weakest level since 1986, extending a slump that has persisted despite Japan spending roughly $73 billion on unannounced currency intervention in April and May. Bloomberg reported on July 22 that Bank of Japan officials are now privately open to raising interest rates at a faster pace than markets expect, because the weak yen is making imported goods — especially energy — more expensive and pushing inflation risk higher. Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama has repeatedly said Tokyo stands ready to take 'decisive action' on the currency, without naming a specific level that would trigger it.
key_points: Yen slid past ¥163 to the dollar, a level last seen in 1986
Bank of Japan (BOJ) already hiked its key rate to 1% (a 31-year high) this month
BOJ officials now signal they could hike even faster to fight yen-driven inflation
Japan already burned $73 billion defending the yen earlier this year with little lasting effect

depth.background: A currency's value largely tracks the gap between two countries' interest rates: money flows toward wherever it earns more interest. The US Federal Reserve has kept its rates well above Japan's for years, so investors keep borrowing cheap yen to buy higher-yielding dollar assets — a trade that keeps pushing the yen down. The Bank of Japan has been raising rates cautiously (now 1%, its highest in 31 years) to narrow that gap without choking off Japan's fragile growth. Japan's Ministry of Finance also has a history of stepping into currency markets directly to buy yen and prop up its price when the slide gets disorderly.

stories.title: IndusInd Bank's profit jumps 72% as the scandal-hit lender's turnaround gathers pace
stories.what_happened: IndusInd Bank reported a 72% year-on-year jump in net profit to ₹1,037 crore for the quarter ended June 2026 (Q1 FY27), a 75% jump from the previous quarter. Net interest income grew just 1% to ₹4,685 crore as loan growth stayed weak, while other income fell 17%. The bank's net interest margin — a measure of how profitably it lends — slipped to 3.35%. Deposits grew 4% to ₹4.14 trillion with a stable CASA ratio (the share of low-cost current and savings deposits) of 29.43%.
key_points: Net profit rose 72% year-on-year (and 75% quarter-on-quarter) to ₹1,037 crore for the June quarter
The jump came mainly from lower bad-loan provisions and cost cuts, not stronger core lending
Net interest income (interest earned minus interest paid) grew just 1%, and retail loans actually shrank 4%
Deposits grew 4% to ₹4.14 trillion, a sign customer trust is stabilizing after last year's scandal

depth.background: IndusInd Bank spent the past year and a half working through a major accounting scandal: in March 2025 it disclosed that derivative trades (financial contracts used to hedge risk) had been wrongly accounted for going back to 2016, leading to a nearly ₹2,000 crore write-off, followed by further misstatements found in its microfinance business. CEO Sumant Kathpalia resigned in April 2025 taking 'moral responsibility,' and regulators including SEBI and the Serious Fraud Investigation Office have since scrutinized the bank. The scandal wiped out investor confidence and hammered the stock.

stories.title: Modi launches ₹5,650 crore in Tamil Nadu projects and holds a big NDA rally — an early opening shot for next year's state election
stories.what_happened: On July 22, Modi inaugurated and laid foundation stones for development projects worth roughly ₹5,650 crore across Tamil Nadu, including Bharat Petroleum's City Gas Distribution Network for the Nilgiris and Erode districts (over ₹3,680 crore) and Indian Oil's lube-oil blending plant in Chennai's Manali area (₹1,490 crore). He also flagged off five new train services — two Amrit Bharat Express trains among them — and inaugurated 89 rural roads under the PMGSY scheme, before addressing an NDA rally in Tiruchirappalli.
key_points: PM Modi inaugurates/lays foundation for ₹5,650 crore of projects in Tiruchirappalli
Flags off five new train services, including two Amrit Bharat Express trains
Big-ticket items: a ₹3,680 crore city gas network and a ₹1,490 crore fuel-blending plant
Addresses an NDA rally — Tamil Nadu votes in April 2026, with BJP allied to AIADMK against the DMK

depth.background: Tamil Nadu goes to the polls in a single phase on April 23, 2026, with 234 assembly seats at stake. The BJP has historically struggled to win seats on its own in Tamil Nadu, so for 2026 it's contesting just 27 seats as part of an alliance led by the AIADMK (a former ruling party) against the incumbent DMK, led by Chief Minister M.K. Stalin. High-visibility infrastructure launches by the sitting Prime Minister are a common pre-election tool in India — they let the ruling coalition showcase delivery while also drawing a large public crowd for a political rally.

stories.title: India arrive in Zimbabwe winless in eight T20Is since becoming world champions, as Iyer hunts his first win as captain
stories.what_happened: India lands in Zimbabwe for a three-match T20I series starting July 23 having lost seven straight T20Is under new captain Shreyas Iyer since the World Cup win — including a 4-0 series whitewash by England and a series loss to Ireland. Zimbabwe, led by veteran Sikandar Raza, arrive with momentum from recent home wins over Bangladesh.
key_points: India haven't won a single T20I since winning the T20 World Cup in March
New captain Shreyas Iyer is 0-for-7 since taking over, including a 4-0 whitewash by England
Three-match series in Harare starts Thursday, July 23
Zimbabwe, led by Sikandar Raza, arrive in good form after a landmark Test win over Bangladesh

depth.background: India won the T20 World Cup in March 2026, but its short-format team has cratered since — a normal part of the sport's post-title transition, as senior players step back from the format and a new captain, Shreyas Iyer, takes over from scratch. Zimbabwe, historically one of the weaker Test nations, has been on an upswing at home, including a rare Test win over Bangladesh.

stories.title: Nestle India's profit jumps 48% as Maggi and KitKat sales surge — shares hit a record high
stories.what_happened: Nestle India reported July 22 that its standalone net profit jumped 47.9% year-on-year to about ₹975 crore (roughly $117 million; a crore is 10 million rupees), with consolidated profit up 48% to ₹958.7 crore. Revenue grew 25% to ₹6,378 crore, ahead of analyst forecasts of around ₹6,065 crore, driven by strong growth in Maggi noodles and KitKat chocolates, both up roughly 25%. The company's operating profit margin (EBITDA margin) expanded to 24.2% even as it raised advertising spending by more than 40%, and shares climbed about 4% to a record high.
key_points: Q1 net profit up ~48% year-on-year to about ₹959 crore
Revenue up 25% to ₹6,378 crore, beating analyst estimates
Maggi noodles and KitKat sales each grew about 25%
Shares jumped ~4% to a record high on the results

depth.background: Nestle India is the local arm of the Swiss packaged-foods giant and one of India's most-watched consumer-staples ('FMCG' — fast-moving consumer goods, the everyday household and food products people buy repeatedly) companies, making it a bellwether for how much ordinary Indian households are spending on branded food and drinks.

stories.title: Supreme Court won't block a rival Shiv Sena faction's growing clout in Parliament — but agrees to hear Uddhav's challenge
stories.what_happened: A Supreme Court bench of Justices PS Narasimha and Alok Aradhe issued notice on a petition by Shiv Sena (UBT) MP Arvind Sawant, challenging Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla's decision to approve six more MPs' merger into the Shinde-led faction — taking its Lok Sabha strength from 7 to 13. Senior advocate Devadutt Kamat, arguing for UBT, said no disqualification petitions were even pending against those MPs, so the Speaker had no occasion to approve a merger at all. The bench issued notice but refused to grant an interim stay at this stage.
key_points: SC issues notice on Shiv Sena (UBT)'s plea against the Lok Sabha Speaker's ruling
Speaker had approved 6 more MPs switching to the rival Shinde-led Shiv Sena
That move took Shinde Sena's Lok Sabha strength from 7 to 13 seats
Court refused an interim stay — the merger stands for now while the case proceeds

depth.background: In 2022, Eknath Shinde led a rebellion that split the original Shiv Sena, taking most of its legislators with him and eventually becoming Maharashtra's chief minister, while Uddhav Thackeray kept a smaller loyalist faction now known as Shiv Sena (UBT) — 'UBT' for Uddhav Balasaheb Thackeray. In 2023, the Election Commission gave the original party's name and symbol to Shinde's faction, a decision UBT has contested ever since. India's anti-defection law lets a legislature's presiding officer (here, the Lok Sabha Speaker) decide whether lawmakers who switch sides count as a legitimate 'merger' or should be disqualified — a power that's been at the center of the Sena split's legal fights.

stories.title: India presses China to fix trade imbalance and market access, even as both sides talk up border calm
stories.what_happened: External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar met Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi and told him the two countries need to address 'fair market access and trade balance' as part of repairing relations. Jaishankar reiterated that peace and calm along the disputed border — the Line of Actual Control (LAC) — remains the essential precondition for any broader normalization of ties between the two nuclear-armed neighbors.
key_points: Foreign Minister Jaishankar told his Chinese counterpart Wang Yi that trade issues need fixing
He said peace at the border is the essential precondition for normal ties
Comes two years after the 2024 thaw that ended years of border-clash tension
Trade imbalance has been a long-standing Indian grievance — China exports far more to India than it imports

depth.background: India and China fought a deadly border clash in the Galwan Valley in 2020, freezing relations for years. Ties began thawing after talks between PM Modi and China's President Xi Jinping in October 2024, and diplomats from both sides have since been meeting periodically to manage the relationship, including a visit by China's Foreign Minister Wang Yi to Delhi in June. Despite the political thaw, India runs a large and persistent trade deficit with China — it buys far more Chinese goods (electronics, machinery, chemicals) than China buys from India.

stories.title: NEET protests escalate: opposition wears black in Parliament, CJP says 'no more talks' as Wangchuk's hunger strike hits day 24
stories.what_happened: Today, opposition MPs including Rahul Gandhi, Priyanka Gandhi Vadra and Samajwadi Party's Akhilesh Yadav wore black clothes on Parliament's premises in solidarity with the protesting students, as the Lok Sabha saw repeated adjournments over the issue. The CJP's founder said there would be no further talks with the government. Separately, two Union ministers visited Wangchuk, now 24 days into his hunger strike, at a Gurugram hospital.
key_points: Opposition MPs led by Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra wore black in Parliament today, a day after their sit-in at PM Modi's residence
The Cockroach Janata Party (CJP), which is running the Jantar Mantar protest, says it will no longer negotiate with the government
Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited hunger-striker Sonam Wangchuk in hospital as his fast enters its 24th day
Lok Sabha was repeatedly disrupted as the opposition pressed for Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation

depth.background: NEET is India's national medical-entrance exam. A leaked question paper triggered weeks of student protests and a hunger strike by activist Sonam Wangchuk, demanding accountability and Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation. Police action against protesting students in Delhi further inflamed the standoff, and on July 21 Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra were detained after marching on the Prime Minister's residence — the first time a Leader of the Opposition had protested there.

thread.previously: Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra were detained by Delhi Police yesterday after marching on PM Modi's residence to protest the NEET paper leak and the police crackdown on student protesters.
thread.changed: The opposition escalated further today with a coordinated black-clothes protest inside Parliament, the protest group leading the Jantar Mantar sit-in ruled out further talks, and two Union ministers visited hunger-striker Sonam Wangchuk in hospital as his fast passed the three-week mark.

prev_ref.title: Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra detained by Delhi Police after marching on PM Modi's residence over the NEET crackdown

stories.title: Sonam Wangchuk sets a new condition to end his hunger strike — from his ICU bed
stories.what_happened: In a letter to the central government, Wangchuk said he wants to live but will only break his 24-day hunger strike if the Centre gives a written assurance that no legal cases, harassment, or other punitive action will be taken against the students and protesters involved in the demonstrations. Doctors at Medanta Hospital, where he remains under ICU observation, say his vital signs are currently stable. The letter follows a visit from government ministers a day earlier, signaling the Centre may be willing to negotiate after weeks of standoff.
key_points: Wangchuk says he'll end his fast only if the Centre promises no legal action against protesters
He is stable but remains in the ICU at Delhi's Medanta Hospital
Fast now in its 24th day, over alleged NEET and other exam-paper leaks
Comes a day after the Centre signaled openness to talks by sending ministers to visit him

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a well-known Ladakh-based education reformer and climate activist (his work inspired a character in the film '3 Idiots'). He has been fasting at a Delhi protest site since late June in solidarity with the Gen Z-led 'Cockroach Janta Party' (CJP), which is demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign over exam-paper leaks — including the NEET medical-entrance exam — that affected millions of students earlier this year.

thread.previously: We reported the Centre had sent ministers to visit Wangchuk at Medanta Hospital, signaling a possible opening for talks after weeks of refusing to engage.
thread.changed: Wangchuk has now responded with a specific, written condition — an assurance of no legal action against protesters — turning the vague 'openness to talks' into a concrete demand the government must answer.

prev_ref.title: Centre signals it's open to talks with Sonam Wangchuk as ministers visit him at Medanta Hospital

stories.title: India's biggest earnings day of the season: nearly 60 companies report results, from Adani Power to Nestle to Zomato's parent
stories.what_happened: Nearly 60 companies released April-June quarter results on Wednesday, one of the busiest single earnings days of the season. Reporting companies span energy (Adani Power, Adani Green Energy, BPCL, HPCL), consumer goods (Nestle India, United Spirits), pharmaceuticals (Dr Reddy's Laboratories), and consumer internet (Eternal, the parent company of food-delivery app Zomato), among dozens of others including SRF, NTPC Green, and Schaeffler India.
key_points: Close to 60 companies report April-June (Q1 FY27) results on the same day
Big names include Adani Power, Adani Green, Nestle India, Dr Reddy's, BPCL, HPCL, and Eternal (Zomato's parent)
Results land the same week as a three-day losing streak for the Sensex and Nifty, driven by high oil prices
Investors will use these results to judge how much the Iran-war oil spike and new US tariffs are already denting Indian corporate profits

depth.background: Indian listed companies report earnings once a quarter, and in a fast-growing economy like India's, these results are one of the clearest windows into how businesses are actually holding up versus how the stock market is pricing them. This particular quarter (April-June, called Q1 of the FY27 fiscal year that started in April) is being watched more closely than usual because it lands right as oil prices are spiking due to the Iran war and Indian exporters are getting hit with new US tariffs — both of which raise costs or squeeze revenue for many of the companies reporting.

stories.title: India's 'Cockroach' movement, explained: how a joke about students became a nationwide challenge to Modi
stories.what_happened: Tens of thousands of Cockroach-movement supporters marched through New Delhi this week demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation over repeated exam-paper leaks and the government's handling of the fallout. Delhi Police say the crackdown that followed injured roughly 180 people — 118 security personnel and 60 protesters — and opposition leader Rahul Gandhi was briefly detained after leading a sit-in outside Prime Minister Modi's residence in solidarity. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing Gandhi of using students as 'props in a political campaign.'
key_points: Started ~2 months ago as satire, is now one of India's biggest youth protests in years
Core demand: Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign over exam-leak scandals
Protesters have camped at Delhi's Jantar Mantar for over a month
Delhi Police say ~180 people were hurt in this week's crackdown, including 118 officers

depth.background: The movement takes its name from young Indians mockingly calling themselves 'cockroaches' — a jab at officials seen as dismissive of student grievances — after a wave of scandals over leaked entrance-exam papers (including the medical-entrance exam NEET) and alleged deaths by suicide among affected students. What began online as satire has, over about two months, hardened into sustained street protest led largely by students and young professionals, camping at Jantar Mantar (a traditional protest site near India's Parliament) since June.

stories.title: Centre signals it's open to talks with Sonam Wangchuk as ministers visit him at Medanta Hospital
stories.what_happened: Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited Wangchuk at Medanta Hospital, where he remains in the ICU under specialist Dr Sushila Kataria. Sources say the central government signaled it is now open to holding talks to resolve the standoff, even as Wangchuk told the ministers he still needs to keep looking after the students' protest rather than step back.
key_points: Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited Wangchuk in the ICU at Medanta
Sources say the centre is now open to talks over the NEET protest crisis
Wangchuk reportedly told ministers he still has to look after the students' protest
He remains under Dr Sushila Kataria's care as his hunger strike continues

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk, a climate and education activist, has been on a hunger strike since a protest at Delhi's Jantar Mantar on June 28 over alleged irregularities and paper leaks in the NEET-UG medical entrance exam. His health has declined for weeks; the Delhi High Court ordered him moved from Safdarjung Hospital to Medanta Hospital in Gurugram on Tuesday evening, citing doctors' consensus that he needs continuous monitoring. The broader opposition-backed 'Chalo Sansad' protest movement, demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation, has repeatedly clashed with police and disrupted Parliament.

thread.previously: The Delhi High Court ordered Wangchuk moved from Safdarjung Hospital to a private Gurugram hospital (Medanta) as his hunger strike entered its fourth week, citing the need for continuous monitoring.
thread.changed: Union ministers have now visited him in the ICU there, and sources say the centre is signaling openness to talks to resolve the standoff.

prev_ref.title: Sonam Wangchuk shifted to a private Gurugram hospital after Delhi High Court order, as his NEET hunger strike enters its fourth week

stories.title: Indian pharma stocks slide as Trump's steep generic-drug tariff rattles a $10bn+ export industry
stories.what_happened: Shares of India's biggest generic drugmakers — Sun Pharma, Cipla, Dr Reddy's Laboratories, Lupin and Aurobindo Pharma — came under pressure on Wednesday, July 22, after President Trump proposed a phased tariff of up to 200% on generic drug imports into the US, starting from a 100% rate after an initial two-year tariff-free period from August 1, 2026. Some technical analysts flagged that pharma stocks could fall as much as 30% if the tariff is implemented as described, though others called the initial reaction largely sentiment-driven rather than based on confirmed earnings damage.
key_points: Sun Pharma, Cipla, Dr Reddy's, Lupin, Aurobindo all in focus/falling
India is the US's single biggest supplier of generic medicines
Tariff-free window lasts two years, so no immediate hit to earnings
Analysts split on how much real damage this can do if reshoring lags

depth.background: Indian pharmaceutical companies are the backbone of the US generic drug supply — cheap, high-volume medicines like statins, antibiotics and blood-pressure pills. This export relationship, worth well over $10 billion a year, is one of India's most important trade ties with the US, and any US tariff threat to it is closely watched on Dalal Street.

stories.title: India-US trade deal is stuck at the 'last 1%' of legal text with just two days left before tariffs jump
stories.what_happened: As of this week, negotiators describe the India-US Bilateral Trade Agreement as parked at the 'last 1 per cent' of legal text — meaning the substance is largely agreed but nothing has actually been signed or published. The interim US tariff arrangement, including the extra 10% reciprocal levy on all countries, is due to lapse on July 24. A senior US official has said the two sides are 'very, very close' to a deal, but India's negotiators have said publicly they are prioritizing getting favorable terms — particularly tariff parity with competing Asian exporters like Vietnam — over rushing to beat the calendar deadline.
key_points: A temporary US tariff arrangement on Indian goods expires July 24 — two days from now
The deal is reportedly down to final legal wording, but nothing is signed yet
Earlier in 2026, the US already cut its reciprocal tariff on India from 50% to 18%
India is holding out for better terms than Asian rivals rather than rushing to beat the deadline

depth.background: Trade deals between countries usually take years, but the US and India have been negotiating an interim ('first-phase') bilateral trade agreement at unusual speed this year under pressure from President Trump's tariff policy. Earlier in 2026, Washington agreed to lower its 'reciprocal tariff' — a tax the US charges on imports roughly matching what it says the other country charges American exports — on Indian goods from 50% to 18%, partly in exchange for India agreeing to stop buying Russian oil and to purchase more American goods. But that lower rate, and a temporary 10% global tariff Trump also imposed on all trading partners for 150 days, is set to expire, which is what makes the July 24 deadline significant.

stories.title: Sensex and Nifty extend losses to a third day as Brent crude tops $92 on Middle East fears
stories.what_happened: The BSE Sensex declined around 400-700 points and the NSE Nifty slipped below 24,100 in Wednesday trade, marking a third consecutive day of losses. The drop was driven by Brent crude rising to roughly $92 a barrel — its highest in five weeks — as Iran's renewed strikes on Gulf states raised fresh fears of supply disruption. Banking stocks and other index heavyweights led the decline.
key_points: Sensex fell over 400 points and Nifty slipped below 24,100 in early trade Wednesday — a third straight losing session
Brent crude climbed to around $92 a barrel, a five-week high, on fears the Iran-Gulf conflict could disrupt oil supply
Bank stocks led the fall, alongside caution ahead of key corporate earnings this week
Comes on top of Indian pharma stocks already reeling from Trump's new generic-drug tariff

depth.background: India imports roughly 85% of the crude oil it consumes, so global oil-price swings pass through quickly to Indian markets, the rupee, and inflation. Markets have already been under pressure this week from the escalating US-Iran war pushing oil higher, the RBI's emergency dollar-inflow scheme, and a new US tariff on generic drug exports that hit pharma stocks.

thread.previously: Sensex and Nifty fell for a second straight day yesterday as the US-Iran war pushed crude oil toward $90 a barrel.
thread.changed: The losing streak extended to a third day today as Brent crude broke above $92 — a five-week high — after Iran's fresh strikes on Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, with bank stocks now leading the decline.

prev_ref.title: Sensex and Nifty fall for a second straight day as the US-Iran war pushes crude oil toward $90 a barrel

stories.title: Assam's flood toll nearly triples in a day to 31 dead, as the Brahmaputra breaches danger levels in 12 districts
stories.what_happened: The Assam State Disaster Management Authority said the flood situation worsened sharply on Wednesday: 21 people died in the preceding 24 hours alone, taking the state's 2026 monsoon death toll to 31, with more than 5.64 lakh people affected across 12 districts. Sivasagar district recorded 13 of the new deaths, including four women and two children; Charaideo recorded five, Golaghat two, and Jorhat one. The Brahmaputra is flowing above its danger mark at Neamatighat, and tributaries including the Buridihing and Dhansiri rivers are also above danger levels. Authorities are running 273 relief camps housing over 12,000 displaced people, and the India Meteorological Department has forecast more rain in the coming days.
key_points: 21 more people died in Assam in the 24 hours to Tuesday — this year's toll now 31
5.64 lakh (564,000) people affected across 12 districts
The Brahmaputra and several tributaries are now above their danger marks
13 of the new deaths were in Sivasagar district alone, including four women and two children

depth.background: Assam sits on the floodplain of the Brahmaputra, one of the world's largest rivers, and floods every monsoon (India's June-September rainy season) to some degree. This year's monsoon has been unusually intense in the northeast: flash floods and landslides had already killed dozens across Assam, Kashmir and Nagaland over the past few days, with the state's disaster management authority reporting a toll past 10 as of Monday.

thread.previously: We reported Monday that Assam's flood toll had passed 10 with over 3 lakh people affected, alongside separate deadly flooding in Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts.
thread.changed: The Assam toll alone has now nearly tripled to 31 (21 dead in a single day), with affected people up to 5.64 lakh and the Brahmaputra now above its danger mark in multiple districts — this is a serious escalation, not a continuation of the same level of flooding.

prev_ref.title: Assam's flood toll climbs past 10 as over 3 lakh are hit; Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts report at least 15 more dead

stories.title: India open their Zimbabwe T20 series Thursday — a fresh audition for Sooryavanshi and a captaincy test for Iyer
stories.what_happened: India begin a three-match T20I series against Zimbabwe on Thursday at the Harare Sports Club, fielding a young squad captained by Shreyas Iyer that includes Abhishek Sharma, Ishan Kishan, Tilak Varma, Shivam Dube and Rinku Singh in the batting, with Ravi Bishnoi and raw pace option Mayank Yadav anchoring the bowling. With Samson out of the squad, Sooryavanshi has a clear run across all three games to press his case for a regular T20I spot. India go in as overwhelming favourites on a Harare pitch that typically favours pace bowlers early before flattening out for batting.
key_points: 1st T20I in Harare on Thursday, India favourites in a 3-match series
Shreyas Iyer captains after series losses in Ireland and England
Sanju Samson left out — opens the door for Vaibhav Sooryavanshi
Ravi Bishnoi and pacer Mayank Yadav lead a young bowling attack

depth.background: India regularly sends a second-string T20I squad on short tours like this one so its top stars can rest or prepare elsewhere, using the games to test fringe players. Shreyas Iyer has been leading these second-string sides but has overseen series defeats in Ireland and England recently, putting some pressure on his captaincy credentials. Vaibhav Sooryavanshi is the teenage batting prodigy who smashed a match-winning 175 in the Under-19 World Cup final in January and is now trying to force his way into the senior T20I picture with wicketkeeper-batter Sanju Samson left out of this squad.

stories.title: India's biggest-ever road InvIT opens for bidding — Cube Highways Trust wants to raise ₹5,000 crore from toll-road investors
stories.what_happened: Cube Highways Trust's initial public offering opened for subscription today, July 22, and closes July 24, with units expected to list on the NSE and BSE on August 3. The trust set a price band of ₹151-152 per unit for the ₹5,000 crore offering, which is entirely an offer-for-sale by existing unit holders — meaning no fresh money goes to the trust itself, only to the investors selling their stakes. As of March 31, 2026, the trust's portfolio spans 27 operational toll roads covering 8,754 lane-kilometres across 12 states and one union territory, with total assets under management of about ₹36,842 crore. Ahead of the public offer, it also raised ₹1,250 crore from strategic investors including a firm called Prazim Trading and Investment.
key_points: Cube Highways Trust's IPO opened today and runs through July 24
Price band set at ₹151-152 per unit; the offer is entirely a sale by existing owners
It owns 27 toll highways spanning 8,754 lane-kilometres across 12 states
Backers include Abu Dhabi's ADIA, Mubadala and Canada's BCI pension fund

depth.background: An InvIT (Infrastructure Investment Trust) is India's version of a REIT (real estate investment trust) but for infrastructure — it lets ordinary investors buy a slice of income-generating assets like toll roads and collect a share of the cash they earn, instead of needing to buy and run a highway themselves. Cube Highways was set up in 2014 to buy and operate toll roads across India and has since built one of the country's largest private road portfolios, backed by large global infrastructure investors.

stories.title: Scientists found a way to make a 70-year-old antibiotic work again against superbugs it had stopped killing
stories.what_happened: Researchers at Scripps Research, publishing in Nature Communications, paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4 that blocks a bacterial enzyme (called SagA) the resistant bacteria rely on to fend off the drug. In lab tests, this combination restored vancomycin's ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium strains that had previously survived it. The molecule itself isn't an antibiotic — it's what's called an 'antibiotic adjuvant,' a helper compound that makes an existing drug work again.
key_points: Vancomycin is a 'last resort' antibiotic that some hospital superbugs now resist
Scripps Research scientists found a molecule that disables the bacteria's defense
Paired together, the old drug kills the resistant bacteria again in lab tests
The approach could work as a template for other failing antibiotics too

depth.background: Vancomycin has been one of medicine's most important 'last-line' antibiotics since the 1950s — the drug doctors reach for when other antibiotics have already failed. Over decades, a hospital-acquired bacterium called Enterococcus faecium evolved defenses against it (known as VRE, vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus), making some infections nearly untreatable. Antibiotic resistance broadly is one of modern medicine's biggest slow-moving crises: bacteria evolve faster than we invent new drugs.

stories.title: Iran strikes US allies Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan again — claims to hit an Amazon data centre and a US F-15
stories.what_happened: Iran's IRGC (paramilitary Revolutionary Guard) announced fresh missile and drone strikes on US-linked targets across Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, claiming it destroyed an Amazon Web Services data centre in Bahrain and a US F-15 fighter jet at a Jordanian base — claims that US and host-government officials have not confirmed. Separately, a commercial tanker off Oman was hit in the Strait of Hormuz, forcing its crew to abandon ship. It's the latest escalation after 11 consecutive nights of US airstrikes on Iran.
key_points: Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it fired on US-linked bases and infrastructure in three Gulf countries again
Claims include hitting Amazon's cloud data centre in Bahrain and destroying a US F-15 jet in Jordan — neither confirmed
Comes as the US bombs Iran for an 11th straight night, still without hitting the 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear site
A tanker was also struck in the Strait of Hormuz, forcing its crew to abandon ship

depth.background: In February 2026, Israel and the US struck Iran aiming to end its nuclear and missile programs; Iran's Supreme Leader was killed and the war has ground on in waves ever since, with brief ceasefires collapsing repeatedly. Since mid-July, unable to hit Israel or the US mainland directly, Iran has increasingly targeted US military bases and allied infrastructure in nearby Gulf states — Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan — that host American forces.

stories.title: Ukraine's drones hit Russia's biggest online retailer again, this time in the south
stories.what_happened: Ukrainian drones struck two more Wildberries logistics centres — in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk in southern Russia — in the early hours of Wednesday, July 22. Krasnodar regional officials said three people were injured at the warehouse there; Stavropol region's governor said five more were hurt at the Nevinnomyssk site. Wildberries confirmed the strikes but said the facilities were purely commercial.
key_points: Drones struck Wildberries warehouses in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk early Wednesday
At least 8 people hurt across the two sites, regional officials say
It's the second Wildberries hit in under a week — Kyiv says the sites double as military logistics hubs
Wildberries denies any military use; is Russia's largest e-commerce company

depth.background: Wildberries is essentially Russia's Amazon — a sprawling network of warehouses that ships everything from clothes to electronics across the country. On July 18, Ukrainian drones hit a Wildberries hub in Kotovsk, killing several workers, after President Zelensky said the facility was secretly being used to store navigation equipment and drone components for the Russian military. Moscow denied it. Ukraine has increasingly targeted logistics and industrial sites deep inside Russia, arguing that modern militaries can't fight without civilian supply chains feeding them.

stories.title: Nearly 1 in 4 US workers are stuck in jobs they hate just to keep their health insurance
stories.what_happened: A new survey from the West Health–Gallup Center on Healthcare in America finds that 24% of US employees — roughly 23 million adults — say they are staying in a job they would otherwise leave purely to hold on to employer health insurance, up from 16% in 2021. Among workers with a chronic health condition, that figure jumps to 41%. Ellyn Maese, the Center's research director, called the situation 'crazy' for people who want to leave but feel trapped.
key_points: 24% of US workers — about 23 million people — say they're staying in a job just to keep their health coverage
That's up from 16% in 2021; workers with chronic illness are hit hardest at 41%
The jump follows the expiration of enhanced Affordable Care Act subsidies at the end of 2025
Economists call this 'job lock' — it freezes people out of new jobs, startups, and early retirement

depth.background: In the US, most working-age adults get health insurance through their employer rather than the government — a quirk left over from WWII-era wage controls, when tax-free health benefits became a way for companies to compete for workers without breaking pay caps. The Affordable Care Act (ACA, 'Obamacare') created an alternative: marketplaces where people can buy their own individual plans. In 2021, Congress temporarily made those marketplace plans much cheaper with enhanced subsidies, which gave people real freedom to quit a job without losing affordable coverage.

stories.title: US carries out an 11th straight night of strikes on Iran as Trump threatens to bomb a power plant for every ship Iran hits
stories.what_happened: CENTCOM (the US military's Middle East command) carried out its 11th straight night of strikes on Iran late Tuesday, hitting military operations centers, aircraft hangars, drone-storage sites and logistics infrastructure meant to degrade Iran's ability to threaten shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's health ministry says the campaign has now killed 50 civilians and wounded 500. The Pentagon separately confirmed a 4th American service member has died in the conflict, with US wounded now well over 500. Trump added a new threat overnight: for every commercial ship Iran hits, the US will bomb an Iranian power plant.
key_points: US warplanes and ships struck Iranian military sites for about 75 minutes — the 11th consecutive night of strikes
Trump says he'll hit an Iranian power plant every time Iran attacks a commercial ship
Iran's health ministry says 50 civilians have been killed and 500 wounded by the US strikes so far
A 4th American service member's death in the war has now been confirmed, with over 500 US troops wounded

depth.background: The US and Israel opened this war on Iran back in late February 2026, and Washington has since run a nightly bombing campaign aimed at Iran's military, naval and nuclear-linked sites. A ceasefire announced in mid-June collapsed within weeks after Iran-linked strikes on tankers near the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway that a fifth of the world's oil passes through. Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth told Congress this week the war has already cost $37.5 billion, and yesterday Trump threatened to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site after Tehran struck three more Gulf allies (Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan).

thread.previously: Yesterday, Trump threatened to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site after Iran struck three more Gulf allies (Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan), as the Pentagon told Congress the war had already cost $37.5 billion.
thread.changed: The US has now completed an 11th consecutive night of strikes, Iran's health ministry has given its first civilian casualty count (50 killed, 500 wounded), a 4th American service member's death was confirmed, and Trump has issued a new, more specific threat: a power plant hit for every ship Iran attacks.

prev_ref.title: Trump threatens to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site as Tehran strikes three more Gulf allies

stories.title: NYC Mayor Mamdani says the city can't arrest Netanyahu — but says Washington should
stories.what_happened: Mamdani said late Tuesday that his administration reviewed every option and concluded New York City itself has no independent legal authority to enforce the ICC's warrant. In a video message, he called Netanyahu a 'war criminal,' said he is not welcome in New York, and called on the US federal government to join the ICC and execute the warrant on Washington's behalf. President Trump has already said Netanyahu will not be arrested when he visits the city in September, effectively closing that door at the federal level too.
key_points: Mamdani: NYC lacks legal authority to execute the ICC's Netanyahu arrest warrant
He calls Netanyahu a 'war criminal' and says he's not welcome in the city
He urges the federal government to join the ICC and act on the warrant instead
Trump has separately said Netanyahu 'will not be arrested' if he visits NYC in September

depth.background: In November 2024, the International Criminal Court (ICC) — a permanent tribunal in The Hague that prosecutes war crimes and crimes against humanity when national courts won't — issued an arrest warrant for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, accusing him of using starvation as a weapon of war and directing attacks on civilians in Gaza. The US and Israel don't recognize the ICC's jurisdiction over them, so the warrant has never been enforced. Zohran Mamdani, a vocal critic of Israel's Gaza campaign, became New York City's mayor this year and has repeatedly said he'd have Netanyahu arrested if he set foot in the city.
