stories.title: Drone strike sets fire near Russia's largest steel plant in Lipetsk
stories.what_happened: Overnight on July 21–22, explosions and a large fire (reported to cover roughly 2,500 square metres) broke out in Lipetsk near the Novolipetsk Metallurgical Plant (NLMK), one of Russia's biggest steelworks, and near a power station (CHPP-2). Russian regional governor Igor Artamonov confirmed a fire at industrial facilities but said the cause was still under investigation and did not confirm an attack. Ukrainian officials have not publicly claimed it.
key_points: Overnight explosions and a fire broke out near NLMK's Lipetsk steelworks
NLMK makes ~18% of Russia's total steel and 14 million tonnes a year
A power plant (CHPP-2) in the same area also caught fire
Russian officials confirm the fire but not the cause; Kyiv hasn't commented

depth.background: Since the full-scale war began, Ukraine has increasingly used long-range drones to hit Russian industrial and energy sites far from the front line — refineries, power stations, and factories that help fund or supply the Russian war effort — rather than only battlefield targets. Lipetsk, about 400km south of Moscow, is normally well outside the usual strike zone, making a hit there notable.

stories.title: The US launches its 11th straight night of strikes on Iran — and still hasn't hit Pickaxe Mountain
stories.what_happened: US Central Command said it launched an 11th straight night of strikes on Iranian military targets late Tuesday, extending a bombing campaign that has now run for over a week and a half without a resolution. Despite Trump's public threat, the US has not yet struck Pickaxe Mountain itself. War Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate this week the campaign has already cost $37.5 billion, with the administration asking Congress for $67 billion more. Iran's military command warned that any strike on its nuclear or other sensitive sites would be treated as an escalation of the war across the region.
key_points: CENTCOM confirmed an 11th consecutive night of US strikes on Iranian military targets
Trump repeated his threat to hit the fortified 'Pickaxe Mountain' site but gave no timing
Iran's Khatam al-Anbiya command warned a strike on nuclear sites would expand the war
US officials say Trump faces a fork: a new 10-day ceasefire, or a bigger joint campaign with Israel

depth.background: The US and Iran have been trading direct strikes for over a week and a half after the fight over the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil — reignited. On Tuesday, July 21, Trump named Pickaxe Mountain, a fortified underground tunnel complex near Iran's Natanz nuclear site, as a target 'pretty soon.' Iran responded the same day by striking Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan — three US-allied Gulf states hosting American bases — rather than absorbing the threat quietly.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Trump named Pickaxe Mountain as a target 'pretty soon, and very heavily' for the first time, on the same day Iran struck Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan in retaliation — the 10th straight night of US strikes.
thread.changed: The US has now extended the campaign to an 11th straight night without actually striking Pickaxe Mountain, and officials have laid out the two broad paths Trump is weighing next: a new ceasefire or a bigger joint campaign with Israel.

prev_ref.title: Trump threatens to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site as Tehran strikes three more Gulf allies

stories.title: President Murmu makes history as first Indian head of state to visit North Macedonia, pushes to double trade
stories.what_happened: President Murmu held bilateral talks with North Macedonia's president and prime minister, addressed the country's Assembly (parliament), and spoke at the India-North Macedonia Business Forum, where she urged both countries to aim to double their bilateral trade volume by the end of the decade and to diversify trade into new sectors.
key_points: Murmu addressed the India-North Macedonia Business Forum in Skopje
She called for doubling bilateral trade by the end of the decade
Part of a three-nation Europe tour: Moldova, North Macedonia, Romania
Focus areas flagged: agriculture, pharma, IT and tourism

depth.background: India has been working to deepen ties with Central and Eastern Europe as it looks to diversify trade partnerships beyond its traditional markets. President Droupadi Murmu began a tour of Moldova, North Macedonia and Romania on July 19 — her stop in North Macedonia is the first-ever visit by an Indian head of state to the country.

stories.title: Oil jumps toward $95 a barrel as the US-Iran ceasefire fully collapses and Rubio calls Iran 'not serious' about peace
stories.what_happened: US Central Command carried out an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran this week, as Secretary of State Marco Rubio declared Tehran 'not serious' about diplomatic talks — a notable hardening from his comments just a day earlier that the US remained open to a deal. Brent crude jumped nearly 5% toward $95 a barrel and US pump prices extended their climb above $4 a gallon as traders priced in a ceasefire that looks fully collapsed rather than merely strained.
key_points: Brent crude rose as much as 4.9% toward $95/barrel, a nearly six-week high
US strikes hit Iran for an 11th straight night as diplomacy stalls
Rubio says Tehran is 'not serious' about talks — a sharp reversal from his stance a day earlier
US pump prices are climbing again, extending their rise above $4/gallon

depth.background: The US and Iran agreed a ceasefire in April after a direct war, and signed a memorandum of understanding in June meant to lead to a lasting peace. That truce has repeatedly broken down since, with both sides trading strikes — the pattern our earlier coverage has tracked through Iran's tanker attacks and Trump's retaliation threats.

thread.previously: We reported Rubio saying the US remained open to an Iran deal, while warning that Iran's demand over a Hormuz shipping toll could 'threaten the world economy.'
thread.changed: Rubio has now hardened his tone, calling Iran 'not serious' about talks, as US strikes reach an 11th straight night and oil jumps toward $95 a barrel — the ceasefire looks fully collapsed rather than just strained.

prev_ref.title: Rubio says the US is open to an Iran deal, but warns Iran's Hormuz toll demand would 'threaten the world economy'

stories.title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine's top general and defense minister in one week, installs a younger war cabinet
stories.what_happened: President Volodymyr Zelenskyy fired commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi on Wednesday and replaced him with 43-year-old General Mykhailo Drapatyi, described as part of a newer generation of commanders. The move came days after Zelenskyy removed defense minister and close ally Mykhailo Fedorov, whose public clashes with Syrskyi over military strategy triggered rare street protests in Kyiv. Drapatyi said the military would 'continue and intensify offensive operations in all domains.'
key_points: Zelenskyy sacked army chief Oleksandr Syrskyi, days after removing defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov
43-year-old Mykhailo Drapatyi named the new commander-in-chief
Move followed a week of rare wartime protests in Kyiv over war strategy
Drapatyi has pledged to 'intensify offensive operations in all domains'

depth.background: Ukraine has fought Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022. Zelenskyy had largely kept the same senior military and political team in place for most of the war, valuing continuity. Syrskyi, a Soviet-trained general nicknamed 'the Butcher' by critics for costly battlefield decisions, had led Ukraine's armed forces since February 2024. Frustration over his strategy — and over the war effort's broader direction — had been building for months among soldiers and the public.

stories.title: BCCI bans no-ball offenders for the rest of the match and axes forfeiture in new domestic playing rules
stories.what_happened: The BCCI announced playing-condition changes for India's 2026-27 domestic season. The headline change: a bowler found by umpires to have deliberately bowled a no-ball — overstepping or not releasing the ball properly — is now suspended for the remainder of the match, up from just the rest of that innings. The board also scrapped the option for teams to declare or forfeit the final innings of a red-ball match, redefined what counts as an 'overthrow,' and eased wicketkeeper glove-positioning rules.
key_points: A bowler ruled to have deliberately bowled a no-ball is now suspended for the rest of the match, not just the innings
Teams can no longer declare or forfeit an innings to manufacture a red-ball result
Wicketkeepers only need gloves behind the stumps at the moment of delivery, not through the run-up
New rules apply from next month's Duleep Trophy, the start of the 2026-27 domestic season

depth.background: Domestic cricket boards set their own playing conditions and often trial changes before they're proposed as international law through the MCC, cricket's rule-making body. India's BCCI runs the world's largest domestic cricket program, and its rule changes often shape the global conversation about the sport's laws.

stories.title: Verstappen can now trigger an exit clause to leave Red Bull for 2027 — and rival drivers are already calling
stories.what_happened: A clause in Verstappen's Red Bull contract has been triggered because he finished the Belgian Grand Prix outside the top two in the championship standings, the threshold written into his deal. That gives him the option — not the obligation — to leave for another team in 2027. Red Bull principal Laurent Mekies confirmed this week that 'many good drivers' have already been in touch about a potential Red Bull seat, while saying he remains confident Verstappen will stay.
key_points: A performance clause in Verstappen's contract activates because he wasn't top-2 in the standings after Belgium
Red Bull boss Laurent Mekies admits 'many good drivers' are already enquiring about a seat
17 F1 driver contracts expire after this season, setting up a chaotic 2027 market
Verstappen's camp says a decision could come before the summer break

depth.background: Max Verstappen has won four world championships with Red Bull, but the team's form has dipped this season against Mercedes and Ferrari. Like most top F1 drivers, his contract — running on paper through 2028 — contains performance-linked clauses letting him leave early if results fall short of a set bar, a common way both driver and team hedge against decline.

stories.title: A US judge sets a June 2027 trial date for Venezuela's ousted leader Maduro
stories.what_happened: At a hearing in Manhattan federal court on Wednesday, Judge Alvin Hellerstein set June 1, 2027 as the trial date for Maduro and Flores. He also scheduled a separate hearing for November on a defense motion to dismiss the case on grounds of sovereign immunity — the argument that Maduro cannot be prosecuted in a US court because he was acting as the head of a sovereign nation.
key_points: Judge sets June 1, 2027 as the trial date for Maduro and wife Cilia Flores
Both face US drug-trafficking, kidnapping and murder charges
Maduro was captured by US special forces in Caracas in January
Defense will argue in November that Maduro is immune from US prosecution

depth.background: In January, US special forces carried out an overnight raid on Nicolás Maduro's home in Caracas and flew him and his wife, Cilia Flores, to the United States, ending his rule of Venezuela. Since then the country has been run by his former vice president, Delcy Rodríguez, who has built closer ties with the Trump administration. US federal prosecutors indicted Maduro and Flores in New York, accusing them of conspiring with drug cartels to move thousands of tons of cocaine into the US. Both pleaded not guilty and, through their lawyers, have disputed the very legitimacy of trying a former head of state in a US court.

stories.title: A Chinese coast guard attack injures a Philippine sailor near a disputed reef — Manila summons Beijing's envoy as the US, Japan and Australia rally behind it
stories.what_happened: Chinese coast guard personnel clashed with Philippine Navy personnel at Ayungin Shoal on Monday, July 20, injuring at least one Filipino sailor. President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. summoned China's ambassador, Jing Quan, to the presidential palace on Wednesday to protest the incident; China, in turn, summoned the Philippine ambassador in Beijing. Days later, Marcos met US Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who underscored Washington's commitment to freedom of navigation in the sea, and separately met with Japanese and Australian counterparts.
key_points: Chinese coast guard personnel injured a Philippine Navy sailor at Ayungin Shoal on Monday
Marcos summoned China's ambassador; Beijing summoned the Philippine envoy right back
It's the 10th anniversary of the 2016 international ruling that favored the Philippines over China here
US Secretary of State Rubio met Marcos this week to reaffirm support for freedom of navigation

depth.background: Ayungin Shoal (also called Second Thomas Shoal) is a reef in the South China Sea inside the Philippines' internationally recognized economic zone, but China claims almost the entire sea as its own. The Philippines keeps a small marine garrison on a deliberately grounded, rusting warship there to assert its claim; China's coast guard regularly harasses supply missions to it with water cannons and blocking maneuvers. In 2016, an international tribunal ruled decisively that China's sweeping claim has no legal basis — a ruling Beijing has ignored ever since.

stories.title: Trump unveils a phased tariff plan on generic drugs — up to 200% within three years
stories.what_happened: President Trump announced that imported generic drugs will face 0% tariffs for the next two years (effective August 1, 2026), rising to 100% for a third year, and 200% after that — explicitly designed to give foreign manufacturers a window to build US plants or face escalating penalties. This is separate from tariffs on large branded pharmaceutical companies' imports, which take effect July 31 for a list of 17 major drugmakers. Trump's statement did not name specific countries, companies, or how exemptions (for those who do start building US plants) would be verified.
key_points: Generic drug imports face 0% tariffs for 2 years, then 100%, then 200% after that
Aim: force overseas generic drugmakers to build US factories or pay steep penalties
Separately, tariffs on big branded/patented drugmakers' imports already start July 31
No countries or companies named yet, and no exemption details given
India, the world's largest supplier of generic medicines, has the most at stake

depth.background: A 'generic' drug is a cheaper copy of a brand-name medicine, sold once the original's patent expires — most pills Americans take (and most global medicine supply) are generics, and a huge share are made in India and, to a lesser extent, China. The Trump administration has spent 2026 rolling out tariffs (an extra tax on imported goods, paid by the importer and usually passed on in prices) on pharmaceuticals in stages, first targeting big branded-drug makers, and has repeatedly threatened action on generics to bring manufacturing back to the US ('reshoring').

stories.title: Yemen's Houthis open a second shipping front, blockading the Red Sea's Bab al-Mandeb Strait
stories.what_happened: This week the Houthis announced a full naval blockade of Saudi Arabia, using the threat of missile and drone strikes near Bab al-Mandeb to force ships away. Within days, at least seven tankers bound for Saudi ports reversed course rather than risk it, and a Cosco-operated Chinese vehicle carrier turned back after receiving a direct warning from Houthi forces. Some vessels are now taking the much longer route around via the Suez Canal instead. The Joint Maritime Information Center — the body that warns commercial sailors of threats — told mariners the Houthis have finished positioning weapons near the strait, though no ship has actually been struck yet.
key_points: Houthis declare a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia via the Bab al-Mandeb Strait
At least 7 oil tankers have already turned around rather than risk it
A Chinese vehicle carrier reversed course after a Houthi warning email
Missiles and drones are reportedly positioned near the strait, but no ship has been hit yet
This opens a SECOND global shipping chokepoint crisis on top of the Strait of Hormuz

depth.background: Bab al-Mandeb ('Gate of Tears') is the narrow strait between Yemen and the Horn of Africa that roughly 10% of global seaborne trade passes through on its way to and from the Suez Canal. Yemen's Houthi rebels, an armed group backed by Iran, have attacked Red Sea shipping before (2023-2025) in what they called solidarity with Gaza. That campaign quieted down earlier this year. But the US and Iran have been at war for weeks over the OTHER key chokepoint, the Strait of Hormuz (where a fifth of the world's oil passes) — and the Houthis are Iran's ally.

stories.title: India and the US race to close a trade deal before Thursday's tariff deadline — India still holding out
stories.what_happened: Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal completed a two-day round of talks with US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer, with both sides calling the progress 'substantial.' Washington has already cut its reciprocal tariff on Indian goods from 25% to 18% as talks progressed. But no deal has been signed, and reports describe the legal text as sitting at the 'last 1 per cent.' India is deliberately not rushing to meet the July 24 deadline, instead pushing for tariff terms more competitive than those given to Vietnam, Indonesia and other Asian exporters it competes with.
key_points: Interim US tariff arrangement (including an extra 10% levy) expires July 24
Deal described as being at the 'last 1%' of legal text, but still unsigned
India wants better tariff terms than Asian rivals get, not just any deal by the deadline
Washington already cut its reciprocal tariff on Indian goods from 25% to 18%
Goal on both sides: $500 billion in two-way trade by 2030 ('Mission 500')

depth.background: Since April 2026, the US has run a 'reciprocal tariff' regime — extra import taxes matched roughly to what other countries charge US goods — with a temporary lower rate for countries actively negotiating trade deals. India has been negotiating a first-phase Bilateral Trade Agreement with the US covering market access, digital trade rules, and non-tariff barriers (rules and standards, rather than tariffs, that make imports harder), aiming to eventually double trade to $500 billion by 2030.

stories.title: Rahul Gandhi, Priyanka Gandhi and Akhilesh Yadav detained, then freed, after a sit-in outside PM Modi's residence
stories.what_happened: Congress leader Rahul Gandhi, his sister Priyanka Gandhi Vadra, and Samajwadi Party chief Akhilesh Yadav led a sit-in protest march from Rajaji Marg to Prime Minister Narendra Modi's residence at 7 Lok Kalyan Marg, demanding accountability for police action against students and the resignation of Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. Delhi Police forcibly removed the protesters: Rahul Gandhi sat on the road and was carried by several officers into a police bus, while Priyanka Gandhi Vadra was pushed into a bus as well. Senior Congress figures including Mallikarjun Kharge also joined. All those detained were released later that evening; former Congress president Sonia Gandhi visited Priyanka before her release.
key_points: Top opposition leaders sat down on the road outside Modi's official residence, refusing to move
Delhi Police physically carried Rahul Gandhi and pushed Priyanka Gandhi into a police bus
Akhilesh Yadav (Samajwadi Party chief) was also forcibly removed
Demand: resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the NEET-UG paper leak
All were released hours later; Sonia Gandhi visited Priyanka before her release

depth.background: This escalation sits inside a much bigger story we've been tracking: the 'Cockroach' movement — a student-led protest movement that grew out of anger over India's NEET-UG medical entrance exam, after its 2026 paper was leaked and about 2.2 million students had to retake it under heavy security. A youth march ('Chalo Sansad') toward Parliament days earlier was met with tear gas and baton charges, injuring students, which is what pulled the opposition's top leadership into the streets themselves.

thread.previously: We explained how a joke about students calling themselves 'cockroaches' (able to survive anything) grew into the youth-led Cockroach Janta Party movement demanding accountability over the NEET paper leak.
thread.changed: The movement's demands have now pulled in the national opposition leadership directly — Rahul Gandhi, Priyanka Gandhi and Akhilesh Yadav were themselves detained by police outside the PM's residence, escalating this from a youth protest into a full opposition-versus-government confrontation.

prev_ref.title: India's 'Cockroach' movement, explained: how a joke about students became a nationwide challenge to Modi

stories.title: Aston Martin's biggest upgrade of the season lands in Hungary — new chassis, new nose, reworked aero
stories.what_happened: Aston Martin has confirmed its single biggest upgrade package of the season will run for the first time at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix: a re-homologated (formally re-certified with the FIA) lighter chassis, a new nose, and reworked aerodynamics, all designed under Newey. The upgrade lands on a Hungaroring circuit that has also had sections resurfaced at Turns 1 and 12, adding another variable for every team to adapt to.
key_points: Package includes a re-homologated (re-certified) lighter chassis, new nose and reworked aerodynamics
Designed by Adrian Newey, F1's most decorated car designer, in his first full season at Aston Martin
Arrives after a difficult season for the team and Newey's own recent health scare
Comes on newly resurfaced sections of the Hungaroring (Turns 1 and 12)

depth.background: We've been following Aston Martin's rocky 2026 season and the pressure on Adrian Newey, the legendary designer behind championship-winning cars at Williams, McLaren and Red Bull, who joined Aston Martin to help engineer a turnaround. His future — and that of driver Fernando Alonso, a two-time champion weighing whether to keep racing — has been tied to whether this promised 'big upgrade' actually delivers on track.

thread.previously: Newey had said his major upgrade for Aston Martin would arrive at the Hungarian Grand Prix, after a difficult season and his own health scare.
thread.changed: The upgrade's specifics are now confirmed: a re-homologated lighter chassis, new nose and reworked aero — the team's biggest single package of 2026, arriving on a freshly resurfaced Hungaroring.

prev_ref.title: Adrian Newey says his big Aston Martin upgrade lands in Hungary — after a torrid season and his own health scare

stories.title: Antonelli's championship lead grows to 45 points as Russell slips to third after a scoreless Spa
stories.what_happened: After Antonelli's win at Spa-Francorchamps (his sixth of the season), the standings now read: Antonelli 204 points, Hamilton 159, and Russell a further 50 points back in third after retiring from Spa with zero points, following a collision with Hamilton on the opening lap. Heading into the Hungarian Grand Prix — round 11 of 2026, and the final race before F1's four-week summer shutdown — Antonelli holds a commanding 45-point cushion.
key_points: Antonelli: 204 points, Hamilton: 159, Russell now a further 50 points back
Russell failed to score at Spa after a first-lap collision with former teammate Hamilton
This flips the story we told earlier this week — Hamilton, not Russell, now clearly F1's #2
Hungary is the last race before F1's 4-week summer break — a title cushion matters more now

depth.background: We've been tracking the emerging title fight between Mercedes rookie sensation Kimi Antonelli and Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton, whose 2026 form has surged after a rocky start following his Ferrari move. George Russell, Antonelli's Mercedes teammate, had also been in that mix — but a first-lap incident at the Belgian Grand Prix (Spa) involving Hamilton has now dropped him well behind both.

thread.previously: We reported Hamilton's Ferrari turnaround had lifted him to third in the drivers' standings, chasing an eighth title.
thread.changed: The picture has since clarified: Hamilton is actually second, not third — it's Russell who has dropped to third after scoring zero points at Spa, and Antonelli's lead has grown to a commanding 45 points.

prev_ref.title: Lewis Hamilton says he 'won't stop' until an 8th title, as his Ferrari turnaround puts him 3rd in the standings

stories.title: US House passes stopgap funding bill to push shutdown fight past the midterms — Senate could still blow it up
stories.what_happened: The House voted 220-205 on Tuesday to extend government funding through December 4 at existing levels. Only six Democrats crossed the aisle to support it, while Kentucky Republican Thomas Massie was the sole GOP defector. House Democratic leaders opposed it, saying it fails to rein in the administration's immigration enforcement funding. The bill now moves to the Senate, where it needs 60 votes to survive a filibuster — meaning Republicans need at least seven Democratic votes they do not currently have.
key_points: House votes 220-205 to fund the government at current levels through December 4
Only 6 Democrats crossed over; nearly all Republicans backed it except one
Bill needs 60 votes in the Senate, where Republicans hold 53 — a real shutdown risk
Government funding otherwise runs out September 30, days before the midterm campaign closes

depth.background: The US Congress has to pass funding bills every year to keep federal agencies open; when it can't agree, the government 'shuts down' — federal workers get furloughed, some services stop, and it becomes a political blame game. This is a 'continuing resolution' (CR): a stopgap that just keeps last year's spending levels running for a few more months instead of passing a full new budget, buying time for a fuller deal later.

stories.title: West Indies deny New Zealand a clean sweep, win the fifth ODI as series ends 3-2
stories.what_happened: New Zealand batted first at Kensington Oval in Bridgetown and posted 268/9 in 50 overs, built on half-centuries from Tom Latham (69), Nick Kelly (64) and Will Young (56). West Indies slipped to 123/4 chasing 269, but Shimron Hetmyer built a 91-run partnership with Sherfane Rutherford before finishing the chase unbeaten on 69, getting his side home in 47.3 overs — a two-wicket win with 15 balls left. Hetmyer was named Player of the Match.
key_points: West Indies chased down 269 to win the fifth and final ODI by 2 wickets, with 15 balls to spare
Shimron Hetmyer's unbeaten 69 anchored the chase and won him Player of the Match
New Zealand had already sealed the series 3-1 before this dead-rubber match
Final result: New Zealand win the series 3-2, missing out on a whitewash

depth.background: This was the fifth and final match of a five-game One Day International (ODI) series between touring New Zealand and hosts West Indies, played across Guyana and Barbados through July. New Zealand had already wrapped up the series with a nail-biting one-wicket win in the fourth match, making this last game a 'dead rubber' — one that no longer decided the series, but still mattered for pride, ranking points and individual form.

stories.title: Nicaragua's Ortega declares 'never, never, never' to future elections, cementing 20-year rule
stories.what_happened: Speaking in a public address that attacked the United States, Ortega said Nicaragua 'won't be holding any more elections' — 'never, never, never.' The comment effectively kills the general election originally due in November 2027, a vote his own Congress had already pushed back once, in January 2025, when it voted to stretch the presidential term from five to six years. There is now no legal mechanism or public timeline for Nicaraguans to vote him out.
key_points: Ortega says Nicaragua will hold no more elections, effectively cancelling the 2027 vote
His rubber-stamp Congress already extended his term by a year back in January 2025
At least 46 people are jailed in Nicaragua for political reasons, rights groups say
Opposition calls it confirmation that the country is now a one-party dictatorship

depth.background: Daniel Ortega, a former Sandinista revolutionary leader, returned to Nicaragua's presidency in 2007 after first ruling the country in the 1980s. Since then he has rewritten the constitution to remove term limits, jailed or exiled nearly every serious political rival, and packed the courts, electoral council, and army with loyalists — including his wife Rosario Murillo, who is now co-president. In the 2021 election, he ran essentially unopposed after arresting every candidate who could have beaten him; the vote was widely called illegitimate abroad.

stories.title: Wall Street snaps a 3-day losing streak on a chip-stock rally — but JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon says he wouldn't buy stocks or bonds at these prices
stories.what_happened: The S&P 500 rose 0.89% to close near 7,509 on Tuesday, its first gain in four days, as semiconductor (computer-chip) stocks rallied on unexpectedly strong export numbers from South Korea and Taiwan — two countries whose chip exports are seen as an early signal of global tech demand. At the same time, Brent crude oil (the global benchmark price for oil) jumped 2.26% to $91.24 a barrel on fears that the US-Iran war could disrupt Middle East oil supply. In a podcast interview recorded July 16 and released this week, Dimon said he wouldn't buy either stocks or long-dated US government bonds (Treasurys) at current prices, warning that investors are underpricing risks from the Ukraine and Middle East wars, US-China tensions, and rising military spending against a backdrop of large government deficits.
key_points: S&P 500 closed up 0.89% near 7,509, breaking three straight days of losses
Semiconductor stocks jumped on stronger-than-expected export data from South Korea and Taiwan
Brent crude oil hit $91.24 (+2.26%) on Middle East supply fears, a headwind for inflation
JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon says markets are underestimating how big the risks really are

depth.background: The S&P 500 is the benchmark index of America's 500 biggest public companies — when people say 'the market,' this is usually what they mean. It had fallen for three straight sessions as investors grew nervous about the US-Iran conflict and its effect on oil supplies. Jamie Dimon runs JPMorgan Chase, America's biggest bank, and is one of Wall Street's most-watched voices because he sits at the center of the financial system and talks to CEOs and governments constantly.

stories.title: Trump unveils a 100–200% tariff on generic drugs — with a two-year grace period to reshore production
stories.what_happened: President Trump announced that generic drug imports into the US will keep a 0% tariff for two years starting August 1, 2026, after which the tariff jumps to 100%, then to 200% a year after that. He said the delay gives companies time to "reshore" — build plants and equipment inside the US — and that firms which don't will face the penalty. It follows an earlier order this year putting a 100% tariff on branded (patented) drugs unless makers accept US price deals or build domestically.
key_points: Generic drugs stay tariff-free for two years starting August 1
Then the tariff jumps to 100%, and to 200% a year after that
Aimed at forcing drugmakers to build factories inside the US
Follows an earlier 100% tariff Trump already slapped on branded drugs

depth.background: Generic drugs are copies of medicines whose patents have expired — they're not fancy, but they're what most people actually take (blood-pressure pills, antibiotics, insulin). The US makes very little of its own generic drug supply anymore; countries like India make most of it cheaply and ship it over. Trump has spent 2026 trying to force manufacturing of all kinds — chips, cars, now medicine — back onto US soil using tariffs as the stick.

stories.title: F2's Belgian round gets stranger: a driver disqualified over a pit-lane headset, and Tsolov's title lead grows to 27 points
stories.what_happened: FIA stewards disqualified Campos Racing's Noel Leon from the F2 feature race at Spa-Francorchamps after finding that an unauthorized person used a headset with a microphone in the pit lane during the race, breaching F2's sporting regulations. Leon lost his seventh-place finish and its points; Campos Racing was fined €20,000 and had 25 championship points withdrawn, though that points penalty is suspended unless the team breaches the same rule again this season. With Leon's points erased, series leader Nikola Tsolov's advantage over second-placed Gabriele Mini grew to 27 points, while Rafael Camara closed in on the top two with pole position and his second win of the season.
key_points: Campos Racing's Noel Leon disqualified from the Spa feature race
Reason: an unauthorized person wore a headset/microphone in the pit lane during the race
Campos fined €20,000; 25 championship points withdrawn from the team (suspended)
Nikola Tsolov's championship lead over Gabriele Mini grows to 27 points

depth.background: Yesterday we reported that Red Bull junior Nikola Tsolov extended his F2 (Formula 2, the feeder series just below F1) points lead after the Spa round, with Alpine's Gabriele Mini second and Ferrari junior Rafael Camara closing in. F2 stewards enforce strict rules about who is allowed to communicate with cars and pit crews during a race.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported that after the Spa round, Nikola Tsolov led the F2 standings with 161 points from six wins, with Gabriele Mini second on 134, Rafael Camara third on 125 and Alex Dunne fourth on 108.
thread.changed: Today, a stewards' disqualification of Noel Leon over an illegal pit-lane headset erased points from that race, widening Tsolov's lead over Mini to 27 points and fining his team €20,000.

prev_ref.title: F1's next generation duels it out: Tsolov stretches his F2 points lead as the junior title fight tightens at Spa

stories.title: Babar Azam marks his return as Pakistan Test captain with a century in the West Indies tour warm-up
stories.what_happened: In Pakistan's warm-up match against a West Indies Select XI at Tarouba, Trinidad, Babar Azam scored an unbeaten 100 off 151 balls — with 12 fours and a six — before retiring out, his first innings since being reappointed Pakistan's Test captain. Opener Azan Awais also made a century as Pakistan posted 455/4 on day one, while bowler Mohammad Ali took three wickets to reduce the West Indies Select XI to 268/8. The two-match Test series against West Indies begins July 25 at the same venue, followed by a three-match series in England from August 19.
key_points: Babar scored 100 off 151 balls (12 fours, a six) before retiring out
Opener Azan Awais also made a century as Pakistan piled up 455/4 on day one
It's Babar's first innings back as Test captain, replacing axed Shan Masood
The two-match Test series against West Indies starts July 25 in Trinidad

depth.background: Babar Azam is Pakistan's most prolific modern batter and a former captain across formats, having earlier lost the Test captaincy. The Pakistan Cricket Board recently reappointed him Test captain for the upcoming West Indies and England tours, replacing Shan Masood, as the team looks to arrest a difficult run in the format.

stories.title: Maruti Suzuki hikes car prices by up to ₹30,000 from August — its second increase in two months
stories.what_happened: In a stock-exchange filing on July 21, Maruti Suzuki said it will raise prices across its model range by up to ₹30,000 from August 2026, citing persistent input-cost inflation. The company did not detail model-by-model increases. It's the second hike in as many months, following a similar move in June.
key_points: Prices rise by up to ₹30,000 across Maruti's range starting August 2026
The company blames sustained input-cost inflation, not a one-off shock
It's Maruti's second price hike in quick succession after a June increase
Tata Motors, Hyundai and Kia have also raised prices recently

depth.background: Maruti Suzuki is India's largest carmaker, selling roughly two in five new cars in the country. Automakers periodically raise prices to offset rising costs of steel, aluminium and other components, but rapid back-to-back hikes signal costs are climbing faster than firms can absorb through internal savings alone.

stories.title: Nicholas Pooran blitzes 58* as MI London chase down Sunrisers Leeds by 7 wickets in The Hundred's men's opener
stories.what_happened: MI London captain Sam Curran won the toss and chose to field first at The Oval. Sunrisers Leeds were bowled out for 143, with Mitchell Marsh (41 off 27) and Tom Alsop (31 off 18) top-scoring before Richard Gleeson's 4-25 broke through the middle order. Chasing 144, MI London slipped to 4-2 after Brydon Carse struck twice early, but Curran (60* off 39) and Pooran (58* off just 22 balls) put on a rapid stand to seal victory with 16 balls unused.
key_points: Sunrisers Leeds bowled out for 143; Richard Gleeson took 4-25
MI London reduced to 4-2 early, then rebuilt around Sam Curran's 60* and Pooran's 58*
MI London won by 7 wickets with 16 balls to spare
Pooran (58* off 22 balls, plus 2 catches) named Player of the Match

depth.background: The Hundred is England's short, simplified white-ball franchise competition — 100 balls per side instead of the usual 120 — designed to be quicker and more accessible than a normal T20 match, and it runs men's and women's competitions side by side each English summer. MI London and Sunrisers Leeds are two of the eight city-based franchises, each stocked with a mix of English players and short-term overseas signings like West Indies' Nicholas Pooran.

stories.title: Nicaragua's Ortega vows Nicaragua will 'never again' hold elections — Rubio calls for the country to be isolated
stories.what_happened: During a speech marking the 47th anniversary of the Sandinista revolution on July 19, Ortega declared Nicaragua would never hold elections again, saying he would work with parliament to 'put up a wall' against opposition parties trying to 'seize power.' Two days later, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio publicly condemned the move, saying Ortega and Murillo have 'abandoned even the pretense of popular consent,' and called on the international community to isolate Nicaragua diplomatically and economically.
key_points: Ortega says there will be no more elections, scrapping the 2027 vote
He accuses opposition parties of being US-controlled 'Somocistas'
US Secretary of State Rubio calls it proof of 'true authoritarian nature'
Rubio urges other countries to stop 'business as usual' with Nicaragua

depth.background: Daniel Ortega, 80, is a former Sandinista revolutionary who has ruled Nicaragua continuously since 2007 (he first led the country in the 1980s too). His wife, Rosario Murillo, has been made co-president, and the family has spent a decade jailing opponents, closing independent media and NGOs, and rewriting the constitution — including a change that already stretched the presidential term from five to six years, pushing the next election back to 2027.

stories.title: Houthis' Red Sea 'embargo' turns real — Saudi oil tankers start U-turning as the threat to enter the US-Iran war grows
stories.what_happened: By Tuesday, July 21, the threat stopped being just words: at least five tankers carrying Saudi crude reversed course in the southern Red Sea rather than risk the Bab al-Mandeb strait, the narrow chokepoint Houthi fighters would need to control to make good on the embargo. Two named examples — the Xin Long Yang, loaded with 2 million barrels for China, and the Rodos, carrying about 700,000 barrels for India — both abandoned the strait for the far longer Suez Canal route. Saudi Arabia rejected the Houthi embargo claim as 'disinformation,' saying it has supported Yemen with aid and fuel, and the UN called for immediate de-escalation.
key_points: At least 5 Saudi-linked oil tankers have now reversed course in the Red Sea
Two big examples: a China-bound ship carrying 2 million barrels, an India-bound one with 700,000
Ships are taking the long way round via the Suez Canal instead of the Bab al-Mandeb strait
Analysts warn oil could push above $100 a barrel if the rerouting continues

depth.background: Yemen's Houthis are an Iran-backed armed group that controls most of northern Yemen after a decade-long civil war against a Saudi-led coalition. Since the Gaza war began in 2023, they've repeatedly attacked Red Sea shipping they tie to Israel, forcing many shipping firms to avoid the route already. On Monday, July 20, Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree declared an immediate 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia, saying it was retaliation for a Saudi blockade of Yemen and a strike on Sanaa's airport — an announcement made with no real detail on how it would be enforced.

thread.previously: Yesterday, Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree declared an immediate naval blockade of Saudi Arabia, framing it as retaliation for a Saudi blockade of Yemen and a strike on Sanaa's airport.
thread.changed: The blockade turned real: at least five Saudi-linked oil tankers have now actually reversed course away from the Bab al-Mandeb strait, and analysts warn oil could break above $100 a barrel if the rerouting continues.

prev_ref.title: Yemen's Houthis declare a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia — a new front opens in the widening Gulf war

stories.title: Hashmatullah Shahidi resigns as Afghanistan's ODI captain
stories.what_happened: ESPNcricinfo reported on 21 July that Shahidi has resigned as Afghanistan's ODI captain, ending a tenure in which he led the side in 55 ODIs. The report did not detail his reasoning, and the Afghanistan Cricket Board has not yet named who will replace him.
key_points: Steps down after leading Afghanistan in 55 One Day Internationals (ODIs)
His reign included famous 2023 World Cup wins over England, Pakistan and Sri Lanka
Comes weeks after Ibrahim Zadran replaced Rashid Khan as Afghanistan's T20I captain
Afghanistan Cricket Board has not yet named a successor

depth.background: Hashmatullah Shahidi, a steady middle-order batter, took over as Afghanistan's ODI and Test captain in 2021 after the board removed Asghar Afghan. Under him, Afghanistan grew from a plucky underdog into a genuine 50-over threat, peaking at the 2023 World Cup in India where they beat three former champions — England, Pakistan and Sri Lanka — a run that put the cricket world on notice. That rise has come alongside broader turbulence in Afghan cricket leadership: after the team crashed out in the group stage of the 2026 T20 World Cup, the board already replaced Rashid Khan with Ibrahim Zadran as T20I captain.

stories.title: Punjab Police say they've foiled a UK-directed plot to bomb a train — 17 arrested over the Moga police-station blast
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 2026, Punjab's Director General of Police Gaurav Yadav announced that police had 'busted' the conspiracy behind the July 8 Moga blast, arresting 17 people and recovering an IED, an electronic detonator, explosive material, and a 9mm Glock pistol with a magazine. Police say the plot's alleged mastermind, Iqbal Singh (alias Ravi Dhaliwal), directed the group from the UK, and that the accused were also planning to plant an explosive device on a train running between Ferozepur and Jammu. An FIR was registered under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (India's new criminal code), the Explosive Substances Act, the anti-terror UAPA law, and property-damage statutes.
key_points: 17 people arrested; an IED, detonator and a 9mm pistol recovered
Alleged mastermind: Iqbal Singh, alias Ravi Dhaliwal, based in the UK
Group allegedly planned to plant an IED on a Ferozepur–Jammu train
Case traces back to a grenade attack on Moga's Sadar police station on July 8

depth.background: On July 8, 2026, an explosion — suspected to be a hand grenade — went off outside the Sadar police station in Moga, Punjab, causing no injuries. It fits a pattern of recent attacks on Punjab police stations and rail infrastructure that investigators have increasingly linked to Pakistan-backed, diaspora-directed militant networks, run by handlers abroad rather than fighters based locally.

stories.title: Trump administration freezes over $1 billion in Medicaid payments to California and Minnesota
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 2026, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS, part of HHS) announced it is withholding more than $1 billion in Medicaid payments — $867.5 million from California and about $199 million from Minnesota — citing spending patterns it says raise fraud red flags. The freeze is a 'deferral,' meaning the states can still get the money if they document that the disputed claims are legitimate.
key_points: CMS held back $867.5M from California, about $199M from Minnesota over suspected fraud
California's frozen funds tied to in-home care claims growing faster than the national trend
Minnesota's hold covers 14 'high-risk' service areas needing more paperwork
Payments are deferred, not cancelled — states can submit proof to get the money released

depth.background: Medicaid is the US government's health insurance program for low-income and disabled Americans, jointly funded by federal and state governments — the feds typically cover 50-90% of costs, sent to states as reimbursements. The Trump administration has made 'fraud, waste and abuse' a signature target across federal spending, and Medicaid — a program costing over $900 billion a year — is a frequent focus because of its complexity and reliance on state-level oversight. California and Minnesota, both run by Democratic governors, have each already had money withheld earlier this year: California lost $1.3 billion since May, Minnesota $243 million since February.

stories.title: Zelensky fires Ukraine's top general after nationwide protests, names a younger successor
stories.what_happened: President Volodymyr Zelensky fired Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief of Ukraine's armed forces, following days of mass protests in Kyiv and other cities. He named Mykhailo Drapatyi, a 43-year-old general who built his career during the current war with Russia, as the new army chief.
key_points: Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi is out as commander-in-chief after days of protests
Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, takes over — seen as part of a new generation of commanders
Protesters wanted Syrskyi gone and ex-defense minister Fedorov's reforms revived
Rare case of a wartime leader bending to street pressure over military command

depth.background: Ukraine had erupted in wartime protests demanding Syrskyi's removal, with reports that Zelensky was already interviewing possible replacements. Syrskyi, who has led Ukraine's armed forces since February 2024, was blamed by critics for a rigid, Soviet-style command culture linked to costly battlefield losses. Protesters also demanded the reinstatement of former defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov, seen as a driver of military modernization.

thread.previously: Ukraine saw nationwide wartime protests demanding the removal of army chief Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi, with reports that Zelensky was already interviewing potential replacements.
thread.changed: Zelensky has now actually fired Syrskyi and named Mykhailo Drapatyi, a 43-year-old general who rose during the current war, as the new commander-in-chief.

prev_ref.title: Ukraine erupts in wartime protests demanding army chief Syrsky be sacked — Zelensky is already interviewing replacements

stories.title: Yemen's Houthis threaten to blockade Saudi shipping as mediators float a 10-day Iran ceasefire Washington is playing down
stories.what_happened: The US and Iran exchanged an 11th consecutive night of strikes into Wednesday. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Iran is 'not serious about talks.' Separately, regional mediators presented both Washington and Tehran with a proposal for a 10-day ceasefire, but US officials have downplayed the odds Trump accepts it. Adding a new front, Yemen's Houthis threatened to blockade shipping bound for Saudi Arabia. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth put the war's cost so far at $37.5 billion, with experts telling reporters the real bill is likely higher.
key_points: US and Iran traded an 11th straight night of strikes overnight
Rubio says Tehran is 'not serious about talks'
Regional mediators proposed a 10-day ceasefire; Washington is downplaying it
Houthis in Yemen now threaten to blockade Saudi Arabia's shipping too

depth.background: The US-Iran war has run in cycles since a shaky ceasefire in April 2026 and a follow-up Memorandum of Understanding (a non-binding statement of intent) signed in June that was meant to lead to a permanent end to the fighting. Instead, strikes have resumed and continued for over a week straight. Yemen's Houthi movement, an Iran-aligned armed group that controls much of Yemen, has separately used its position near the Red Sea and Bab-el-Mandeb strait to threaten shipping during past regional flare-ups.

stories.title: BCCI overhauls domestic rules: match-long bans for deliberate no-balls, new overthrow and wide-ball tweaks
stories.what_happened: The BCCI has circulated new domestic playing conditions to all state cricket associations. The headline change: a bowler found guilty of deliberately bowling front-foot no-balls or full-toss ('non-landing') deliveries — tactics sometimes used to disrupt a batter's rhythm — will now be suspended for the rest of the match, up from just the rest of that innings. Overthrows are redefined to count only when a fielder's throw was a genuine attempt to break the stumps or effect a run-out, closing a grey area with simple misfields. Umpires get a new crease marking to help judge leg-side wides, and wicketkeepers no longer have to keep their gloves behind the stumps for the entire bowler's run-up, only from the moment of delivery. A separate change requires the last scheduled over of a day's play in multi-day matches to be bowled in full even if a wicket falls mid-over.
key_points: Bowlers caught deliberately bowling no-balls or full tosses now face suspension for the rest of the match, not just the innings
Overthrows are redefined to only count when a fielder genuinely tries to run a batter out
A new marking helps umpires judge leg-side wides, and keepers no longer must stay behind the stumps during the bowler's run-up
The changes kick in from 1 August, just ahead of the Duleep Trophy

depth.background: The BCCI (Board of Control for Cricket in India) runs India's domestic cricket pyramid — tournaments like the Ranji and Duleep Trophies that develop the players who go on to play for India. 'Playing conditions' are rule tweaks a board layers on top of cricket's core Laws, which are written globally by the MCC (Marylebone Cricket Club). The BCCI's changes arrive about two months ahead of the MCC's own global law updates, which take effect worldwide on 1 October.

stories.title: Pentagon chief tells Congress the Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far, as the US death toll climbs to 18
stories.what_happened: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs, Gen. Dan Caine, testified before the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday, July 21. Hegseth said the war has cost $37.5 billion so far and used the hearing to press the case for a new supplemental funding package (roughly $95 billion is being discussed for military and other priorities). Protesters interrupted his opening remarks. This came days after the Pentagon confirmed three more US service members had died, taking the total death toll to 18, with close to 100 injured since strikes intensified this month.
key_points: Hegseth testifies to Senate Appropriations Committee, defends more war funding
War has cost $37.5 billion in its fifth month, cost tracked to justify a new budget ask
US death toll has climbed to 18 with roughly 100 troops wounded since strikes began
Protesters interrupted the hearing; senators pressed the Pentagon on its original justification

depth.background: The US and Iran have been in an active, reignited war for several months, triggered by tit-for-tat strikes across the Middle East (Iran hitting US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan; the US and Israel striking Iranian targets). Congress controls the money the Pentagon needs to keep funding a war, which is why the defense secretary has to appear and justify the bill to lawmakers — this is one of the few times Congress has had a chance to question the administration directly about how the war is going and why it's being fought.

stories.title: As a protester fights for her life, the Cockroach party sacks its own spokesperson over a burger video mocking critics
stories.what_happened: Videos surfaced showing CJP spokesperson Vijeta Dahiya absent from the march during the police crackdown, then mocking critics online by eating a burger and biscuits and joking about snacks available at the protest site. The party publicly condemned the videos as "deeply insensitive" and removed him from his post. Separately, senior BJP leader J.P. Nadda visited RML Hospital to check on those injured, including police personnel, as the female student's critical condition continued.
key_points: CJP (Cockroach Janta Party) fires spokesperson Vijeta Dahiya
Videos showed him skipping the crackdown, then eating a burger and biscuits, mocking critics
Comes as a young woman protester remains critical on a ventilator at RML Hospital
BJP national chief J.P. Nadda visits the injured at the same hospital

depth.background: The CJP-led 'Chalo Sansad' march by NEET aspirants and students demanding accountability over the exam paper leak turned violent on Monday when police used tear gas and lathi charges, injuring well over a hundred people on both sides. One student, in her early 20s, remains on ventilator support in the ICU.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the protest entered its second day at Jantar Mantar, with roughly 60 protesters and 118 police injured in Monday's clashes, a young woman critical on a ventilator at RML Hospital, and Congress leaders including Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi detained after marching on PM Modi's residence demanding the education minister's resignation.
thread.changed: Today the movement is managing an internal scandal — sacking its own spokesperson over an insensitive viral video — even as the injured student remains critical and BJP's national chief makes a hospital visit to the injured.

prev_ref.title: A day after Delhi's crackdown, India's youth-led 'Cockroach' protest regroups — Congress demands Modi and his education minister resign

stories.title: Tom Prest's 89-ball century and IPL hitter Ashutosh Sharma's overseas debut sink Yorkshire
stories.what_happened: Hampshire beat Yorkshire in their One-Day Cup group match, with Tom Prest scoring 109 off 94 balls — his third List A hundred — hitting four sixes as he and Lehmann put on a record fourth-wicket partnership for the ground. On his Hampshire debut, Indian all-rounder Ashutosh Sharma took 3 for 48 from nine overs with the ball, striking twice in the same over, as Yorkshire were bowled out for 270.
key_points: Tom Prest hits his third List A century, 109 off 94 balls
Indian all-rounder Ashutosh Sharma takes 3-48 on Hampshire debut
Yorkshire bowled out for 270 in the One-Day Cup group match
Prest and Lehmann share a ground record fourth-wicket stand

depth.background: The One-Day Cup is English domestic cricket's 50-over competition, played by the 18 first-class counties largely with squads full of fringe internationals, young prospects and short-term overseas signings looking to build List A (professional one-day) form outside the international calendar. Ashutosh Sharma is better known in India as a big-hitting IPL finisher for Delhi Capitals; county deals like this let overseas players sharpen their game and earn a pay day between IPL seasons.

stories.title: It's not just Verstappen — Piastri has his own exit clause, and he's currently below the threshold to trigger it
stories.what_happened: New reporting reveals Piastri's own McLaren contract, which runs through 2028, includes a similar performance-linked exit clause — reportedly triggered if he's outside the top five in the standings at the summer break. Piastri currently sits sixth, 15 points behind teammate Lando Norris, putting him right on the edge of that threshold. That means both Verstappen's and Piastri's contractual trigger points fall in roughly the same 72-hour window around the Hungarian GP and the start of the summer break, raising the odds of overlapping driver-market moves rather than just one.
key_points: Piastri's McLaren contract reportedly lets him leave early if he's outside the top 5 at the summer break
He's currently 6th in the standings, 15 points behind teammate Norris
Verstappen's own exit clause (top 2 required) is expected to trigger around the same time
Both clauses converge in the ~72 hours around the Hungarian GP and the start of the summer break

depth.background: F1's 'silly season' — the annual scramble over which drivers race for which teams next year — has centred on Max Verstappen's Red Bull exit clause, reportedly triggered if he's outside the top two in the standings when the summer break begins after the Hungarian Grand Prix (July 24-26). McLaren has publicly downplayed any rift, with Oscar Piastri himself dismissing rumours of a Verstappen swap just days ago, saying he was 'very happy' at McLaren.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported that Verstappen's realistic destinations had collapsed to almost nothing — Mercedes and Ferrari have their seats locked up, and pundits expected him to stay at Red Bull because McLaren was seen as 'satisfied with Piastri and Norris.'
thread.changed: New reporting shows Piastri himself has a matching exit clause tied to being in the top five, and he's currently outside it — meaning the McLaren seat pundits assumed was locked up may not be as settled as it looked.

prev_ref.title: Verstappen's escape routes from Red Bull are closing — pundits now expect him to stay put for 2027

stories.title: Supreme Court orders a fresh trial in the 1996 Rajasthan bus bombing — 29 years after 14 people were killed
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 2026, a three-judge Supreme Court bench (Justices Vikram Nath, Sanjay Karol and Sandeep Mehta) set aside both Hameed's conviction and the High Court's 2019 order upholding it, ruling that he had been denied effective legal representation during the original trial. The Court ordered a genuinely fresh trial — not merely a review of the existing evidence — at a new fast-track special court in Jaipur, to be completed within a year, with Hameed guaranteed a lead lawyer of at least 10 years' experience. It also upheld the acquittals of three co-accused, including Pappu alias Salim, who remains on parole.
key_points: SC set aside Abdul Hameed's death sentence and ordered a complete retrial
Reason: he was denied effective, competent legal representation at trial
Co-accused Pappu alias Salim stays acquitted; two other acquittals also upheld
A new fast-track special court in Jaipur must finish the retrial within a year

depth.background: On May 22, 1996, an RDX bomb tore through a Rajasthan Roadways bus near Samleti village on the Agra–Bikaner route, killing 14 people and injuring 37 — a day after a similar bombing in Delhi's Lajpat Nagar market killed 13. Investigators blamed a terror conspiracy tied to the Jammu & Kashmir Islamic Front and Harkat-ul-Ansar militant groups, allegedly timed to disrupt Jammu & Kashmir's 1996 state elections. Abdul Hameed was convicted only in 2014 — 18 years after the blast — and sentenced to death; Rajasthan's High Court upheld that conviction in 2019.

stories.title: Trump readies fresh tariffs on dozens of countries as his 10% global tariff truce expires this Friday
stories.what_happened: On July 21, the Financial Times reported — per Reuters, which said it could not independently verify the FT's account — that Trump is preparing to announce fresh tariffs on dozens of countries, potentially as soon as this week. The trigger is that his existing 10% global tariff is due to expire this Friday, July 24. The FT says the most likely new duties would start at around that same 10% rate, but the administration is also pursuing separate trade investigations that could give it legal grounds to impose higher tariffs on specific countries later.
key_points: FT reports Trump could unveil new tariffs on dozens of countries as soon as this week
His current across-the-board 10% global tariff expires Friday, July 24
New duties are expected to start around that same 10% level
Separate trade investigations could let the administration push some rates higher later

depth.background: Since returning to office, Trump has used a temporary, blanket tariff — a flat import tax applied to goods from almost every country — as a baseline trade tool, alongside country-specific deals and higher 'reciprocal' tariffs matched to what other nations charge US exporters (like the 50% tariff just placed on Canada over autos, alcohol and cheese). These blanket tariffs have been repeatedly set to expire and then renewed or replaced, becoming a recurring deadline that markets and trading partners now watch closely.

stories.title: Ben Stokes marks his return from Test retirement with a stunning comeback century for Durham
stories.what_happened: Playing for Durham against Derbyshire in the One-Day Cup at Derby on 21 July, Stokes made his first appearance in competitive cricket since retiring from Tests. Despite carrying a leg injury, he produced a matchwinning century that carried Durham to victory, drawing a crowd of around 1,300 to a domestic fixture that would normally attract far less attention.
key_points: First competitive innings since retiring from Test cricket
Battled a leg injury to score a matchwinning hundred
Powered Durham to victory over Derbyshire in the One-Day Cup
His innings overshadowed The Hundred's opening night the same evening

depth.background: Ben Stokes, England's Test captain and one of the sport's biggest names since his match-winning heroics in the 2019 World Cup final, recently stepped away from Test cricket. Durham is his boyhood county, and rather than featuring in this year's Hundred (English cricket's flagship T20 franchise league), he chose to return to the game through the more traditional 50-over County format — a lower-key stage for such a high-profile comeback.

stories.title: Kandy Royals thump Dambulla Sixers by 43 runs in the Lanka Premier League, led by Hasaranga's side bouncing back from an opening loss
stories.what_happened: Kandy Royals scored 194 for 6 in their 20 overs and bowled Dambulla Sixers out for 151 for 8 in reply, winning the rematch by 43 runs at the Rangiri Dambulla International Stadium.
key_points: Kandy Royals posted 194/6, Dambulla Sixers fell short at 151/8
6th match of LPL 2026, played at Rangiri Dambulla International Stadium
Kandy, captained by Wanindu Hasaranga, avenge their tournament-opening loss to the same side
Dambulla, captained by Dinesh Chandimal, drop to a losing head-to-head record this LPL

depth.background: The Lanka Premier League (LPL) is Sri Lanka's Twenty20 franchise cricket tournament, now in its 2026 edition. Kandy Royals and Dambulla Sixers had already met once this tournament, on July 18, when Dambulla won by 18 runs behind an unbeaten 87 from Reeza Hendricks.

stories.title: Ruturaj Gaikwad named West Zone captain for the Duleep Trophy as Prithvi Shaw earns a recall
stories.what_happened: West Zone named India ODI vice-captain Ruturaj Gaikwad as captain for the 2026-27 Duleep Trophy, with all-rounder Shams Mulani as his deputy. Prithvi Shaw earned a recall to the squad after a strong debut season for Maharashtra, where he scored 537 runs in 11 innings at an average of 48.81, including a 156-ball 222 — the third-fastest century in Ranji Trophy history. The 15-man squad includes six Mumbai players, among them all-rounder Shardul Thakur and batter Sarfaraz Khan. The tournament runs from August 23 to September 10.
key_points: Gaikwad leads West Zone in the 2026-27 Duleep Trophy, India's domestic season opener
Shams Mulani is vice-captain
Prithvi Shaw is back after 537 runs at 48.81 for Maharashtra last season, including a 156-ball 222
The six-team tournament runs August 23 to September 10

depth.background: The Duleep Trophy is India's marquee first-class tournament, contested between zonal teams, and traditionally opens the domestic cricket season — a proving ground selectors watch closely for national-team contention. Prithvi Shaw, once a rising India Test opener, fell out of favour after being dropped by Mumbai and had to rebuild his case at a new state side, Maharashtra.

stories.title: A judge approves Anthropic's $1.5 billion payout to authors — the biggest copyright settlement ever, over AI trained on pirated books
stories.what_happened: On Monday, US District Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin gave final approval to a $1.5 billion class-action settlement, calling it 'meaningful relief' for writers. Authors will receive roughly $3,000 for each affected book, across more than 482,000 works — about 91% of which have already been claimed. Anthropic must also destroy the pirated copies it used. Plaintiff lawyers called it 'the largest known copyright recovery in history.'
key_points: Largest known copyright recovery in history
About $3,000 per book to 500,000+ authors
Anthropic must destroy the pirated book copies
Landmark test of AI vs copyright law

depth.background: Anthropic is the San Francisco AI company behind the Claude chatbot. To build such a system, AI firms feed it enormous amounts of text so it learns to write and reason. A group of authors sued Anthropic in 2024, saying it had trained Claude on their books — obtained from pirate websites that host stolen copies for free. Last year the judge drew a crucial line: training AI on books you legally own can be 'fair use' (legal), but downloading millions of pirated copies to do it is not. That ruling pushed Anthropic to settle rather than risk a far larger jury verdict.

stories.title: India's monsoon is stuck in two moods at once: a below-normal July overall, but extreme flooding risk in the west right now
stories.what_happened: The IMD's July forecast points to below-normal rainfall for most of the country this month, building on a roughly 40% nationwide rainfall deficit already recorded in June, alongside above-normal daytime and nighttime temperatures. At the same time, the IMD's short-range alert (issued July 21) warns of 'extremely heavy' rainfall — meaning more than 204.4mm in 24 hours — at isolated spots in Gujarat on both July 21 and 22, and in Madhya Maharashtra on July 22. Authorities have told fishermen to stay off the sea near Gujarat and north Maharashtra through July 24 as a precaution. The northwest, northeast, and parts of east-central India are forecast to buck the dry trend with normal-to-above-normal rain.
key_points: IMD forecasts below-normal rainfall for July nationally, after a ~40% deficit in June
But Gujarat and Madhya Maharashtra are under alert for 'extremely heavy' rain July 21–22
Fishermen told to stay off the sea near Gujarat and north Maharashtra until July 24
Northwest, northeast and east-central India may still see normal-to-above-normal rain

depth.background: India's June-to-September monsoon delivers about 70% of the country's annual rainfall and is the backbone of its farm economy — a weak or erratic monsoon can hit crop yields, rural incomes, and food inflation all at once. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) issues both a broad seasonal outlook and short-range alerts for specific states; this year the two pictures have diverged sharply, with the big-picture forecast pointing to a dry July even as specific western states face sudden extreme-rain warnings.

stories.title: The Hundred is back: defending champions Sunrisers Leeds thrash MI London to open the 2026 season
stories.what_happened: The 2026 edition opened on Tuesday, 21 July at the Kennington Oval in London with the women's match. MI London posted 107 for 8, and Sunrisers Leeds chased it down comfortably, reaching 109 for 3 with 31 balls to spare to win by seven wickets. Australia's Phoebe Litchfield top-scored with a brisk 43 from 26 balls and was named player of the match; the champions' side also included India all-rounder Deepti Sharma. Sunrisers were, however, left sweating on the fitness of England seamer Kate Cross.
key_points: New season of England's 100-ball league begins at The Oval
Sunrisers Leeds women win by 7 wickets, chasing 108 with ease
Australia's Phoebe Litchfield stars with 43 off 26 balls
India's Deepti Sharma features in the champions' line-up

depth.background: The Hundred is a short, fast cricket tournament run by the England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) since 2021. Each innings is exactly 100 balls (instead of the usual 120 in a T20 match), and eight city-based teams play parallel men's and women's competitions across mid-summer. It was designed to be simple, quick and family-friendly to pull in new fans — and it now runs alongside a major commercial shake-up, with the ECB having sold stakes in the eight franchises to private investors, several of them linked to owners of India's IPL teams. Sunrisers Leeds' women went in as the reigning 2025 champions.

stories.title: McLaren finally brings its big upgrade — and its unusual 'Macarena' rear wing — to this weekend's Hungarian GP
stories.what_happened: McLaren confirmed it will unleash a large upgrade package at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix (24–26 July) at the Hungaroring near Budapest, which it has long marked as its main chance to claw back performance before F1's summer break. As part of that push, it will finally trial an experimental rear wing — nicknamed the 'Macarena' because it changes shape as the car moves — during Friday's practice sessions. The team had planned to test the wing earlier, at the Austrian GP, but decided it needed more development first.
key_points: McLaren's 2026 car has been off the pace all year
A 'significant' upgrade package lands at the Hungarian GP
It will trial an upside-down, shape-shifting 'Macarena' rear wing in practice
Team admits its car was 2–3 months behind on aerodynamics

depth.background: McLaren won both Formula 1 world titles in 2024 and 2025, but the sport tore up its rulebook for 2026 — all-new cars with a 50/50 split between engine and electric power. McLaren's new car, the MCL40, started the season badly: the team has openly admitted its design was two to three months behind where it needed to be on aerodynamics, the science of shaping a car so the air flowing over it presses it onto the track for grip. That has left drivers Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri fighting in the midfield instead of for wins.

stories.title: Google ships three new Gemini models but its flagship Gemini 3.5 Pro misses its release target again
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, Google DeepMind released three new models: Gemini 3.6 Flash, a faster and cheaper general-purpose model that cuts token usage (roughly, the units of text an AI reads and writes, which is how these models are priced) by up to 17% versus the previous Flash model; Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite, an even cheaper, higher-speed option; and Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber, a version fine-tuned specifically to hunt for and patch cybersecurity vulnerabilities, which Google is only giving to governments and 'trusted partners' in a limited pilot rather than releasing broadly. Notably absent again was Gemini 3.5 Pro, the flagship model Google has repeatedly said is coming but has not yet shipped. Google said it's now begun pre-training Gemini 4, the generation after that.
key_points: Google released Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and a security-tuned 3.5 Flash Cyber model
3.6 Flash is cheaper and uses up to 17% fewer tokens than its predecessor
The Cyber model, built to find and fix hacking vulnerabilities, is limited to governments and trusted partners for now
The bigger, more capable Gemini 3.5 Pro is still not out — Google has now missed its own timeline for it more than once

depth.background: Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic are locked in a race to keep releasing more capable AI models faster than each other, since falling behind risks losing developers and paying customers to a rival. Google's Gemini family is split into tiers: cheap, fast 'Flash' models for everyday tasks, and a slower, more powerful 'Pro' model meant to be its best all-round system. Companies build on top of these models via APIs (a way for outside software to plug into them), so a cheaper, faster Flash model can lower costs across thousands of products at once.

stories.title: Union ministers visit Sonam Wangchuk in ICU as his hunger strike enters its 24th day
stories.what_happened: Wangchuk was shifted from Delhi's government-run Safdarjung Hospital to the private Medanta Hospital in Gurugram on a Delhi High Court order, arriving in the ICU around 7:30 pm. Union Ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited him there to ask after his health. In a handwritten note, Wangchuk said he would continue his fast until protesting student leaders are permitted to meet MPs at Parliament House — sharpening his demand beyond just the minister's resignation.
key_points: Wangchuk is on day 24 of his hunger strike, now in Medanta's ICU
BJP leaders JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited him in hospital
He says he'll keep fasting until student leaders can meet MPs
Move follows a Delhi High Court order shifting him from Safdarjung

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a Ladakh-based education reformer and climate activist, well known for pushing India's government on Himalayan glacier protection and for inspiring the 'rocket boys' school-reform movement. He began an indefinite hunger strike on June 28 over the NEET-UG (India's national medical entrance exam) paper-leak scandal, becoming the emotional centerpiece of the wider 'Cockroach' youth protest movement demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation.

thread.previously: The Delhi High Court had just ordered Wangchuk moved from government-run Safdarjung Hospital to the private Medanta Hospital in Gurugram for closer monitoring, with doctors reporting pancytopenia (a drop in red cells, white cells and platelets) but stable vitals.
thread.changed: Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited Wangchuk in Medanta's ICU-8 to personally enquire about his health — a direct government-level engagement that goes beyond court-ordered medical care. Wangchuk, now on day 24 of his fast, wrote a handwritten note saying he will keep fasting until protesting student leaders are allowed to meet Members of Parliament at Parliament House — a new, specific demand.

prev_ref.title: Sonam Wangchuk shifted to a private Gurugram hospital after Delhi High Court order, as his NEET hunger strike enters its fourth week

stories.title: Hamilton targets a record-extending 9th Hungary win to erase a rough Spa weekend
stories.what_happened: Ahead of this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, Hamilton said he is optimistic about Ferrari's chances at the Hungaroring, a track whose tight, twisting layout suits cars that corner well — a strength he believes plays to Ferrari's current car. He said he needs to 'come correct' after the disappointing Spa result.
key_points: Hamilton already holds the record for most wins at the Hungaroring (8) but hasn't won there since 2020
He says Ferrari's car 'will be strong' at Hungary's tight, high-downforce track after a difficult Belgian GP
Comes after his car struck a Ferrari mechanic in the pit lane at Spa, which saw the team fined
Sets up a chance to close in on championship leader Antonelli before the summer break

depth.background: Hamilton moved to Ferrari for the 2026 season in one of F1's biggest-ever driver moves, chasing the championship that eluded him at Mercedes. The switch has produced flashes of pace but also frustration, and last week's Belgian Grand Prix weekend was compromised — including a pit-lane incident where his car made contact with a mechanic.

stories.title: Zelensky fires army chief Syrsky after days of protests, names Drapatyi as Ukraine's new top general
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, President Volodymyr Zelensky fired Syrsky as commander-in-chief and named Mykhailo Drapatyi, previously commander of Ukraine's ground forces, as the new army chief. The move follows the five-day wave of protests demanding Syrsky's removal. Zelensky thanked Syrsky publicly for his role defending Kyiv and for operations around Kharkiv and Kursk, while Drapatyi said in a statement that leading Ukraine's army during the war is a matter of 'absolute responsibility.'
key_points: Syrsky is out as commander-in-chief after 5 days of Kyiv protests
Mykhailo Drapatyi, a ground-forces commander, named as replacement
Protesters wanted reform-minded ex-minister Fedorov's ally installed
Zelensky publicly thanked Syrsky for defending Kyiv and Kharkiv

depth.background: Ukraine has been fighting Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022. Oleksandr Syrsky became commander-in-chief (the single general who runs the whole war effort) in 2024 after Zelensky removed the popular Valery Zaluzhny. Anger at Syrsky boiled over this month when a reshuffle pushed out Mykhailo Fedorov, a 35-year-old former tech minister credited with building Ukraine's drone programme — Fedorov publicly accused Syrsky of blocking the tech-first reforms Ukraine needs. Thousands then rallied for days outside the presidential office in Kyiv demanding Syrsky be sacked by Friday.

thread.previously: For five straight days, thousands protested outside Zelensky's office in Kyiv demanding Syrsky be dismissed by Friday, after a reshuffle pushed out reform-minded defence official Mykhailo Fedorov; Zelensky said he was reviewing replacement candidates.
thread.changed: Zelensky has now actually fired Syrsky — ahead of the protesters' Friday deadline — and named Mykhailo Drapatyi, the ground-forces commander, as Ukraine's new commander-in-chief.

prev_ref.title: Ukraine erupts in wartime protests demanding army chief Syrsky be sacked — Zelensky is already interviewing replacements

stories.title: F1's rookie rule kicks in at the Hungarian GP — Piastri, Bearman and Colapinto all sit out first practice
stories.what_happened: At this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, three regular race drivers are stepping aside for Friday's first practice session to satisfy this rule. Leonardo Fornaroli, the reigning Formula 2 champion and McLaren's protégé, again takes over from Oscar Piastri. Toyota-backed Ryo Hirakawa replaces Oliver Bearman at Haas, and Paul Aron — on his third outing this year — takes Franco Colapinto's Alpine seat.
key_points: McLaren's Oscar Piastri, Haas's Oliver Bearman and Alpine's Franco Colapinto all miss FP1 at the Hungaroring
Their seats go to juniors: Leonardo Fornaroli (McLaren), Ryo Hirakawa (Haas) and Paul Aron (Alpine)
