F1 rules force every team to hand two FP1 sessions per car per year to a rookie
It's Fornaroli's second FP1 outing of 2026, Aron's third — both are proving themselves for a future seat

depth.background: F1 cars are so expensive and complex that teams almost never let an unproven driver near one in an actual race. So the sport's rulebook forces the issue: every team must give a 'rookie' — someone who has driven in two or fewer F1 races — a run in first practice (FP1) at least twice per car, every season. It's how young drivers from a team's junior academy get real seat time in a current F1 car instead of just simulators.

stories.title: Real-estate firm Anant Raj splits off its data-centre business into a new company, Ashok Cloud
stories.what_happened: Anant Raj's board approved a Composite Scheme of Arrangement on July 21 to carve its data-centre and cloud services operations into a newly created company, Ashok Cloud Private Limited. Existing Anant Raj shareholders will receive one fully paid-up Ashok Cloud share for every Anant Raj share they hold. Anant Raj will keep its real estate and infrastructure business (residential, commercial, hospitality); Ashok Cloud will house data centres, co-location, sovereign cloud, AI-ready infrastructure and disaster-recovery services. The scheme still needs sign-off from the NCLT (India's company-law tribunal), SEBI, stock exchanges, shareholders and creditors.
key_points: Anant Raj's board approves a demerger, effective via a court-approved scheme
Data-centre and cloud unit spun into new entity Ashok Cloud Pvt Ltd
Shareholders get 1 Ashok Cloud share for every Anant Raj share held
Move separates real estate from India's booming AI/data-centre infrastructure bet

depth.background: Anant Raj is an Indian real-estate developer that, like several property firms, expanded into data centres — the large, power-hungry warehouses of servers that run cloud computing and AI — because building and land expertise transfers well to that business. Demergers (splitting a company in two) are common in India when a conglomerate wants each business valued on its own terms by investors, rather than one being dragged down or masked by the other.

stories.title: Lebanon's president meets Trump at the White House as Israel starts its first troop pullback from the south
stories.what_happened: President Aoun met Trump face-to-face for the first time on July 21, 2026, presenting a plan for disarming Hezbollah and securing Israel's full withdrawal from southern Lebanon. The meeting came a day after the US State Department confirmed Israel had begun withdrawing from several villages it had occupied in south Lebanon — the first concrete step under the June 'pilot zones' framework.
key_points: First Lebanese president to visit the White House since 2009
Aoun brought a plan to disarm Hezbollah and secure a full Israeli withdrawal
Israel has begun pulling out of some occupied villages in south Lebanon, per the State Department
Trump pledged to 'help Lebanon a lot' and said he'd be open to speaking with Hezbollah

depth.background: Lebanon and the Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah fought a war with Israel that escalated after October 2023; Israel has occupied parts of southern Lebanon since. Joseph Aoun, formerly commander of Lebanon's US-backed army, was elected president last year partly on a promise to restore state control over the country's south, where Hezbollah has long operated as a state-within-a-state. On June 26, Lebanon and Israel announced a 'framework agreement' for a phased Israeli withdrawal tied to Hezbollah's disarmament.

stories.title: France becomes the first EU country to ban social media for under-15s
stories.what_happened: French lawmakers in both the National Assembly and Senate adopted a bill this week banning social media access for children under 15, with Macron confirming the passage in a social media post. The law also bars mobile phone use in schools. New accounts for under-15s are blocked starting in September, with a full wipe of existing underage accounts coming in January once enforcement tools are ready.
key_points: Both houses of France's parliament passed the bill Tuesday
Kids under 15 can't open new accounts from September; existing accounts wiped by January
Phones also banned in French middle and high schools
France's Constitutional Council must still clear it before it's final

depth.background: Governments worldwide have grown increasingly worried about social media's effect on children's mental health, with Australia already moving to restrict under-16 access. President Macron had signaled this ban was coming after months of debate over screen time, bullying, and algorithm-driven content aimed at minors.

stories.title: French Parliament passes a landmark ban on social media for under-15s — Macron's flagship reform becomes law
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, France's National Assembly passed the bill by a 279-81 vote and the Senate also approved it, making France the first country in the European Union to enact a blanket ban on social media for children under 15. The law blocks new accounts from September 1, 2026, and cuts off access for existing under-15 accounts from January 2027. It also bans mobile phone use in French high schools.
key_points: National Assembly passes the bill 279 votes to 81, Senate also approves it
Blocks new social media accounts for under-15s from September 1, 2026
Existing under-15 accounts must be closed by January 2027
Also bans mobile phones in French high schools

depth.background: France's push to restrict children's social media use has been building for months, championed by President Macron as a flagship policy of his final term and driven by growing concern about the mental-health and safety impact of algorithm-driven apps on kids. Yesterday we reported the two houses of France's parliament had just reached a compromise on the bill's wording, clearing the way for a final vote.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported France's two houses of parliament had just reached a compromise on the bill's exact wording, clearing the way for a final adoption vote expected Tuesday which lawmakers believed would pass.
thread.changed: The vote happened: the National Assembly passed it 279-81 and the Senate followed, making the ban law — the first in the EU — with new accounts blocked from September 1 and existing ones cut off from January 2027.

prev_ref.title: France is on the verge of becoming the first country in Europe to ban children under 15 from social media

stories.title: Sikkim tunnel death toll climbs to 20 as gas and flooding hamper the hunt for 5 still trapped
stories.what_happened: Rescue teams have now recovered 20 bodies, up from the initial count of 7, with 5 workers still unaccounted for more than a day after the collapse. The operation has been slowed by leftover gas inside the tunnel — some rescuers have felt dizzy or briefly lost consciousness while trying to reach trapped workers — and by heavy monsoon rain sending water pooling into the tunnel. Sikkim's Chief Minister Prem Singh Tamang announced ₹4 lakh (about $4,800) in compensation for each deceased worker's family and ₹50,000 for the injured; the Prime Minister separately announced a further ₹2 lakh per family from the central government.
key_points: Death toll up from 7 to 20 as recovery work continues
5 workers still trapped; rescuers battling gas and water inside
Some rescue personnel felt dizzy or lost consciousness from gas
Centre and Sikkim government announce compensation for families

depth.background: A methane gas explosion tore through an under-construction tunnel at NHPC's Teesta Stage-VI hydropower project in Sikkim's Namchi district, triggering a collapse that trapped workers roughly 1.5 km inside. NHPC is India's state-owned hydropower company; tunnelling in the Himalayas is especially risky because the rock is unstable and pockets of natural gas can build up underground, as this project has shown.

thread.previously: A methane explosion collapsed the NHPC Teesta Stage-VI tunnel in Namchi district, killing at least 7 with several workers still trapped; the PM and Home Minister had spoken with Sikkim's Chief Minister as rescue efforts began.
thread.changed: The confirmed death toll has nearly tripled to 20, with 5 workers still trapped; rescue crews are now battling residual gas and monsoon flooding inside the tunnel, and both the state and central governments have announced compensation for victims' families.

prev_ref.title: A methane blast collapses a hydro-project tunnel in Sikkim — at least 7 killed, many feared trapped

stories.title: The Pentagon says the Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far — and wants $67 billion more from Congress
stories.what_happened: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee that the war against Iran has cost $37.5 billion so far — a new, higher estimate. He and Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Dan Caine asked Congress to approve a $67 billion supplemental: $46 billion for munitions and expanding production lines, and $21 billion for military pay, fuel, and replacing equipment.
key_points: Defense Secretary Hegseth gave the new estimate at a Senate hearing
The administration wants $67B more: $46B for weapons/munitions, $21B for troops/fuel/equipment
Top Senate Democrat warns of 'another forever war'
Comes as US strikes on Iran continue for a 10th straight night

depth.background: The US has been bombing Iran for over a week now, part of a widening Gulf conflict that also includes Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping and Israel-Hamas tensions. Wars are expensive to run — munitions, fuel, troop pay, and replacing damaged equipment all draw from the Pentagon's budget, which needs Congress to approve extra 'supplemental' funding when a conflict runs longer or hotter than planned.

stories.title: F1's next generation duels it out: Tsolov stretches his F2 points lead as the junior title fight tightens at Spa
stories.what_happened: After the Spa-Francorchamps round of the 2026 F2 season, Red Bull's Nikola Tsolov leads the drivers' standings with 161 points, built on six wins this year including a run of three in a row — a record for the series, previously only matched in its GP2-era predecessor. Alpine Academy's Gabriele Mini sits second on 134, with Ferrari Driver Academy's Rafael Camara third (125) and Alpine's Alex Dunne fourth (108).
key_points: Red Bull junior Nikola Tsolov leads Formula 2 on 161 points, 27 clear of second place
He's won six races this season, including three in a row — a Formula 2 record
Alpine's Gabriele Mini (134), Ferrari junior Rafael Camara (125) and Alpine's Alex Dunne (108) chase him
Eight of 14 rounds are done — the standings were set after this weekend's Spa-Francorchamps round

depth.background: Formula 2 is the main feeder championship directly below F1 — nearly every current F1 driver raced here first. Most F2 drivers are funded by an F1 team's junior 'academy' program (Red Bull, Ferrari, Alpine and others all run one), which pays for their seat and grooms them as a future F1 prospect. A dominant F2 season is usually the strongest evidence a young driver can present when lobbying for a real F1 seat.

stories.title: England's Jacob Bethell ruled out of The Hundred with a knee injury — and his Pakistan Test place is now in doubt
stories.what_happened: After an MRI scan on Monday, the England and Wales Cricket Board confirmed on Tuesday, 21 July that Bethell has been ruled out of the entire 2026 Hundred with a right-knee injury, with no recovery timeline given yet. That ends his planned stint captaining Birmingham Phoenix — South Africa's Donovan Ferreira has been named to lead the side instead for their opening game on Friday. More significantly, it casts doubt over his availability for England's Test series in Pakistan, where the first Test at Headingley is scheduled to start on 19 August.
key_points: Rising all-rounder hurt his right knee during the India ODI series
MRI on Monday ruled him out of the whole Hundred season
He loses the chance to captain Birmingham Phoenix
Availability for August's Test series in Pakistan now uncertain

depth.background: Jacob Bethell is one of England's most talked-about young all-rounders — a left-hand batter and left-arm spinner tipped as a future leader, who had been handed the captaincy of Birmingham Phoenix for this year's Hundred. He picked up the knee problem during England's recently concluded one-day international (ODI) series against India, which England won 2-1.

stories.title: Sonam Wangchuk shifted to a private Gurugram hospital after Delhi High Court order, as his NEET hunger strike enters its fourth week
stories.what_happened: The Delhi High Court's division bench overturned an earlier single-judge order and allowed Wangchuk to be shifted from the government-run Safdarjung Hospital to the private Medanta Hospital in Gurugram. Safdarjung discharged him around 6:40 PM local time with 'stable' vital signs, and he arrived at Medanta roughly an hour later, where doctors say pancytopenia — a drop in red cells, white cells and platelets — persists, alongside a slightly low blood potassium level.
key_points: Educationist Sonam Wangchuk moved from Safdarjung Hospital to Medanta, Gurugram
Delhi High Court ordered the transfer for 'continuous medical monitoring'
He has been on hunger strike since June 28 over the NEET-UG paper leak
Doctors say his vitals are stable but pancytopenia (a blood-cell deficiency) persists

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a well-known education reformer and climate activist from Ladakh, previously known for pushing India's government on Himalayan glacier protection and reforming rote-learning in schools. He began an indefinite hunger strike on June 28 over the NEET-UG medical entrance exam paper leak, part of the broader protest wave now gripping Delhi. His wife, Dr Gitanjali Angmo, went to court after a lower judge initially refused to let him move to a hospital of his choosing.

stories.title: Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has nearly ground to a halt as the US-Iran war grinds on
stories.what_happened: Shipping-data firm Lloyd's List Intelligence reports that no large vessel crossed the strait via the US-coordinated 'safe lane' hugging the Omani coast while broadcasting its location for several days this week. Combined traffic across both shipping corridors fell to just 9 outbound and 5 inbound vessels in a single 24-hour window — versus more than 100 ships a day before the war. Container giants Maersk, CMA CGM and Hapag-Lloyd have rerouted Middle East-Europe and Middle East-US services around Africa's Cape of Good Hope, adding roughly 10-14 days and an estimated $40-50 million a week in extra costs across their fleets, while war-risk insurers pull back or sharply raise the price of covering ships that still attempt the strait.
key_points: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — the only sea route out of the Persian Gulf — has collapsed to a handful of ships a day, down from 100+
The IEA calls it the largest oil-supply disruption in the history of the global oil market
Major shipping lines are now sailing around Africa instead — adding about two weeks and tens of millions of dollars a week in cost
Brent crude is near $92 a barrel and climbing on fears it could push back above $100

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman that Gulf oil producers — Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, and Iran itself — must use to ship crude to the rest of the world; there is no easy substitute sea route. Since a US/Israeli air campaign against Iran began earlier this year, the US has kept a naval blockade posture in the Gulf and Iran has periodically threatened or attacked shipping near the strait, on top of the direct US-Iran strikes already underway.

stories.title: The White House's 10% global tariff truce expires Friday — and it already has a replacement ready: 12.5% duties on 46 countries over forced labor
stories.what_happened: The US Trade Representative's office completed parallel Section 301 investigations into 86 countries' enforcement of forced-labor import bans. USTR now recommends 12.5% tariffs on 46 countries found to have failed to block forced-labor-made goods, and a lighter 10% tariff on roughly 40 countries with partial or agreed compliance systems. Trade adviser Jamieson Greer confirmed the administration is set to activate the new regime on or around July 25 — the day after the old Section 122 tariff lapses — so there's no tariff-free gap.
key_points: Section 122's temporary 10% tariff on almost every country expires this Friday, July 24
USTR's replacement is ready: 12.5% duties on 46 countries, citing forced-labor import enforcement failures
China, Vietnam, India, Thailand, Japan and South Korea are among the 46 named
A softer 10% tier applies to ~40 other countries that have adopted forced-labor import bans

depth.background: Trump has been running a 10% 'emergency' tariff on most US imports under Section 122, a fast-track law that lets a president act without Congress or a formal investigation — but only for up to 150 days, and it's already survived one Supreme Court challenge to the president's authority to use it this way. Section 301 is the slower alternative: it requires a real investigation into a country's trade practices, but the tariffs it produces are much harder to challenge in court once imposed.

thread.previously: Yesterday's story reported Trump was preparing fresh tariffs on dozens of countries as the existing 10% global tariff truce headed toward its Friday, July 24 expiry, with no detail yet on the replacement mechanism.
thread.changed: The replacement is now confirmed: USTR will shift the legal basis from Section 122 to Section 301, setting a 12.5% rate on 46 countries (including China, Vietnam, India, Thailand, Japan and South Korea) that failed forced-labor import enforcement, versus 10% for ~40 more-compliant countries — timed to activate right as the old tariff lapses.

prev_ref.title: Trump readies fresh tariffs on dozens of countries as his 10% global tariff truce expires this Friday

stories.title: Trump threatens to hit Iran's underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' site as Tehran strikes three more Gulf allies
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, Trump said the US military would soon strike Pickaxe Mountain 'very heavily,' calling out the site by name for the first time as a target. On the same day, Iran expanded its retaliation beyond direct US forces, striking Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan — three US-allied Gulf states that all host American military bases. It marked the 10th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, with no sign of de-escalation.
key_points: Trump says the US will strike Iran's Pickaxe Mountain site 'pretty soon and very heavily'
Pickaxe Mountain is a fortified tunnel complex about 2km from the Natanz nuclear site
Iran struck US-allied Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan on Tuesday — all host American bases
It's the 10th straight night of US strikes on Iran

depth.background: The US and Iran have been trading direct strikes for over a week after the conflict over the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway carrying roughly a fifth of the world's oil — reignited. Pickaxe Mountain (Kuh-e Kolang Gaz La) is a fortified underground tunnel complex Iran declared to the UN's nuclear watchdog in 2020 as a future site to assemble centrifuges, the machines that enrich uranium into nuclear fuel.

stories.title: Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra detained by Delhi Police after marching on PM Modi's residence over the NEET crackdown
stories.what_happened: Leader of the Opposition Rahul Gandhi, his sister Priyanka Gandhi Vadra, Samajwadi Party leader Akhilesh Yadav and other Congress workers staged a sit-in protest march toward Prime Minister Narendra Modi's residence, demanding accountability for the NEET-UG paper leak and the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. Delhi Police moved in around 6:40 PM local time and detained the leaders and several workers as they dispersed the demonstration.
key_points: Congress leaders marched from Rajaji Marg toward PM's Lok Kalyan Marg residence
Police broke up the sit-in protest around 6:40 PM, detaining Rahul, Priyanka and Akhilesh Yadav
Protest demanded Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign over the NEET-UG paper leak
Followed police action a day earlier against students protesting the same issue

depth.background: India has been gripped for weeks by anger over a leaked NEET-UG exam — the national entrance test that decides admission to medical colleges — with student-led protests (nicknamed the 'Cockroach' movement) accusing the government of covering up the leak and mishandling the fallout. A police crackdown on student protesters the previous day, which reportedly injured some of them, escalated the standoff and drew the opposition Congress party directly into the streets.

thread.previously: Earlier today's feed reported that a day after Delhi's crackdown, India's youth-led 'Cockroach' protest regrouped, with Congress demanding Modi and his education minister resign.
thread.changed: The opposition's demand turned into direct action: Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra personally marched on the PM's residence and were detained by police, a sharp escalation beyond students regrouping.

stories.title: Alpine's Colapinto hands his car to reserve driver Paul Aron for Hungarian GP practice — it's a rule, not a benching
stories.what_happened: Alpine announced that Paul Aron will drive in place of Franco Colapinto during first practice (FP1) at the Hungarian Grand Prix, run on Friday July 24. The team says this satisfies the mandatory rookie-outing rule and has nothing to do with Colapinto's on-track form. Colapinto returns for second practice (FP2), also on Friday, and drives the rest of the weekend as normal.
key_points: Alpine confirms Paul Aron will drive Colapinto's car in FP1 at the Hungarian GP
F1 rules force every team to give a rookie/reserve driver track time twice a season
Not a performance penalty — Colapinto returns for FP2 and the rest of the weekend
Comes as McLaren separately benches Piastri for FP1 with Fornaroli in his seat

depth.background: Since 2025, Formula 1 has required every full-time driver to give up their car to a rookie or less-experienced driver for two practice sessions across the season — a rule designed to give young talent real seat time in a current F1 car, since testing opportunities are otherwise scarce. Franco Colapinto is Alpine's race driver; Paul Aron is the team's reserve/development driver, an Estonian who has been racing in F2/F3 feeder categories.

stories.title: FDA prepares to vote on legalizing 7 'gray market' wellness peptides — after stacking the review panel with people who sell them
stories.what_happened: Ahead of this week's advisory vote, reporting from the Washington Post, CNN, PBS NewsHour and others revealed the FDA assembled a review panel unusually stocked with doctors and pharmacists who prescribe, produce or promote peptides — a departure from past panels made up mainly of academic researchers. FDA staff scientists had already released briefing documents in June recommending against approving any of the seven peptides under review.
key_points: FDA's Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee votes Thursday-Friday (July 23-24) on 7 unproven peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500
FDA's own career scientists already recommended AGAINST all seven, citing no solid human safety or efficacy data
At least 3 of the panel's members have financial ties to the peptide industry — two currently sell the products themselves
HHS Secretary RFK Jr. has publicly pushed to widen access to these substances, popular with fitness influencers

depth.background: Peptides are short chains of amino acids that some wellness influencers and podcasters promote for muscle recovery, weight loss and anti-aging. They are not FDA-approved drugs — today they exist in a 'gray market,' bought online from unregulated suppliers. The FDA decides which raw substances licensed pharmacies can legally use to custom-compound medicines via its '503A Bulk Drug Substances List'; being added to that list would give these peptides a legal, prescription-based sales channel for the first time.

stories.title: Sunrisers Leeds Women thump MI London by 7 wickets to open their Hundred title defence
stories.what_happened: In the opening match of the 2026 Hundred women's season at Kennington Oval, MI London Women were restricted to 107/8, with Hayley Matthews (33) and Danni Wyatt-Hodge (29) their top scorers. Sunrisers Leeds Women chased it down comfortably, winning by 7 wickets, with Phoebe Litchfield's 43 off 26 balls earning her player of the match.
key_points: MI London Women were bowled out for just 107/8 at The Oval
Phoebe Litchfield blazed 43 off 26 balls to help Sunrisers Leeds cruise home
Sunrisers Leeds (formerly Northern Superchargers) are the defending champions
The result opens the 2026 Hundred season alongside the men's competition

depth.background: The Hundred is England's 100-balls-a-side franchise cricket competition, running men's and women's tournaments in parallel each summer. Teams were rebranded this year — Northern Superchargers became Sunrisers Leeds, and Oval Invincibles became MI London — part of a wider push to bring in city-branded ownership groups (including IPL franchise owners) to grow the competition's profile.

stories.title: Microsoft strikes a multibillion-dollar deal to power up Mistral — Europe's bid to run frontier AI on its own soil
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, Microsoft and Mistral announced an expanded partnership that Microsoft describes as a multibillion-dollar commitment to build AI computing capacity across Europe. Microsoft will fund Mistral's growing fleet of GPUs — the specialised chips that train and run AI — including thousands of Nvidia's latest 'Vera Rubin' processors installed in data centres in France. Crucially, European enterprises and regulated industries (banks, hospitals, governments) will be able to run Mistral's models on 'Azure Local' — including fully disconnected servers with no internet connection — so sensitive data never leaves their own premises. Microsoft is providing the money but is NOT taking a new equity stake, a deliberate choice given the earlier EU scrutiny.
key_points: Microsoft will fund Mistral's European data-centre build-out — a multibillion-dollar bet
Thousands of Nvidia's newest 'Vera Rubin' chips go into French data centres
European firms can now run these AI models offline, on their own servers
Microsoft funds the compute but takes NO new ownership stake — dodging EU antitrust

depth.background: Mistral AI is a Paris-based startup, founded in 2023, that has become Europe's flagship answer to America's OpenAI and Google — it builds large language models (the technology behind chatbots like ChatGPT). Europe has grown anxious that almost all cutting-edge AI, and the giant data centres that run it, are controlled by a handful of US firms — a dependence governments increasingly see as a strategic risk. Microsoft already owned a small slice of Mistral from a 2024 deal, an arrangement EU regulators had eyed for antitrust concerns (whether it let a US giant quietly capture Europe's champion).

stories.title: Ukraine erupts in wartime protests demanding army chief Syrsky be sacked — Zelensky is already interviewing replacements
stories.what_happened: For a fifth straight day, several thousand people gathered outside the presidential office in Kyiv, demanding that Fedorov be reinstated and that Syrsky be dismissed by Friday. Fedorov has publicly accused Syrsky of blocking the reforms Ukraine needs 'because the war has changed completely' — meaning cheap drones and software now decide battles more than traditional tactics. President Zelensky, feeling the pressure, has confirmed he is reviewing candidates to replace Syrsky and has reportedly begun interviewing people for the role.
key_points: Crowds have rallied for days outside Zelensky's office
Trigger: a popular, drone-focused defence minister was dropped
Protesters want army chief Syrsky gone by Friday
Rare public dissent inside a country at war

depth.background: Ukraine has been fighting Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022, and for most of that time public criticism of its own leaders has been muted — the country closes ranks in wartime. Oleksandr Syrsky has been commander-in-chief (the top general running the whole war effort) since early 2024, when Zelensky replaced the hugely popular Valery Zaluzhny with him. The current anger was lit by a government reshuffle in which Mykhailo Fedorov — a 35-year-old former tech minister widely credited with Ukraine's game-changing drone programme — was pushed out of the defence job.

stories.title: Red Bull picks Verstappen's next race engineer — and pulls him back from a move to Williams to get him
stories.what_happened: Red Bull has settled on Tom Hart, a more-than-decade Red Bull engineer who currently works as a performance engineer for both Verstappen and Racing Bulls' Isack Hadjar, to take over as Verstappen's race engineer once Lambiase departs. Hart had been on the verge of leaving to become Alex Albon's race engineer at Williams, but Red Bull intervened, asked Williams to reverse the move, and reached an agreement to keep him. Hart may start taking on some race-engineering duties during select sessions later this year so he and Verstappen can build rapport before a full handover.
key_points: Red Bull has chosen Tom Hart to become Max Verstappen's new race engineer
Hart was about to join Williams as Alex Albon's engineer — Red Bull blocked the move
Current engineer Gianpiero Lambiase is leaving for a head-of-race-office role at McLaren from 2028
Hart already works with both Verstappen and Isack Hadjar as a performance engineer

depth.background: A driver's race engineer is the person who talks to them on the radio during every session — advising on strategy, tyres, and car balance. It's one of the most trust-dependent relationships in Formula 1; Verstappen and Gianpiero Lambiase have worked together since Verstappen's rookie season and built one of the sport's most recognisable driver-engineer partnerships. Lambiase is moving to McLaren to become head of its race office starting in 2028, after a period of gardening leave — a notice period where an employee is paid but kept away from a competitor.

stories.title: McLaren puts Haas-seat favourite Fornaroli in Piastri's car for Hungarian GP practice
stories.what_happened: McLaren is running Fornaroli in Oscar Piastri's car for first practice (FP1) at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix — his second FP1 appearance this season after finishing fifth in an earlier outing in Barcelona. It comes weeks after Haas gave him a two-day test in a 2025-spec car at Jerez under F1's 'Testing of Previous Cars' allowance. McLaren team principal Andrea Stella said: 'We should work together with Leonardo to explore possibilities in Formula 1... When he gets in a car, be it a simulator, be it in the TPC car, or in a free practice one session, he is fast.' Haas boss Ayao Komatsu called him 'a credible driver' after the Jerez test. Asked about his own seat, Ocon said: 'Honestly, we have bigger problems with the car at the moment than that.'
key_points: Reigning F2 champion Leonardo Fornaroli gets his second FP1 outing of the season, in Piastri's McLaren
He's emerged as the favourite to take Esteban Ocon's Haas seat for 2027
McLaren can't offer Fornaroli a race seat itself — Norris and Piastri are locked in — so it's angling to place him elsewhere
Haas already gave him a two-day test in a 2025 car at Jerez this month

depth.background: Fornaroli is a McLaren-contracted junior driver, but McLaren's own two race seats are filled long-term by Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri, so the team is trying to get him a seat at another squad instead — the way F1 teams often 'loan out' promising juniors. Haas currently runs Ollie Bearman, a Ferrari junior widely expected to stay, alongside veteran Esteban Ocon, who is out of contract at the end of 2026, has just 3 points this season, and is reportedly under pressure after a difficult run.

stories.title: India's Parliament opens its Monsoon Session with the opposition ready to fight the government on multiple fronts
stories.what_happened: The Monsoon Session of Parliament opened today, July 21, and runs until August 21, 2026. The government is expected to bring several bills before the two houses — the Lok Sabha (House of the People, directly elected) and Rajya Sabha (Council of States, representing the states) — while the Opposition has signalled it will use the session to challenge the government on a range of political and policy issues.
key_points: Monsoon Session runs July 21 to August 21, 2026
Government set to introduce several key bills
Opposition plans to press the government on multiple political and policy issues
Comes amid the youth-led 'Cockroach' protests and Punjab farmers' march already on the streets

depth.background: India's Parliament meets in three sessions a year — Budget (February), Monsoon (July-August) and Winter (November-December). The Monsoon Session is where the government usually pushes through a fresh batch of legislation before the year's second half, while the Opposition uses the session to force debate on the events of the preceding months.

stories.title: Pakistan drops Babar Azam and Saim Ayub from its ODI squad to test young talent against Bangladesh
stories.what_happened: Pakistan announced its squad for an upcoming three-match ODI series against Bangladesh, leaving out regular batters Babar Azam and Saim Ayub. Captain Shaheen Afridi said the move is about resting Babar rather than dropping him permanently, and framed the series as a chance to test younger players. Sahibzada Farhan, who finished the T20 World Cup as the tournament's leading run-scorer, earns a call-up, alongside five other new faces.
key_points: Babar Azam and Saim Ayub left out of Pakistan's ODI squad for the Bangladesh series
ODI captain Shaheen Afridi says Babar is 'rested, not dropped'
Sahibzada Farhan, the T20 World Cup's top run-scorer, gets his ODI call-up
Five other new faces also included in the squad

depth.background: Babar Azam is Pakistan's most prolific modern batter and a former captain, but has faced growing scrutiny over his one-day form and place in a team trying to build depth ahead of future ICC tournaments. Shaheen Shah Afridi, primarily known as a fast bowler, now captains Pakistan's ODI side.

stories.title: Carlos Sainz drops his clearest hint yet that he's staying at Williams for 2027
stories.what_happened: Sainz said: "What I'm doing 100 percent is developing this car. I'm fully committed to helping the team change direction, and the team knows that my priority is to try and help and stay at Williams." He has previously said Williams' current car has fallen short of expectations, but says he'll judge the team's direction by the upgrades it brings to the Azerbaijan Grand Prix in September.
key_points: Sainz says his priority is helping Williams improve for 2027
Comes despite a difficult 2026 season and persistent Audi rumours
Williams will reveal car upgrades at the Azerbaijan GP in September
Sainz had reportedly set that race as an informal deadline to decide his future

depth.background: Carlos Sainz joined Williams for the 2025 season after losing his Ferrari seat, hoping to help rebuild a team that once won championships but has struggled for over a decade. Persistent rumours have linked him to a possible switch to Audi, which is entering F1 as a full works team from 2026, as Williams' 2026 car has underperformed.

stories.title: Haas slumps to a fight with Williams at the back of the grid as Bearman's Ferrari links grow louder
stories.what_happened: Haas has gone over a month without scoring a point, sliding into a fight with Williams for last place in the constructors' championship. Bearman, who is out-scoring Ocon by 15 points this season and is out of contract at the end of 2026, is widely expected to eventually move up to Ferrari. Team principal Ayao Komatsu said this week that decision is 'not up to' Haas. Separately, Haas confirmed reserve driver Ryo Hirakawa will take over Bearman's car for Friday's first practice session in Hungary, fulfilling the team's mandatory rookie-running requirement.
key_points: Haas hasn't scored a point since the Monaco Grand Prix in early June
Bearman leads teammate Ocon by 15 points this season
Team boss Komatsu says Bearman's Ferrari-linked future is 'not up to us'
Reserve driver Ryo Hirakawa replaces Bearman for Hungary's first practice session

depth.background: Haas fields Oliver Bearman, a highly-rated Ferrari junior driver on loan to the smaller team, alongside veteran Esteban Ocon. F1 rules require every team to give a young or inexperienced driver track time in first practice (FP1) at least twice a season, which is why reserve drivers periodically take over a race seat for that one session.

stories.title: Red Bull picks Max Verstappen's next race engineer, ending one of F1's most successful radio partnerships
stories.what_happened: Multiple outlets reported Red Bull has settled on Tom Hart, a performance engineer who has been with the team since 2012, to take over from Lambiase as Verstappen's race engineer. Hart had reportedly already agreed to move to Williams to become Alex Albon's race engineer, but Red Bull persuaded him to stay and take this role instead. He is expected to fully step in from the start of the 2027 season, though he may shadow or take over for some races later in 2026 as part of the handover. Lambiase is set to join McLaren, ultimately becoming its chief racing officer in 2028.
key_points: Tom Hart will replace Gianpiero Lambiase as Verstappen's race engineer
Lambiase is leaving for McLaren, where he'll become chief racing officer in 2028
Hart, a Red Bull performance engineer since 2012, had already agreed to join Williams before Red Bull pulled him back
The handover is expected to fully take effect from the 2027 season

depth.background: In Formula 1, a driver's race engineer is the one voice they hear over the radio during a race — relaying strategy, tyre and fuel info, and reading the driver's mood lap by lap. Gianpiero Lambiase has been Max Verstappen's race engineer since 2016 and has been on the radio for all four of Verstappen's world championships, making it one of the most trusted driver-engineer relationships on the grid. Lambiase's planned departure to a senior role at McLaren was reported last week, leaving Red Bull needing a successor for one of its most sensitive jobs, especially with Verstappen's own future at Red Bull already the subject of constant speculation this year.

stories.title: India's top refiners halt Iraqi oil shipments as the Strait of Hormuz becomes too dangerous to sail
stories.what_happened: Indian Oil Corporation and Mangalore Refinery & Petrochemicals (MRPL) have both suspended fresh crude loadings from Iraq, judging that sending fully-laden tankers through the Strait of Hormuz is no longer worth the risk after a string of attacks on commercial vessels in the area. Indian Oil specifically abandoned plans to load a 2-million-barrel tanker, the Lila Jamnagar. India's shipping ministry has separately instructed shipowners and crewing agencies not to deploy Indian seafarers on any vessel transiting Hormuz until further notice — a precaution given how many merchant sailors globally are Indian nationals.
key_points: Indian Oil Corp and Mangalore Refinery (MRPL) both pause loadings of Iraqi crude
Indian Oil scrapped a planned 2-million-barrel tanker shipment through Hormuz
India's shipping ministry has told crewing agencies not to deploy Indian sailors on ships transiting Hormuz
Iraq is one of India's largest oil suppliers under long-term contracts

depth.background: India imports over 85% of the crude oil it needs, and Iraq has long been one of its top two suppliers alongside Russia. Under India's contracts with Iraq, Indian refiners — not Iraq — are responsible for arranging the tankers that carry the oil home, which means Indian companies bear the risk if that shipping route becomes dangerous. The US-Iran war, now in its second week of intensifying strikes, runs directly through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway at the mouth of the Persian Gulf that roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes through, and Yemen's Houthi rebels have separately declared a blockade on Saudi oil shipments through the Red Sea.

stories.title: F1's silly season domino: Sainz eyes an Audi seat, which could hand Tsunoda a Williams lifeline
stories.what_happened: Reports say Sainz's management held serious talks with Audi bosses during last weekend's British Grand Prix about a possible move to replace Hulkenberg for 2027, with Sainz described as 'ready to sign at any time.' If that move happens, Williams would need a replacement — and Yuki Tsunoda, who Red Bull dropped mid-season, is the name being floated, with reports Williams is preparing to open talks with him. Separately, Tsunoda has been handed a run in a previous-spec Red Bull car this week in Red Bull's testing programme, keeping his name in the conversation regardless of where he lands.
key_points: Sainz reportedly held 'fairly concrete' talks with Audi at the British GP
He could replace Hulkenberg at the rebranded Audi team for 2027
That would leave a seat open at Williams — Tsunoda is the name in the frame
Tsunoda, dropped by Red Bull mid-season, gets a Red Bull test run this week regardless

depth.background: F1's driver market moves like a chain of dominoes — one seat opening up cascades into several more. Carlos Sainz lost his Ferrari seat and joined Williams; Yuki Tsunoda was dropped by Red Bull mid-2026 and is now their reserve/test driver. Audi (which took over the Sauber team) currently races Nico Hulkenberg and Gabriel Bortoleto.

stories.title: Assam's flood toll climbs past 10 as over 3 lakh are hit; Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts report at least 15 more dead
stories.what_happened: As of Tuesday, Assam's flood death toll had climbed past 10, with over 3 lakh people affected in 11 districts; rivers including the Brahmaputra are flowing above the danger mark, and authorities have evacuated thousands by boat into relief camps. Separately, rain-related incidents in Jammu & Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts have killed at least 15 people, with several others still missing. India's weather office has warned that active monsoon conditions and the risk of more extremely heavy rain could persist for up to another week.
key_points: Assam's flood death toll has risen past 10, with more than 3 lakh people affected across 11 districts
The Brahmaputra and tributaries including the Dikhow, Desang and Dhansiri are flowing above the danger mark
Over 9,000 people evacuated by boat in Assam; thousands more sheltering in relief camps
Separately, rain-triggered incidents in Jammu & Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts have killed at least 15, with several still missing

depth.background: Northeast India and Jammu & Kashmir are both in the middle of an active southwest monsoon spell. Heavy rain has been triggering floods in Assam's river districts and landslides in Kashmir's hill districts since the weekend, and the toll first reported — at least 26 dead across Kashmir, Nagaland and Assam combined — has continued to rise as more remote areas report in.

thread.previously: Monday's story reported flash floods across Kashmir, Nagaland and Assam had killed at least 26 people combined, forcing three major pilgrimages to be suspended.
thread.changed: The toll has kept climbing region by region: Assam alone is now past 10 dead with over 3 lakh people affected and thousands evacuated by boat, while Jammu & Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts separately report at least 15 dead with more still missing — and IMD is warning the heavy rain could continue for another week.

prev_ref.title: Monsoon flash floods kill at least 26 across Kashmir, Nagaland and Assam — three major pilgrimages suspended

stories.title: Britain's new PM Andy Burnham holds his first Cabinet meeting — and cuts a tax on energy bills
stories.what_happened: On Monday, Starmer travelled to Buckingham Palace to formally resign to King Charles III, and Burnham returned to Downing Street as Britain's new prime minister — its seventh in a decade. On Tuesday, Burnham chaired his first Cabinet meeting, telling ministers he would run a 'one-team approach' even as he swept out several Starmer loyalists. His first concrete move: scrapping VAT (a sales tax) on domestic electricity bills from October 1, saving the average household about £45 a year, funded by cancelling Starmer's planned digital ID programme for right-to-work checks.
key_points: Burnham became UK's 7th PM in 10 years after Starmer's exit
First act: scrapping VAT on electricity bills from October
Cut funded by axing Starmer's digital ID programme
Burnham calls it a 'cost-of-living government', purges Starmer allies

depth.background: Keir Starmer announced in June he would step down as UK prime minister after less than two years in office, worn down by poor local election results, tax rows and public anger over issues from immigration to the Gaza war. Andy Burnham, the popular former mayor of Greater Manchester known as the 'King of the North', won the contest to succeed him as Labour leader. In Britain's system, a governing party can swap its leader — and therefore the prime minister — mid-term without a general election, which is how Burnham took over without voters choosing him directly.

stories.title: India's Under-19s pile up 411 and reduce Sri Lanka to 122/8 — young batters Kushagra Ojha and Manal Chauhan both score centuries
stories.what_happened: India Under-19 resumed day two on 253/3 and piled on runs, with Kushagra Ojha and Manal Chauhan sharing a 155-run partnership. Ojha was run out for 111, but Chauhan carried on to complete his century and push past 150 before India were eventually all out for 411. In reply, Sri Lanka Under-19 made a promising start at 35/0 but then collapsed dramatically to 122/8 by the close of play, leaving them 140 runs short of avoiding the follow-on with just two wickets standing.
key_points: India U-19 declared/were bowled out for 411 in the 2nd Youth Test in Colombo
Kushagra Ojha (111) and Manal Chauhan (over 150) both scored centuries in a 155-run stand
Sri Lanka U-19 collapsed from a bright start to 122/8 in reply
Sri Lanka still needs 140 more runs just to avoid the follow-on, with only 2 wickets in hand

depth.background: Youth Tests are multi-day matches between national Under-19 teams, used to develop young players before they graduate to senior international cricket — many current India stars, from Virat Kohli to Shubman Gill, came through this pipeline. India's U-19s are on a tour of Sri Lanka playing a two-match Youth Test series, with this second match at Colombo's R Premadasa Stadium.

stories.title: Aston Martin boss admits the team might have picked a different F1 engine partner than Honda, with hindsight
stories.what_happened: Speaking to press this week, Aston Martin team principal Mike Krack said that with hindsight, the team 'would have done things differently, and even chosen other partners' had it known how competitive Honda's 2026 power unit would actually be. He referenced 'a lot of frustration' and pointed comments from Newey after an early-season low point in Melbourne. Krack said the rocky start has pushed Aston Martin and Honda to work more closely together, with the team's first major Newey-designed upgrade due at this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, ahead of Honda's own revised engine at Zandvoort.
key_points: Team boss Mike Krack says Aston Martin might have chosen another engine partner had it known Honda's 2026 form
He cites 'a lot of frustration' and strong comments from design chief Adrian Newey after Melbourne
The team's first major Newey-led upgrade arrives at the Hungarian GP
Honda has its own revised power unit coming at the Dutch GP in Zandvoort

depth.background: Formula 1 introduced all-new engine ('power unit') regulations for 2026, and Aston Martin switched from customer Mercedes engines to a brand-new works partnership with Honda, hoping to build a title-contending team around star signing Adrian Newey. Honda's return to F1 as a full works partner was seen as a coup, but the combined package has struggled to match rivals all season.

stories.title: Alonso says his F1 future rests on the rules, not on Aston Martin — and 2027's engine shake-up is the real deciding factor
stories.what_happened: Speaking at last weekend's Belgian Grand Prix, Alonso said his decision about racing next year will hinge on the rules and how enjoyable the cars are to drive — not on how well Aston Martin performs. He said driving the current hybrid cars at tracks like Spa or Silverstone 'is not what I'm dreaming of.' Aston Martin is pinning hopes on a big upgrade at this weekend's Hungarian GP to help persuade him to stay.
key_points: Alonso, 44, has a contract expiring at the end of 2026
Says his stay depends on whether the cars are fun to drive, not team results
Aston Martin is banking on a Hungary upgrade to keep him
2027 rules will tilt engines back toward petrol power

depth.background: Fernando Alonso is a two-time world champion and, at 44, one of the oldest drivers on the grid. He races for Aston Martin, a team that has struggled badly with F1's all-new 2026 cars — its package has been overweight and unreliable. His contract runs out at the end of this year, so whether he keeps racing into 2027 (past his 45th birthday) has become a major talking point.

stories.title: India's Navy commissions INS Malvan, a 'submarine hunter' built to track Chinese and Pakistani subs in the Indian Ocean
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, the Indian Navy commissioned INS Malvan, an anti-submarine warfare shallow-water craft — a compact vessel specialised in detecting, tracking and attacking submarines close to the coast and in shallow seas where larger warships are less effective. The Navy framed the induction as part of raising its fleet to counter the Chinese and Pakistani underwater threat in the Indian Ocean Region (IOR).
key_points: New warship is designed to detect and hunt enemy submarines in shallow coastal waters
Aimed at China's growing and Pakistan's underwater presence in the Indian Ocean
Part of a homegrown fleet India is building up under 'Make in India'
Named after Malvan, a coastal town in Maharashtra

depth.background: The Indian Ocean is India's strategic backyard — most of its trade and almost all of its oil imports cross it — and for over a decade China has steadily expanded its naval footprint there, sending submarines and warships far from home, while Pakistan has been building up its own submarine fleet, much of it with Chinese help. Submarines are the hardest threat to counter because they hide underwater and can strike ships or blockade ports unseen. To answer that, India has been inducting a class of small, purpose-built anti-submarine vessels made in Indian shipyards, part of a broader push to build warships at home rather than import them.

stories.title: Novo Nordisk sues Eli Lilly, saying its weight-loss ads mislead patients about which drug works better
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, 2026, Novo Nordisk sued Eli Lilly in US District Court in New Jersey, alleging false advertising and unfair competition under the federal Lanham Act and state laws. Novo says Lilly's nationwide ads pit the highest approved dose of Zepbound against a lower, older dose of Wegovy — one commercial claims patients lose 50 pounds on Lilly's drug versus 33 pounds on Novo's — while ignoring Novo's newer high-dose (7.2 mg) Wegovy, approved this year, which narrows the gap. Novo says it sent a cease-and-desist letter in April; Lilly instead added what Novo calls an inadequate disclaimer. Novo wants a court order pulling the ads and forcing a corrective campaign, and will seek an injunction if Lilly does not comply.
key_points: Novo says Lilly's ads compare its top dose to Novo's older, weaker one
The disputed ad claims 50 lbs lost on Zepbound vs 33 on Wegovy
Novo wants the ads pulled and a 'corrective' campaign run
It's the latest front in the two giants' obesity-drug war

depth.background: Two companies dominate the booming market for GLP-1 drugs — a class of medicines (first made for diabetes) that curb appetite and produce large weight loss. Novo Nordisk makes Ozempic and Wegovy (both semaglutide); Eli Lilly makes Mounjaro and Zepbound (both tirzepatide). Together they form a near-duopoly worth tens of billions of dollars, and Lilly's Zepbound has been winning market share partly by advertising bigger average weight loss than Novo's drugs.

stories.title: Aston Martin's boss admits the team might have picked a different F1 engine partner than Honda
stories.what_happened: Team principal Mike Krack said Aston Martin might have picked a different engine partner had it known in advance how Honda's 2026 power unit would actually perform. The comment comes as the team prepares its first major upgrade of the year — a Newey-led chassis package — for this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix, while Honda saves its own engine upgrade for the Dutch Grand Prix at Zandvoort in August.
key_points: Team principal Mike Krack says with hindsight, Aston Martin might not have chosen Honda
The AMR26 has struggled with performance and reliability all season
A major Adrian Newey-designed chassis upgrade lands this weekend in Hungary
Honda's own engine upgrade is saved for the Dutch GP at Zandvoort in August

depth.background: Formula 1 teams don't build their own engines from scratch — most partner with a manufacturer (Mercedes, Ferrari, Honda, Renault) who supplies the power unit. Aston Martin struck a high-profile deal to become Honda's works team starting in 2026, alongside hiring star designer Adrian Newey, hoping the pairing would make them frontrunners under F1's new 2026 rules. Instead, the team has spent the season at the back of the grid.

stories.title: Israel fires warning shots at Lebanese troops on day one of the new Israel-Lebanon 'Safe Zone' pilot
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, Lebanese army troops deployed into the town of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh after an Israeli withdrawal — the first time this has happened under the new framework. The IDF says its soldiers spotted Lebanese personnel, with a military engineering vehicle, cross about 150 meters beyond the pilot zone's agreed boundary and breach barriers there. Israeli troops fired warning shots into the air; the IDF says it did not intend to endanger the Lebanese soldiers, who retreated without casualties. Lebanon's army confirmed Israeli forces fired near its troops.
key_points: Lebanese army deployed into a Hezbollah-cleared 'pilot zone' in south Lebanon for the first time
IDF says Lebanese troops with an engineering vehicle strayed ~150m past the agreed boundary
Israel fired warning shots in the air — no injuries, troops withdrew
It's the first real-world test of a fragile new US-brokered stabilization plan

depth.background: Israel and Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militant group that has fought Israel from Lebanon for decades, ended their most recent war with a fragile ceasefire. Since then, the US, Israel and the Lebanese government have been negotiating a plan to gradually replace Hezbollah's grip on southern Lebanon with the official Lebanese army — the idea being that if the Lebanese state visibly controls its own border, Israel has less reason to keep striking there. On Monday, that plan moved from paper to practice: a 'Safe Zone' pilot began in three villages, with Israeli troops withdrawing from small designated areas and the Lebanese army moving in to confirm Hezbollah weapons and fighters are gone, under US and Lebanese-army coordination.

stories.title: Bandhan Bank's profit jumps 35% as bad-loan pain eases — a sign India's smaller lenders are turning a corner
stories.what_happened: Bandhan Bank reported a 35% year-on-year jump in net profit to roughly ₹502 crore for the April-June quarter (Q1 FY27), driven by lower provisions for bad loans and improving asset quality. Net interest income grew nearly 6% to ₹2,921 crore, and total advances grew over 16%, with the bank's retail loan book growing 45%.
key_points: Net profit rose 35% year-on-year to about ₹502 crore in the June quarter
Lower provisions for bad loans and improving asset quality drove the jump
Total business grew over 11% to ₹3.2 lakh crore
Comes right after Bandhan's profit had crashed 65% a year earlier on rising bad loans

depth.background: Bandhan Bank built its business on microfinance — small, uncollateralized loans to low-income borrowers, especially women in rural India. That model is highly profitable when repayment holds up, but a rough patch (over-borrowing, election disruptions to loan collections, and a slowing rural economy) hit the sector hard over the last two years, and Bandhan's profits collapsed as a result.

stories.title: Shubman Gill named North Zone captain for the Duleep Trophy, with Arshdeep Singh and Harshit Rana in the squad
stories.what_happened: The BCCI named Shubman Gill as captain of the North Zone squad for the 2026-27 Duleep Trophy, which begins on August 28 in Bengaluru. The squad also includes fast bowlers Arshdeep Singh, Harshit Rana, and Anshul Kamboj, alongside players like Ayush Badoni and Yash Dhull.
key_points: Gill leads a North Zone squad that also includes Anshul Kamboj
Comes right after Gill captained India to a 2-2 Test series draw in England
The Duleep Trophy opens India's 2026-27 domestic season on August 28 in Bengaluru
West Zone (Ruturaj Gaikwad, Prithvi Shaw) was announced separately this week

depth.background: The Duleep Trophy is one of India's oldest domestic cricket tournaments, pitting six zonal teams (North, South, East, West, Central, North East) built from state players against each other. It's traditionally used to kick off India's long domestic season and as a shop window for players hoping to break into the national team.

stories.title: India's first private rocket reached orbit — now its maker wants to launch one every month
stories.what_happened: On July 18, 2026, Skyroot's Vikram-1 lifted off from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre at Sriharikota — after a 35-minute countdown hold for a last-minute technical check — and reached its planned low Earth orbit about 450 km up, releasing several small technology-demonstration satellites. That made Skyroot the first private Indian company to put a payload into orbit from Indian soil. In an interview aired on July 21, CEO Pawan Kumar Chandana said the next goal is to ramp up to roughly one launch a month.
key_points: Skyroot's Vikram-1 is India's first private rocket to reach orbit
It flew from Sriharikota on July 18, hitting a ~450 km orbit
CEO now says 'one launch a month' is the next goal
A milestone for India's newly opened private space sector

depth.background: For decades every Indian rocket was built and flown by ISRO, the government space agency. In 2020 India opened space to private companies and created a regulator, IN-SPACe, to license and encourage them. Hyderabad-based Skyroot Aerospace, founded in 2018, was the first to fly a private Indian rocket — the small suborbital Vikram-S in November 2022, which went up and came straight back down without circling the Earth. The far bigger prize was 'orbital' flight: actually placing a satellite into a stable path around the planet, which takes vastly more power and precision.

stories.title: Lebanon's army moves into the south as Israel starts pulling back — the first real test of a US-brokered deal
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, July 21, 2026, Lebanese army troops began deploying in the southern town of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh after Israeli forces pulled out — the first handover under the US plan, nicknamed the 'pilot zone' program. The US State Department said the Lebanese army was also taking over security in the nearby villages of Froun and Srifa. Lebanese President Joseph Aoun was due in Washington later the same day to meet President Donald Trump. But Hezbollah has rejected the deal and refuses to disarm, and there is still no timetable for Israel's full withdrawal.
key_points: Israeli troops left a first town; the Lebanese army moved in
Part of a US 'pilot zone' plan to unwind the occupation
The deal asks Lebanon's army to disarm Hezbollah in the south
President Aoun heads to Washington to meet Trump

depth.background: Israel and Hezbollah — the powerful Iran-backed armed group and political party in Lebanon — fought a bruising war that left Israeli troops holding a strip of southern Lebanon several kilometres deep. Washington has been brokering a plan to unwind that occupation in stages: Israel withdraws town by town, and in return Lebanon's national army moves in and strips Hezbollah of its weapons in those areas.

stories.title: Fernando Alonso sits out Hungarian GP first practice with a back injury, cleared to return for FP2
stories.what_happened: Aston Martin announced Alonso would skip first practice (FP1) for the Hungarian Grand Prix to manage a back muscle injury in his lumbar area, picked up over the Belgian Grand Prix weekend. Reserve driver Felipe Drugovich drove the car in Alonso's place during FP1. Alonso was medically cleared afterward and returned for second practice (FP2), finishing fifth — one place behind teammate Lance Stroll — and said he has no pain while driving, though he'll need to keep managing his comfort in the seat for the rest of the weekend.
key_points: Alonso has a muscular injury in his lower-back (lumbar) area from the Belgian GP weekend
Aston Martin reserve driver Felipe Drugovich stood in for him in first practice
Alonso was medically cleared and returned for second practice, finishing fifth
He says the injury is manageable but needs rest, with F1's summer break coming up

depth.background: Fernando Alonso is a two-time F1 world champion driving for Aston Martin, one of the sport's veteran drivers still competing at the top level. F1 cars generate huge cornering forces that put constant strain on drivers' backs and necks, and niggling injuries picked up over a race weekend are common — teams have to weigh a driver's discomfort against the risk of racing hurt.

stories.title: A Chinese military helicopter crosses the Taiwan Strait median line for the first time — a new 'gray zone' warning shot
stories.what_happened: Taiwan's Ministry of National Defense said a People's Liberation Army helicopter and a PLA drone operated on Taiwan's side of the median line between 10:20 a.m. and 7:40 p.m. on Monday, July 20. A defense researcher at Taiwan's government-funded INDSR institute said it appears to be the first time a PLA helicopter has been detected crossing the line — a rotary-wing aircraft doing what previously only fixed-wing jets and drones had done. Separately that same day, another PLA aircraft flew into a restricted zone east of the median line for what researchers say was also a first. Over the following 24 hours, Taiwan tracked five PLA warships, six China Coast Guard vessels, and four additional aircraft sorties operating around the island.
key_points: A PLA helicopter and a PLA drone entered Taiwan's side of the Strait median line Monday
Experts say it's the first confirmed helicopter crossing ever recorded
Same day, a separate PLA aircraft entered a restricted zone further east than usual
Taiwan's defense ministry also logged 5 warships, 6 official vessels, 4 aircraft sorties nearby in 24 hours

depth.background: The Taiwan Strait median line is an unofficial boundary roughly down the middle of the waters separating Taiwan from mainland China. For decades both sides largely respected it as a way to avoid direct confrontation. China considers Taiwan a breakaway province it intends to eventually control, by force if necessary, while Taiwan governs itself democratically and rejects Beijing's claim. Since 2022, China has increasingly sent military aircraft and ships across or near the line — a strategy analysts call 'gray zone' warfare: actions aggressive enough to pressure and exhaust Taiwan's military, but calibrated to stop short of an act of war that would trigger a shooting response.

stories.title: France is on the verge of becoming the first country in Europe to ban children under 15 from social media
stories.what_happened: France's National Assembly (the lower house of parliament) and the Senate (the upper house) reached a compromise on the bill's exact wording on Monday, July 20, clearing the way for a final adoption vote expected on Tuesday, which lawmakers believe will pass despite objections from parties on the left. The bill would block anyone under 15 from Facebook, Snapchat, TikTok and YouTube and require platforms to run age-verification systems, with exceptions for educational sites and online encyclopaedias. Under-15s could not open new accounts from September 1, and platforms would get four more months to close accounts children already have. Because of an EU rule requiring member states to notify Brussels first, the law could not take effect before August 10 even if adopted this week. Digital Minister Anne Le Henanff said France would become 'the first country in Europe to introduce a digital age limit,' and President Emmanuel Macron has backed the push.
key_points: Upper and lower houses reached a compromise; a final vote is expected this week
The ban covers TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and YouTube; platforms must check users' ages
Under-15s couldn't open new accounts from September 1
Left-wing critics and many teens oppose it, and enforcement is the hard part

depth.background: Governments worldwide are wrestling with growing evidence that heavy social media use can harm children's mental health and expose them to bullying and harmful content. Australia passed a world-first law in 2024 banning under-16s from social media, and France has been pushing the European Union to set a 'digital age of majority' of 15. This debate runs in parallel with the trial in the US where the state of Tennessee accuses Meta of designing Instagram to hook children.

stories.title: India's rupee slides toward a record low as the RBI steps in to defend it
stories.what_happened: The rupee weakened to around 96.4 per US dollar this week — its weakest since May and closing in on the all-time low of roughly 96.97 hit in late May — as Brent crude oil climbed on fears tied to the ongoing US-Iran war. The RBI intervened, selling dollars in both onshore and offshore markets to slow the decline, after earlier capital-inflow measures pulled in more than $20 billion but weren't enough to offset the pressure.
key_points: Rupee touched about 96.4/USD, near its record low of 96.97 hit in May
RBI sold dollars in both onshore and offshore markets to slow the fall
Rising oil prices — Brent crude climbing on Middle East fears — are the main pressure
RBI officials are internally divided over how hard to fight the market trend

depth.background: The rupee is India's currency, and its value against the US dollar matters because India imports most of its oil in dollars — a weaker rupee makes fuel, and everything shipped using it, more expensive at home. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is India's central bank; it doesn't fix the exchange rate but occasionally sells dollars from its foreign-currency reserves to slow a fall it judges too sharp or disorderly.

stories.title: Pakistan is asked to broker an Iran ceasefire again — Washington worries Iranian hardliners won't let it hold this time
stories.what_happened: According to reporting this week, Pakistan is again being asked to mediate between Washington and Tehran as the current bout of strikes drags on with no resolution in sight. US officials say their central worry isn't just getting Iran to the table — it's whether Iran's fractured leadership can actually agree on and stick to any deal Pakistan helps negotiate, given the ongoing tension between relative moderates and hardliners inside the regime. This echoes the earlier round of shuttle diplomacy, when Pakistani PM Shehbaz Sharif and army chief Asim Munir personally asked Trump to hold off a planned wave of strikes, credited by Trump himself as instrumental to the original ceasefire.
key_points: Pakistan mediated the original US-Iran truce in April; it's being leaned on to do it again
That ceasefire collapsed over control of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical oil shipping route
US officials say a split between Iranian moderates and hardliners is stopping Tehran from agreeing to terms
Trump has previously credited Pakistan's PM and army chief directly for holding off strikes during the last round

depth.background: After a bruising round of US and Israeli strikes on Iran earlier in 2026, Pakistan quietly brokered a ceasefire in April, sealed with a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) — a non-binding statement of intent, not a legally binding treaty, that laid out steps toward a lasting peace. That truce unraveled over disputes about the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that roughly a fifth of the world's oil shipments pass through. Since then, the US and Iran have traded strikes for more than a week straight. Iran's government is not a single unified voice: elected officials favor different approaches than hardline factions tied to the Revolutionary Guard and senior clerics, and any one of those power centers can undercut a deal the others agree to.

stories.title: 46 militants lay down arms in Manipur — the first mass surrender since the conflict began
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 46 active KCP-PWG cadres surrendered to Manipur Chief Minister Y. Khemchand Singh at the Assam Rifles' Mantripukhri Garrison in Imphal East district. They voluntarily handed over 28 weapons — including AK rifles, INSAS rifles, pistols, carbines and a rocket-propelled grenade launcher — along with ammunition and explosives. Officials say the surrender is the result of weeks of outreach by the Assam Rifles, Manipur Police and state government.
key_points: 46 cadres of the banned KCP (People's War Group) surrendered
They handed over 28 weapons including AK rifles and a rocket launcher
It's the first such surrender by this group since Manipur's violence began
None of the 46 were part of any earlier peace agreement

depth.background: Manipur, a northeastern Indian state, has been gripped by ethnic conflict since 2023, mainly between the Meitei and Kuki-Zo communities, alongside long-running separatist insurgencies. The Kangleipak Communist Party (People's War Group), or KCP-PWG, is one of several banned armed groups operating in the Imphal Valley that have fought both the Indian state and, at times, each other, over demands ranging from greater autonomy to full independence for the Meitei homeland.

stories.title: India vs England's Test series comes down to the Oval decider — and India are missing their in-form keeper
stories.what_happened: India and England began the fifth and deciding Test of the series at The Oval in London today, with India trailing 2-1 after four matches. India's team management confirmed Rishabh Pant, sidelined by a fractured right foot, has been replaced by Dhruv Jurel as wicketkeeper for the match.
key_points: The 5th and final Test of the Anderson-Tendulkar Trophy starts today at The Oval
India trail the series 2-1 going into the decider
Rishabh Pant is out with a fractured right foot from the 4th Test at Old Trafford
Wicketkeeper-batter Dhruv Jurel comes in as his replacement

depth.background: The Anderson-Tendulkar Trophy is the current name for the India-England Test series (renamed in 2025 to honor both countries' great players). It's separate from the one-day series between the same teams that England won 2-1 at Lord's. Rishabh Pant has been one of India's best batters this series, but he was hit on the foot by a Chris Woakes delivery on the opening day of the 4th Test in Manchester, batted on despite the injury, and has now been ruled out for the rest of the tour.

stories.title: Sensex slips for a second day as the Iran war pushes crude oil prices higher
stories.what_happened: The BSE Sensex closed 238.41 points (0.31%) lower at 77,470.11, and the NSE Nifty 50 fell 50.80 points (0.21%) to 24,187.70 on July 21. Market breadth was fairly even — 2,087 stocks rose against 1,942 that fell. HDFC Bank, Infosys and State Bank of India were among the top drags on the index.
key_points: Sensex down 238 points to 77,470; Nifty down 51 points to 24,188
Crude oil's spike from the US-Iran war is the main driver
Foreign investors kept pulling money out; HDFC Bank a heavy drag
IT, consumer durables and FMCG led losses; realty, auto and metals held up

depth.background: India imports over 80% of its oil, so when crude oil prices rise, it costs the country more to keep the lights on and the economy running — a direct hit to companies' costs and to the government's import bill. Indian stocks have been on a losing streak as the widening US-Iran war keeps oil prices elevated and foreign investors nervous.

stories.title: Sri Lanka captain Kusal Mendis ruled out for the rest of the LPL with a hamstring injury, doubt for India Tests
stories.what_happened: Mendis suffered a right hamstring injury while attempting a run during Colombo Kaps' LPL match against the Kandy Royals on July 19. Following medical assessment, Sri Lanka Cricket ruled him out of the rest of the LPL 2026 season, and he has returned to the National High Performance Centre in Colombo to begin rehabilitation.
key_points: Mendis hurt his hamstring running between the wickets on July 19
Ruled out of the rest of Lanka Premier League 2026 for Colombo Kaps
Begins rehab at Sri Lanka's National High Performance Centre
Raises doubt over his fitness for the Sri Lanka vs India Test series from August 15

depth.background: Kusal Mendis is Sri Lanka's white-ball captain and one of its senior batters, currently leading Colombo Kaps in the Lanka Premier League (LPL), the country's domestic T20 tournament. Sri Lanka is scheduled to host India for a two-match Test series starting August 15 — a marquee series for Sri Lankan cricket.

stories.title: Iran's political executions accelerate as its war with the US drags on
stories.what_happened: On July 19, Iranian authorities hanged Erfan Esfandiari (18) and Gol-Mohammad Mohammadi (24) in Isfahan, convicted on charges tied to the January 2026 protests, including 'waging war against God.' Human rights groups NCRI and Iran HRM say this brings the confirmed 2026 total of executed political prisoners to at least 47 — up sharply from 16 at the same point last year. Separately, roughly 1,500 death-row prisoners at Ghezel Hesar prison in Karaj have been on a hunger strike for over a week protesting conditions and pending executions.
key_points: Rights groups say at least 47 political prisoners have been executed in Iran so far in 2026
That's nearly three times the pace of executions at this point last year
Two more protesters from January's uprising were hanged on July 19
1,500 death-row prisoners in one Karaj prison have been on hunger strike for over a week

depth.background: Iran saw a nationwide uprising in January 2026 after a wave of unrest, followed by a mass crackdown and arrests. Iran is currently also fighting a war with the United States (covered separately in this feed), and human rights monitors have long documented Iran using periods of internal or external crisis to intensify executions of dissidents.

stories.title: Pakistan drop senior pacers Shaheen Afridi, Naseem Shah and Hasan Ali for the West Indies Tests
stories.what_happened: Pakistan named a 16-player squad for their two-match Test series in the West Indies (Tarouba, July 25-29, and Port of Spain, August 2-6) that leaves out Shaheen Shah Afridi, Naseem Shah, Hasan Ali and spinner Noman Ali. Head of selection committee Misbah-ul-Haq said the decision reflects workload management and a preference for bowlers currently active in first-class cricket, not a verdict on the dropped players' ability, adding that being left out of one squad 'should not be viewed as the end of any player's Test career.' Mohammad Ali and Aamir Jamal have been recalled, while pacer Ubaid Shah, left-arm spinner Ali Usman and batter Awais Zafar all earn their first Test call-ups. Babar Azam captains the side in his second stint as Test captain.
key_points: Shaheen Shah Afridi, Naseem Shah, Hasan Ali and spinner Noman Ali all left out
Selectors say it's about current red-ball workload and form, not a loss of faith
Mohammad Ali and Aamir Jamal recalled; three uncapped players get maiden call-ups
Babar Azam leads the squad in his second stint as Test captain after Shan Masood's removal

depth.background: Pakistan's Test team has struggled for results away from home in recent years, and the board recently removed Shan Masood as Test captain, reinstating Babar Azam — who marked his return with a century in a West Indies tour warm-up match. Shaheen Shah Afridi and Naseem Shah have long been the spearheads of Pakistan's pace attack, but neither has been playing regular four-day (first-class) cricket recently, and Shaheen in particular has managed only 27 wickets in 16 Test innings since returning from a knee injury in 2023 — a modest return for a strike bowler.

thread.previously: We reported that Babar Azam had returned for his second stint as Pakistan's Test captain, marking it with a century in a West Indies tour warm-up match.
thread.changed: We now know the squad he's actually leading: senior pacers Shaheen Afridi, Naseem Shah and Hasan Ali have all been dropped for workload/form reasons, with three uncapped players brought in instead — a much bigger shake-up than the captaincy change alone suggested.

prev_ref.title: Babar Azam marks his return as Pakistan Test captain with a century in the West Indies tour warm-up

stories.title: India's Zimbabwe T20 squad: four uncapped debutants in, Samson, Axar and Arshdeep among those axed
stories.what_happened: India's selectors named a 15-member squad for the Zimbabwe T20I series that hands maiden senior call-ups to wicketkeeper-batter Prabhsimran Singh and pacers Yash Thakur, Ashok Sharma and Harsh Dubey, while recalling express pacer Mayank Yadav for the first time since late 2024. At the same time, established names Sanju Samson, Axar Patel, Arshdeep Singh and Harshit Rana were all left out of the squad. Leg-spinner Ravi Bishnoi was added late as a replacement after Varun Chakaravarthy was ruled out injured.
key_points: Prabhsimran Singh, Yash Thakur, Ashok Sharma, Harsh Dubey all get maiden call-ups
Mayank Yadav returns to the squad for the first time since late 2024
Sanju Samson, Axar Patel, Arshdeep Singh, Harshit Rana all left out
Ravi Bishnoi drafted in late as a replacement for injured Varun Chakaravarthy

depth.background: India's tour of Zimbabwe (three T20Is in Harare, July 23–26) is being used partly as a testing ground for fringe players outside the core T20 group, with senior names rested. The squad is captained by Shreyas Iyer, with Vaibhav Sooryavanshi among the younger players getting an extended look.

stories.title: Gaza's 'ceasefire' has killed over 1,100 Palestinians since October, campaign says, as residents call the truce a fiction
