The fast demands Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit over the NEET leak
Doctors warn of possible organ damage; the government says he is checked daily

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is one of India's best-known activists — an engineer and education reformer from Ladakh whose life partly inspired the hero of the hit film '3 Idiots.' He has spent years using hunger strikes to press for statehood and constitutional protection for Ladakh, the high-altitude Himalayan region turned into a federally-run Union Territory in 2019. This latest fast, though, is tied to a different national flashpoint: the NEET medical-entrance exam leak — a scandal in which India's single test for admission to medical colleges was compromised, throwing the futures of lakhs (hundreds of thousands) of students into doubt.

stories.title: India draws up a $51bn 'make it at home' hit-list — Modi orders a push to replace imports and shield the economy from oil shocks, a weak rupee and China's grip on supplies
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, reports (led by Reuters and Bloomberg) said Prime Minister Narendra Modi's office has ordered key ministries to comb through India's import bill and identify goods the country is heavily dependent on but could make at home. Officials have flagged about $51 billion of 'critical' imports — inputs used across industries from textiles to solar panels — as the priority pool, with roughly 100 specific items lined up for immediate action. A broader internal analysis suggested that up to $398 billion of India's total imports could, over time, potentially be replaced by domestic manufacturing. To make that happen, the government is weighing subsidies and other incentives to nudge companies to produce these goods locally.
key_points: Govt flags $51bn of 'critical' imports to be made in India instead
About 100 items — from textiles to solar panels — get immediate action
Analysis: up to $398bn of India's $775bn imports could be localised
Subsidies and incentives planned; aim is to ease pressure on the rupee

depth.background: India buys far more from the world than it sells — it imported about $775 billion of goods in the year to March 2026 — and that gap has to be paid for in foreign currency, mostly US dollars. When imports surge (say, when oil prices spike), India needs more dollars, which pushes the rupee down and makes everything imported dearer. India has long wanted to make more of what it uses at home — the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India) drive — and has offered 'PLI' schemes (production-linked incentives: cash rewards paid to companies for making more here). This new plan widens that effort into a systematic hunt for imports the country could realistically replace with local production.

stories.title: Britain nationalises British Steel — the government seizes its last major steel mill from its Chinese owner to keep the country able to make its own steel
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, the UK government formally took British Steel into public ownership. Parliament had just passed the Steel Industry (Nationalisation) Act, which received Royal Assent (the final sign-off that makes a bill law) on July 15; Industry Minister Chris McDonald then signed regulations transferring the company to the state the next day. Ministers said months of talks with Jingye had failed to reach a deal that would both secure the plant's future and give value for taxpayers, so nationalisation was the only option left. Business Secretary Peter Kyle said an independent assessor will now judge whether Jingye is owed any compensation at all — and made clear the answer could be nothing.
key_points: UK takes British Steel into public ownership, effective July 16
Strips Scunthorpe — the UK's last 'virgin' steel plant — from China's Jingye
Talks with Jingye failed; an assessor will decide any compensation (maybe zero)
Protects ~2,700 jobs and the ability to make steel from scratch at home

depth.background: Steel is the backbone material of a modern economy — it goes into railways, cars, buildings, weapons and wind turbines. There are two ways to make it: from scratch, by melting iron ore in giant 'blast furnaces' (called 'virgin' or 'primary' steelmaking), or by recycling scrap in electric furnaces. Britain's steel industry has shrunk for decades, and the Scunthorpe plant in northern England is the country's last site still able to make virgin steel from raw ore. A Chinese company, Jingye, bought British Steel in 2020 and poured in more than £1.2bn, but the plant kept losing money. In April 2025 the government passed emergency powers and stepped in to stop Jingye letting the blast furnaces go cold — because once a blast furnace shuts down and solidifies, it is ruinously hard and expensive to restart.

stories.title: TSMC posts a record quarter and pledges another $100bn to build chips in America — lifting its US bet to $265bn as the AI boom rages
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, TSMC reported April–June net profit of about T$706.6bn (~$22bn), up 77% from a year earlier and a company record, easily beating analysts' forecasts — its ninth straight quarter of double-digit growth. Alongside the results, CEO C.C. Wei announced an additional $100bn investment to expand its Arizona operations, raising TSMC's total planned US commitment to $265bn. The new money will fund several more 'fabs' (chip factories) making 2-nanometer-and-below chips — the smallest, fastest, most advanced class — plus advanced 'packaging' plants that stitch chips together. TSMC also raised its 2026 revenue-growth outlook to roughly 40% (from 'more than 30%') and lifted capital spending to $60–64bn, all on booming demand for AI chips.
key_points: Q2 profit jumped 77% to a record ~$22bn, blowing past forecasts
TSMC will pour another $100bn into Arizona — total US pledge now $265bn
The new money builds 2-nanometer (and smaller) chip and packaging plants
It lifted 2026 revenue guidance to ~+40%, citing surging AI demand

depth.background: TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company) is the world's most important chipmaker — a 'foundry' that manufactures the advanced processors designed by others, from Apple's iPhone chips to Nvidia's AI accelerators. Roughly nine in ten of the world's most cutting-edge chips are made in its factories, almost all of them in Taiwan. That concentration has become a geopolitical worry: Taiwan sits in China's shadow, and Washington has pushed TSMC to build plants on American soil to secure supply. TSMC had already committed about $165bn to US factories in Arizona; today's move stacks another $100bn on top.

stories.title: Brain drain at India's space agency: 100+ ISRO scientists — many on the Gaganyaan crewed mission — have quit, so the government is now freezing exits
stories.what_happened: According to reports across The Times of India, India Today, The Hindu, WION and Business Today, at least 100 — and possibly up to 120 — scientists and engineers have left ISRO over the past year, including roughly 80 from the U R Rao Satellite Centre (URSC) in Bengaluru and about 20 from the Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre (VSSC), many of them attached to Gaganyaan and other flagship projects. In response, an internal Department of Space memo dated July 14 directs ISRO's centre directors not to accept resignation or voluntary-retirement (VRS) requests from scientific and technical staff tied to key missions until those missions are completed, sending all such requests to the Department for a final decision. ISRO chairman V. Narayanan confirmed the departures and framed the curb as protecting critical projects from sudden disruption.
key_points: At least 100–120 ISRO scientists and engineers resigned in the past year
Many worked on Gaganyaan, India's first human spaceflight mission
A July 14 memo bars centres from accepting resignations from key missions
India's booming private space startups are poaching the talent

depth.background: ISRO — the Indian Space Research Organisation — is India's national space agency, the team behind the Chandrayaan Moon landings, the Mangalyaan Mars orbiter and, now, Gaganyaan, India's first attempt to send its own astronauts to space. In 2020 India opened its long-closed space sector to private companies, and a wave of startups (rocket and satellite firms like Skyroot and Agnikul, among others) has grown up since — companies that need exactly the kind of experienced hands ISRO has spent decades training.

stories.title: Trump slaps a 25% tariff on most Brazilian goods — and Brazil says the real target is its politics, not its trade
stories.what_happened: The US announced a 25% tariff on most Brazilian imports, taking effect July 22, after the year-long USTR probe concluded Brazil's rules on digital trade, tariffs, intellectual property, ethanol-market access and deforestation unfairly burden US commerce. Notably, coffee, beef, oranges and orange juice, some oil-and-gas products and aerospace parts were carved out. A separate US forced-labour investigation could pile an extra 12.5% on top, with a decision due next week. Brazil's government said it 'repudiates the decision,' and President Lula da Silva tied it to his October election rival — Senator Flávio Bolsonaro, son of Trump ally and former president Jair Bolsonaro — who recently visited Washington.
key_points: US puts a 25% import tax on most goods from Brazil, starting July 22
Coffee, beef, oranges and orange juice are exempted — to spare US shoppers
A separate forced-labour probe could add another 12.5% next week
Brazil 'repudiates' it; Lula blames the Bolsonaro family's Washington lobbying

depth.background: A tariff is a tax a government charges on goods coming in from another country; it makes those imports pricier, which is meant to pressure the exporting nation or protect local producers. Since returning to office, US President Donald Trump has used tariffs as an all-purpose lever against trading partners. Brazil is the world's 10th-largest economy and a member of BRICS — a bloc of big non-Western economies (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa and newer members) that many in Washington see as a rival to US influence. For the past year the US Trade Representative (USTR) has been running a formal 'Section 301' investigation — a US trade law that lets Washington punish countries it judges to be treating American companies unfairly.

stories.title: Argentina reaches the World Cup final by beating England 2-1 — then unfurls a 'Malvinas are Argentine' banner that could trigger a FIFA punishment
stories.what_happened: On Wednesday, July 15, Argentina beat England 2-1 in the World Cup semifinal to reach the final against Spain. Amid the celebrations, several Argentine players — including Lisandro Martínez and Giovani Lo Celso — held up a banner reading 'Las Malvinas Son Argentinas' ('The Falklands are Argentine'). Reports say the banner was thrown from the stands and the players picked it up. FIFA's Stadium Code of Conduct bans banners of a 'political, offensive, and/or discriminatory nature,' so the display appears to breach the rules and FIFA is expected to examine it — though as of now no formal proceedings or sanction have been announced.
key_points: Argentina beat England 2-1 to reach the World Cup final (vs Spain)
Players held a banner: 'Las Malvinas Son Argentinas' — the Falklands are Argentine
The banner breaks FIFA's ban on political messages inside stadiums
In 2014 FIFA fined Argentina ~£20,000 for the exact same banner

depth.background: The Falkland Islands — which Argentina calls the Malvinas — are a small, windswept British-run territory in the South Atlantic that Argentina has claimed for nearly two centuries. In 1982 the two countries fought a 10-week war over them after Argentina's then-military rulers invaded; Britain retook the islands and about 900 people died, but the sovereignty dispute was never settled. The islanders themselves voted overwhelmingly to stay British in a 2013 referendum, yet the claim still stirs deep national feeling in Argentina, where 'Las Malvinas son argentinas' is taught in schools and printed on official maps. On top of all that sits one of football's most bitter rivalries — think Diego Maradona's 1986 'Hand of God' goal against England.

stories.title: NEET-UG leak, decoded in court: CBI says 111 of 136 questions found on a Latur tutor's phone matched the exam's secret master set
stories.what_happened: In a July 15 court filing opposing bail, the CBI laid out its strongest evidence yet: forensic recovery of 136 handwritten chemistry questions from a Samsung Fold 7 phone belonging to Shivraj Motegaonkar, who runs Renukai Chemistry Classes in Latur, Maharashtra, and a YouTube channel with about 1.68 lakh subscribers. The agency says roughly 111 of those questions matched the NTA's confidential 'master' question sets, and that the images' metadata shows they were captured on April 23, 2026 — ten days before the exam. The CBI alleges the questions were obtained at Siddhivinayak Hospital in Latur, run by paediatrician Manoj Shirure, and that about ₹5 lakh was paid to an NTA panel member/translator, P.V. Kulkarni, named as the alleged leak source. Both Motegaonkar and Shirure are in Tihar jail, with bail pleas pending before a Special CBI Court in Delhi.
key_points: CBI: 111 of 136 questions on a Latur tutor's phone matched NTA's master set
The questions were captured on April 23 — days before the May 3 exam
NTA scrapped the May 3 test; ~22 lakh students had to re-sit on June 21
Two accused sit in Tihar jail as a Delhi court weighs their bail

depth.background: NEET-UG is India's single national entrance exam for medical and dental colleges — a make-or-break test taken by around 22 lakh (2.2 million) students each year and run by the National Testing Agency (NTA). In 2026 the exam was engulfed in scandal after a leaked 'guess paper' circulating before the test turned out to match roughly 135 of the 400-plus real questions. The NTA cancelled the May 3 paper and re-held it on June 21, while the case moved from the Rajasthan police's Special Operations Group to the CBI, India's federal investigating agency.

stories.title: The world just tilted toward Beijing: for the first time in about 20 years of polling, more countries view China favourably than the United States, Pew finds — China leads in 25 of 36 nations
stories.what_happened: Pew surveyed 42,151 adults across 36 countries and territories between 8 February and 13 May 2026. In 25 of those 36 places, more people held a favourable view of China than of the United States — a reversal of the long-standing pattern. Only in six countries was the US still seen more positively than China. On the leaders themselves, respondents in 22 of the 36 countries expressed more confidence in China's Xi Jinping than in America's Donald Trump; in Germany, Greece, Italy, the Netherlands, Spain, Sweden and the United Kingdom, Xi led Trump by double-digit margins.
key_points: First time in ~20 years of Pew polling that China outranks the US on favourability
China seen more positively than the US in 25 of 36 countries — even neighbours Canada and Mexico
Xi beats Trump on personal confidence in 22 nations; leads by double digits across much of Europe
Only 6 of the 36 countries surveyed still favour the US over China

depth.background: For most of the last two decades, global-opinion polls showed the world trusting the United States more than China — America's brands, universities, movies and alliances gave it what analysts call 'soft power', the ability to get its way because others admire and want to work with it, not because it forces them. The Pew Research Center, a respected non-partisan US pollster, has tracked these attitudes across dozens of countries every year. The backdrop to this year's numbers is Donald Trump's second term: sweeping tariffs (import taxes) aimed even at allies like Canada and Mexico, an 'America First' posture, threats over trade and defence, and an ongoing US military confrontation with Iran — all while Beijing has leaned into trade deals and diplomacy in the Global South.

stories.title: SpaceX stock falls below its own IPO price for the first time — wiping out its whole rally and stripping Elon Musk of 'trillionaire' status a month after the record listing
stories.what_happened: On Wednesday, July 15, 2026, SpaceX shares fell below their $135 IPO price for the first time, sliding as much as 2.9% to about $132.15 in intraday trading — a fourth straight day of losses that erased every gain since the listing. The drop came amid a broader pullback in AI- and tech-linked stocks as investors turned more cautious. Bets against the stock have piled up: short interest (shares borrowed and sold by traders betting the price will fall) reached about 181 million shares, or 28% of the roughly 646 million shares available to trade, and those short-sellers were sitting on an estimated $3.8 billion in mark-to-market profit. Forbes pegged Musk's net worth at about $856.8 billion on Wednesday, down from a post-IPO high near $1.45 trillion — a paper loss of roughly $600 billion in a month.
key_points: SpaceX (SPCX) dipped to ~$132, below its $135 IPO price, for the first time
The stock had shot up to $225+ after last month's record $86bn debut
Musk's net worth fell from a ~$1.45tn peak to ~$857bn (Forbes)
Short-sellers sit on ~$3.8bn in paper profit; a Starship test looms Thursday

depth.background: An IPO (initial public offering) is when a private company sells shares to the public for the first time, letting anyone buy a stake and giving the founders and early backers a market price for what they own. Last month SpaceX — Elon Musk's rocket company, best known for reusable Falcon rockets, the Starlink satellite-internet network and the giant Starship vehicle — held the biggest IPO in history, raising about $86 billion by pricing its shares at $135 each. The debut was so frenzied that the stock jumped roughly 20% on its first full day and climbed above $225, and the paper value of Musk's stake briefly pushed his net worth past $1 trillion, making him — on paper — the world's first trillionaire. Crucially, SpaceX floated only a sliver of itself: only about 4% of its total shares actually trade on the Nasdaq, a very 'thin free float' that makes the price swing hard in both directions.

stories.title: Gautam Adani swears there was no 'deal': India's tycoon tells a US court nothing was promised to get his bribery case dropped
stories.what_happened: On July 8, US District Judge Nicholas Garaufis (Eastern District of New York) ordered Adani to state under oath, by July 15, whether he knew of any promise, offer or agreement behind the government's move to drop the case. In his affidavit, Adani said he was not aware of 'anything promised, offered, sought, received, agreed to, or accepted' in connection with the dismissal. He addressed the elephant in the room — the Adani Group's plan to invest $10 billion in the US — by noting it was publicly announced on November 13, 2024, *before* the indictment was unsealed, and that the Justice Department told his lawyers the investment played no role in its decision. The DOJ is asking the court to dismiss the charges 'with prejudice,' meaning they could never be refiled.
key_points: Adani files a sworn affidavit in a New York federal court
Denies any promise or deal behind US prosecutors dropping the case
The 2024 US indictment alleged a ~$250m bribery scheme
Justice Department wants the case closed 'with prejudice' — for good

depth.background: Gautam Adani, one of India's richest men and head of the sprawling Adani Group (ports, power, airports, cement, green energy), was indicted in the US in November 2024. American prosecutors alleged that he and others took part in a scheme to pay roughly $250 million in bribes to Indian officials to win lucrative solar power-supply contracts, and that the group misled US investors while raising money in American markets. The charges were a serious overhang for a conglomerate that borrows heavily from global investors — and the case has since moved toward being dropped, raising the politically charged question of why.

stories.title: Britain is about to swap prime ministers again: Keir Starmer resigns and Andy Burnham is set to take over on Monday — the UK's 7th leader in just over a decade
stories.what_happened: Starmer announced on Monday that he will resign as prime minister, and this week bid farewell to MPs in the House of Commons. Burnham — the runaway favourite to replace him as Labour leader, with party nominations closing on July 16 — is set to formally take over as prime minister on Monday (July 20), becoming the UK's seventh PM in just over a decade. According to Financial Times reports, Burnham will name current Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood as chancellor (the finance minister who controls tax and spending), replacing Rachel Reeves.
key_points: Keir Starmer has resigned; Andy Burnham is set to become PM on Monday
That would make Burnham the UK's 7th prime minister in just over a decade
No general election — Labour's own MPs, not voters, forced the change
Shabana Mahmood is tipped as chancellor; the pound rallied on the news

depth.background: Britain has churned through leaders since the 2016 Brexit vote — David Cameron, Theresa May, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss, Rishi Sunak — and Keir Starmer's Labour Party won power in a 2024 landslide. But his government grew unpopular fast. A bruising set of local-election losses in May 2026 and unhappiness with his policies emboldened his biggest internal rival, Andy Burnham, the popular ex-mayor of Greater Manchester, who engineered a return to Parliament through a by-election so he could challenge for the top job.

stories.title: Toronto's air becomes the world's worst as 100+ Ontario wildfires blanket Canada — and the smoke is now drifting over New York and Washington
stories.what_happened: More than 100 active wildfires in northern Ontario sent thick smoke over Toronto, Canada's largest city, on Wednesday. For part of the day Toronto had the worst air quality of any major city in the world, according to IQAir, a service that ranks live air pollution globally. Environment Canada, the government's weather agency, issued health warnings as the sky turned a sickly yellow. The smoke is already drifting south — reaching New York City and heading toward Washington — with air-quality alerts issued across parts of the US Northeast, Great Lakes and Midwest.
key_points: Over 100 wildfires in northern Ontario turned Toronto's sky yellow
Toronto briefly ranked the single worst air quality on Earth
Smoke is spreading south into New York City and the US Northeast
A heat dome and near-40°C temperatures are feeding the fires

depth.background: Canada's boreal forest — the vast northern woodland that rings the top of the country — burns every summer, but the fires now start earlier and grow bigger as the climate warms and dries the land. In 2023, smoke from Canadian wildfires famously turned New York's skyline orange, showing how one country's forest fire becomes another country's public-health emergency. This week a fresh outbreak in northern Ontario, fanned by extreme heat and drought, has flared into one of the season's worst episodes.

stories.title: India's market watchdog polices itself: SEBI bars its own board members from buying stocks and sets up a formal recusal rulebook
stories.what_happened: SEBI has barred its board's Whole-Time Members — its most senior, full-time officials — from making new investments in stocks and related instruments, and will now treat them as 'insiders' under insider-trading rules, formally presuming they hold unpublished price-sensitive information. Alongside that, SEBI has rolled out a formal recusal framework: officials must step away from any matter where a personal, professional, or financial conflict could arise, and the regulator will keep digital records of those recusals and make the annual data public.
key_points: SEBI's full-time members can't make new stock investments
They're now treated as 'insiders' under insider-trading rules
New code: officials must step back from any conflict
Recusals logged digitally and published yearly

depth.background: SEBI — the Securities and Exchange Board of India — is the watchdog that regulates India's stock markets: it writes the rules, investigates wrongdoing, and can punish companies and traders. Because its top officials see confidential, market-moving information before anyone else, questions about their personal investments and possible conflicts of interest have dogged the regulator in recent years.

stories.title: India moves to bring streaming films under the Censor Board — planning to force OTT movies to get clearance before release, after pulling 'Satluj/Punjab 95'
stories.what_happened: Government sources said the Centre is considering amending the IT Rules, 2021 to make Censor Board certification compulsory for films before they can stream on OTT platforms. The move follows a row over 'Satluj' (also titled 'Punjab 95'), a film based on activist Jaswant Singh Khalra, who exposed alleged extrajudicial killings by Punjab Police during the 1980s–90s insurgency. A government committee is reported to have recommended the film stay blocked for going against India's 'sovereignty and integrity,' and the Information & Broadcasting Ministry ordered Zee5 to take it down, reportedly invoking Section 69A of the IT Act.
key_points: Centre is weighing an IT Rules change to require Censor Board clearance for OTT films
Trigger: Zee5 pulled 'Satluj' (Punjab 95) after a government panel objected
It would end streaming's long-standing exemption from pre-release certification
Critics fear it curbs creative freedom; the government cites 'sovereignty'

depth.background: In India, films shown in cinemas must first be certified — and sometimes cut — by the Central Board of Film Certification, the body popularly called the 'Censor Board'. But movies and shows released only on streaming services (known as OTT, for 'over-the-top') have so far escaped that pre-release check, giving them a freer hand. That gap is now in the government's sights.

stories.title: More than 500 Rohingya feared drowned after two boats sink off Myanmar — one of the deadliest days of the whole refugee crisis
stories.what_happened: The UN refugee agency (UNHCR) and the International Organization for Migration (IOM) said two boats that left Rakhine state in late June may have capsized, with more than 500 people aboard now feared dead. One boat carried about 250 people and lost contact shortly after departure; the second, with roughly 280, is believed to have sunk on July 8 off Myanmar's Ayeyarwady coast. The voyages were made outside the normal sailing season, when the seas are far rougher.
key_points: Two boats carrying ~530 Rohingya sank after leaving Myanmar in late June
UNHCR and IOM say more than 500 people may have drowned
Most were fleeing camps in Bangladesh, heading for Malaysia
2025 was already the deadliest year on record for this sea route

depth.background: The Rohingya are a mostly-Muslim ethnic minority from Myanmar's Rakhine state. Myanmar denies them citizenship, so they are 'stateless' — no country legally recognises them. After a brutal 2017 military crackdown that UN investigators said may amount to genocide, roughly a million Rohingya fled to overcrowded camps in Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh. Trapped there with no legal right to work, study or move on, thousands each year pay smugglers for dangerous boat crossings toward Malaysia or Indonesia.

stories.title: JPMorgan is on the cusp of becoming the world's first $1 trillion bank — after the most profitable quarter in US banking history
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday JPMorgan reported second-quarter net income (profit left after all costs and taxes) of $21.2 billion — the highest quarterly profit any US bank has ever made. Revenue from stock trading jumped 86% from a year earlier to $6.0 billion, investment-banking fees rose 30% to $3.3 billion (their best since 2021), and a one-off $4.6 billion gain on its stake in Visa flattered the total. Its shares hit a record, lifting its market value to roughly $935 billion — within striking distance of $1 trillion.
key_points: JPMorgan posted $21.2bn in quarterly profit — a record for any US bank
Its market value hit about $935bn, nearing a $1tn club only tech giants have reached
Booming stock-trading and dealmaking, plus a $4.6bn Visa gain, drove the surge
A bank — not just an AI-chip maker — nearing $1tn shows how broad this bull market is

depth.background: A company's 'market value' (or market capitalization) is what all its shares are worth added together. Until now, only technology giants such as Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft had ever been worth $1 trillion — no bank had come close. JPMorgan Chase, run by veteran CEO Jamie Dimon, is America's largest bank, and a record-breaking stock market has pushed its shares to all-time highs.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal with the West goes live: the India-UK free-trade pact kicks in — cheaper Scotch and British cars coming in, near-zero tariffs for Indian exports going out
stories.what_happened: On July 15, 2026, the India-UK CETA formally came into force. Under it, the UK removes or reduces tariffs on 98.8% of its tariff lines, covering 99.5% of the value of trade, which gives roughly 99% of Indian exports duty-free entry to Britain — a major win for labour-intensive sectors such as textiles, leather, footwear, gems and jewellery, and marine products. In return, India will gradually cut tariffs on British goods: the steep import tax on Scotch whisky drops from about 150% (to 75% now, and toward 40% over a decade), and British cars get lower duties within set quotas. The deal also eases rules for services and professionals, and exempts many Indian workers on temporary UK postings from paying UK social-security contributions for three years. On the first day, India shipped out roughly $140 million of goods duty-free, with early consignments flagged off from ports including Kolkata and from Karnataka.
key_points: India-UK free-trade deal (CETA) took effect on July 15
UK drops or cuts tariffs on 98.8% of its tariff lines
~99% of Indian exports get duty-free UK access — textiles, leather, gems
Scotch whisky and British cars set to get cheaper for Indian buyers

depth.background: A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a pact in which two countries slash the taxes — called tariffs — they charge on each other's goods, making trade cheaper and larger for both sides. India has historically been cautious about FTAs, worried that a flood of cheap imports could hurt local industry, so it has struck few deals with rich Western economies. After years of on-and-off talks, India and the UK finalised the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA) — India's most significant trade deal yet with a major developed economy.

stories.title: A Bengaluru-built AI startup becomes India's newest unicorn: Emergent raises $130m at a $1.5bn valuation — about a year after launch
stories.what_happened: Emergent raised $130 million in a Series C round led by Indian private-equity firm Creaegis, at a $1.5 billion post-money valuation — a roughly five-fold jump in six months, and unicorn status barely a year after launch. Backers include Ranjan Pai's Claypond Capital, Sentinel Global, and returning investors Khosla Ventures, SoftBank's Vision Fund 2, Lightspeed and Y Combinator, taking total funding to $230 million. The company says it has crossed $120 million in annualised revenue (up 70% in four months), 200,000-plus paying customers, and more than 12 million apps built on its platform. CEO Mukund Jha describes the product as 'an engineering team in a box' for small businesses that used to run on email and spreadsheets.
key_points: Emergent raised $130m; it's now valued at $1.5bn — a unicorn in ~1 year
Its value jumped roughly 5x in six months; total funding is now $230m
200,000+ paying customers and ~$120m annual revenue run-rate
It lets non-coders build working apps by describing them in plain English

depth.background: 'Vibe coding' — using AI to build working software just by describing what you want in plain English — has become one of the hottest corners of tech, with rivals such as Cursor, Replit and Lovable raising billions. Emergent is India's fast-rising entrant. Founded only in June 2025 by brothers Mukund Jha (CEO) and Madhav Jha (CTO), it keeps most of its team and product work in Bengaluru while running a base in San Francisco — a classic cross-border Indian startup.

stories.title: India bets bigger on 'Make in India' phones: Cabinet clears a ₹62,500 crore Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme to deepen local production and exports
stories.what_happened: The Union Cabinet — the senior group of ministers led by PM Modi that takes India's top government decisions — approved a ₹62,500 crore Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme. Over five years it will hand out incentives for manufacturing, for sourcing components locally, and for R&D, with the stated aim of lifting production, exports and 'local value addition'. Officials put the targeted output at ₹39 lakh crore. It was cleared as part of a package that also included the separate ₹1.27 lakh crore Semiconductor Mission 2.0.
key_points: Cabinet clears a ₹62,500 crore, five-year Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme
Incentives target assembly, local component-sourcing and R&D — not just final assembly
Goal: lift exports and 'value addition', with an output target of ₹39 lakh crore
Part of a big Cabinet push, alongside the separate ₹1.27 lakh crore Semicon Mission 2.0

depth.background: Over the last few years India has become one of the world's biggest phone assemblers — Apple and Samsung now build large volumes here — thanks to earlier 'Production-Linked Incentive' (PLI) schemes that pay companies for hitting output targets. But most of the parts inside those phones are still imported, so India captures only a thin slice of each phone's total value. This new scheme is the next step up the ladder: push manufacturers to make more of the components locally and to do design and research here.

stories.title: Lindsey Graham, one of the US Senate's most powerful foreign-policy hawks, has died — and a scramble for his seat is already on
stories.what_happened: Graham has died, according to multiple outlets covering the aftermath and the succession fight. Al Jazeera reports that his politically inexperienced sister, Darline Graham Nordone, has inherited his office and its powers for now, an unusual outcome that has itself become a story. Meanwhile South Carolina Republicans — including Rep. Ralph Norman, Rep. Nancy Mace and William Timmons — are already publicly weighing runs for the seat, with allies criticising the speed of the jockeying.
key_points: Long-serving South Carolina Republican senator Lindsey Graham has died
Reports say his sister, Darline Graham Nordone, has stepped into his office
SC Republicans (Norman, Mace, Timmons) already jockeying for the seat
He was the driving force behind the Russia oil-sanctions bill

depth.background: Lindsey Graham was a Republican senator from South Carolina first elected in 2002 and one of the most influential voices in US foreign policy — a self-styled hawk (someone who favours a tough, often military, response abroad) and one of Washington's staunchest supporters of Israel. He was also the chief sponsor of the Russia oil-sanctions bill now in Congress, the one that has threatened steep tariffs on countries that keep buying Russian crude, including India and China. A single senator with seniority and a signature cause can shape a whole area of policy, which is why his exit ripples well beyond one seat.

stories.title: Google takes aim at Nvidia's AI-chip monopoly — pitching its own TPUs to cloud rentals, as Nvidia quietly moves to protect its turf
stories.source: The Times of India (reporting The Information's scoop)
stories.what_happened: According to a report by the tech outlet The Information, Google has been pitching its TPUs to neoclouds — including Nscale, a cloud company that has been an Nvidia partner. After learning of those talks, Nvidia reportedly offered Nscale financial incentives said to be aimed at cooling its interest in Google's chips. Nscale has publicly denied receiving any such offer, and Google has not confirmed the specific deals. So the pitch is real; the alleged incentive is a reported claim that the subject disputes.
key_points: Google is selling its own AI chips (TPUs) to startups that rent out computing power.
It's a direct challenge to Nvidia, which supplies roughly 8-9 of every 10 AI chips.
The Information reports Nvidia offered its partner Nscale incentives to stay loyal.
Nscale denies any such offer — the fight is still early and unconfirmed.

depth.background: The chips that train and run AI are the most fought-over hardware in tech, and Nvidia dominates that market — controlling on the order of 80-90% of it, which lets it charge premium prices and earn huge margins. Its customers include 'neoclouds' — newer firms like CoreWeave and Nscale that buy stacks of Nvidia chips and rent that computing power to AI companies. Google, meanwhile, has spent years building its own in-house AI chips, called TPUs (Tensor Processing Units), to run its own services, and is now trying to sell them to outside customers for the first time at scale.

sources.name: The Times of India (reporting The Information's scoop)

stories.title: India bets on urea self-reliance: the Cabinet approves a new investment policy to build seven fertiliser plants and cut import dependence
stories.source: Times of India (Semicon 2.0, Varanasi projects, rail upgrades & more: 7 big decisions cleared by Cabinet — incl. National Investment Policy for Urea)
stories.what_happened: On July 15 the Union Cabinet approved a new National Investment Policy for Urea, one of seven decisions cleared that day. According to reporting, the policy aims to add roughly 9–10 million tonnes of domestic urea capacity over about eight years by building seven new plants (each around 1.27 million tonnes), with estimated project costs of about ₹11,000 crore for brand-new (greenfield) plants and ₹9,000 crore for expansions of existing ones (brownfield). To draw in investors, it is expected to offer guaranteed government buyback of the output for eight years from the start of production. India currently imports about 26% of its urea, and roughly 85% of the gas used to make urea at home is imported; officials estimate the shift could save more than ₹10,500 crore a year in subsidies, assuming global urea prices stay near current levels.
key_points: Union Cabinet clears a new National Investment Policy for Urea
Targets ~9–10 million tonnes of new capacity via 7 plants over ~8 years
India now imports ~26% of its urea; ~85% of the gas to make it is imported
Reports say it could save over ₹10,500 crore a year in subsidies

depth.background: Urea is India's most-used fertiliser — a cheap source of nitrogen that farmers spread to boost crop yields, so it sits at the base of the country's food supply. The government keeps it affordable by selling it to farmers far below cost and paying producers the difference; that gap is the 'fertiliser subsidy,' a large, recurring bill on the national budget. India has two linked weaknesses here: it can't make enough urea and imports roughly a quarter of what it uses, and urea is made mainly from natural gas — most of which India also imports. That leaves the whole chain doubly exposed to global energy prices and shipping disruptions.

sources.name: Times of India (Semicon 2.0, Varanasi projects, rail upgrades & more: 7 big decisions cleared by Cabinet — incl. National Investment Policy for Urea)
Free Press Journal (Union Cabinet to clear new urea policy to cut import dependence)
Business Standard (2 new urea plants with 2.5 mn tn capacity to start production shortly: Govt)

stories.title: India bets big on chips: Modi's cabinet clears ₹1.27 lakh crore for 'Semicon 2.0' to build a homegrown semiconductor industry
stories.what_happened: On 15 July 2026 the Union Cabinet, chaired by Prime Minister Narendra Modi, approved ₹1.27 lakh crore (about $13 billion) for a second, bigger phase — 'Semicon 2.0' — IT Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw announced. The government says the scheme rests on six pillars — chip design, supply chain, manufacturing, packaging, research and talent — and, for the first time, will also subsidise suppliers of raw materials such as specialty minerals and gases. Officials expect it to draw around ₹4 lakh crore of investment and generate roughly ₹2 lakh crore of chip production over the scheme's life. It was one of seven big decisions cleared that day, alongside ₹25,400 crore of Varanasi highway projects.
key_points: Cabinet approves ₹1.27 lakh crore (~$13bn) for Semicon Mission 2.0
Government expects it to pull in ~₹4 lakh crore of investment
For the first time, funds raw-material suppliers — minerals, gases
Builds on the ₹76,000-crore first phase launched in 2021

depth.background: Semiconductors — the tiny chips that run phones, cars, weapons and the AI servers now driving global markets — are the most strategically prized product on earth, and their manufacturing is concentrated in a handful of places like Taiwan, South Korea and the US. India today imports almost all of its chips, a dependence that looks risky as chips become central to both the economy and national security. In 2021 India launched its first India Semiconductor Mission (ISM 1.0) with ₹76,000 crore of incentives to lure chipmakers; it has since approved 12 projects worth a cumulative ~₹1.64 lakh crore of investment, including plants by the Tata group.

stories.title: Files from India's biggest nuclear plant, Kudankulam, turn up on the dark web — a contractor admits a 'partial breach' as CERT-In investigates
stories.what_happened: In mid-July 2026, Reuters reported that nearly 19,000 files totalling about 14.3 gigabytes and tied to Kudankulam had been sitting on the dark web — the hidden part of the internet reachable only with special software — since June 11, uploaded by a hacker group calling itself 'World Lix'. Reliance Group, the conglomerate controlled by businessman Anil Ambani and a contractor on the project, confirmed a 'partial breach' of its data on a server run by third-party Indian data-centre provider Yotta. Reuters said it reviewed the documents — which are dated from 2016 to mid-2025 and purportedly include blueprints, supplier details, meeting and inspection records, equipment reviews and insurance policies — but could not verify their authenticity. NPCIL is in touch with Reliance, India's Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) is investigating, and Union Science Minister Jitendra Singh publicly downplayed the risk.
key_points: ~19,000 files (14.3 GB) tied to the Kudankulam nuclear plant leaked on the dark web
Hacker group 'World Lix' claims it; files online since June 11
Reliance Group, a plant contractor, admits a 'partial breach' on a Yotta-hosted server
India's cyber agency CERT-In is investigating; a minister played down the risk

depth.background: The Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant (KKNPP), on the coast of Tamil Nadu in south India, is the country's largest nuclear power station, built with Russian help and run by the state-owned Nuclear Power Corporation of India (NPCIL). Nuclear plants are treated as 'critical infrastructure' — facilities so vital that an attack on them could endanger public safety — so their technical details are meant to be closely guarded. This isn't Kudankulam's first scare: back in 2019 the plant's administrative network was hit by malware later linked to North Korean hackers, a reminder that even air-gapped nuclear sites are targets. Today's incident involves not the reactor controls themselves but a trove of documents held by one of the plant's private contractors.

stories.title: India clears its biggest chip bet yet: Cabinet approves 'Semiconductor Mission 2.0' with a Rs 1.27 lakh crore war chest to make more of its own chips
stories.what_happened: The Union Cabinet approved 'Semiconductor Mission 2.0' (ISM 2.0) with an outlay of about Rs 1.27 lakh crore (roughly $15 billion) — far above the Rs 76,000 crore of the first mission. IT Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw said it targets around Rs 4 lakh crore of investment, Rs 2 lakh crore of production and Rs 1 lakh crore of exports, and broadens support across six 'pillars': chip design, machines and materials, more fabrication plants (fabs), packaging/testing (ATMP/OSAT) units, and research. The same Cabinet meeting also cleared a Rs 62,500 crore second edition of the mobile-phone manufacturing incentive scheme, among seven decisions.
key_points: Rs 1.27 lakh crore (~$15bn) — well above the first mission's Rs 76,000 crore
Widens beyond factories to chip design, materials, machines, more fabs and R&D
Part of a 7-decision Cabinet package (also a Rs 62,500 crore mobile-phone incentive)
Aim: cut reliance on imported chips amid the global chip war

depth.background: Semiconductors — the tiny silicon 'chips' that power phones, cars, appliances and weapons — are almost all made in a handful of places (Taiwan, South Korea, the US, China). India makes very few and imports the rest, a weakness exposed when global chip shortages hit its carmakers and electronics firms. In 2021 India launched the India Semiconductor Mission (ISM 1.0) with Rs 76,000 crore to lure chip factories; 12 units have since been approved (cumulative investment over Rs 1.64 lakh crore) and a few — Micron, Kaynes and CG Semi — have started commercial production. Today's decision is the sequel.

stories.title: China's DeepSeek raises money at a $74bn valuation — and its founder quietly becomes the world's richest AI-startup billionaire
stories.what_happened: According to Reuters, DeepSeek is raising fresh capital that values the company at about $74 billion, ahead of a planned 'onshore' IPO — a stock-market listing in mainland China rather than in New York or Hong Kong. After this round, founder Liang Wenfeng's stake is estimated to be worth roughly $36 billion, making him the world's richest founder of an AI-model startup, and he keeps majority control. The raise comes after US AI firms — including Anthropic's chief executive — complained to the US government about how DeepSeek built and trained its models.
key_points: DeepSeek is raising fresh cash at a ~$74bn valuation
It's the run-up to an 'onshore' IPO on China's own market
Founder Liang Wenfeng's stake is now worth ~$36bn
US AI rivals had complained to Washington about DeepSeek

depth.background: DeepSeek is the Chinese AI startup that stunned the industry in early 2025 by releasing a top-tier chatbot built far more cheaply than its US rivals — and doing it despite US rules that block China from buying the most powerful AI chips. It was spun out of a quantitative hedge fund by founder Liang Wenfeng, and it became the symbol that China could compete at the frontier of AI without Silicon Valley's budgets or Nvidia's best chips.

stories.title: Cracks in Bengal's ruling party: veteran leader Madan Mitra quits TMC posts for a rebel camp as Mamata Banerjee digs in behind her nephew
stories.what_happened: Madan Mitra, a veteran TMC MLA and former minister, has quit his party posts and joined a rebel faction led by Ritabrata Banerjee. His exit came a day after the Enforcement Directorate (ED), India's federal financial-crimes agency, summoned relatives — a wife and son — linked to the party's leadership. Mamata Banerjee publicly rallied behind Abhishek Banerjee ('Abhishek has done no wrong'), accused the rebels of targeting her nephew and his family, tied Mitra's departure to the ED action as a 'setting' (a fix) with the BJP, and struck a defiant note ('To silence me, you will have to kill me'). Opposition CPI(M) leaders framed the churn as leaders being gradually absorbed into the BJP.
key_points: Veteran TMC leader Madan Mitra quits party posts, joins rebel faction
Rebels led by Ritabrata Banerjee; rift deepens ahead of 2026 Bengal polls
Follows federal ED summons to relatives of the party's top leadership
Mamata defends nephew Abhishek, alleges a BJP 'setting'

depth.background: The Trinamool Congress (TMC) is the party that has ruled the eastern Indian state of West Bengal since 2011 under chief minister Mamata Banerjee, one of India's most prominent regional leaders and a fierce opponent of the BJP-led central government. Her nephew, Abhishek Banerjee, is widely seen as the party's number two and heir apparent — a status that has long stirred resentment among older TMC hands. This rift is surfacing with the 2026 Bengal assembly election on the horizon, when internal unity matters most.

stories.title: The Gulf war goes regional: Iran strikes US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan and claims a hit on the Fifth Fleet's HQ, as Trump threatens to bomb Iran's power plants and bridges 'next week'
stories.source: BBC World (Trump threatens to bomb bridges and power plants unless Iran resumes talks)
stories.what_happened: On the night of July 14, Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) launched waves of missiles and drones at US military bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan. Tehran claimed it struck 21 US targets and destroyed four of them — including an F-35 fighter-jet hangar in Jordan and, it says, the Fifth Fleet's command centre in Bahrain — a claim Washington has not confirmed; Gulf states reported intercepting many of the incoming weapons. Hours earlier the US had carried out a fresh round of air strikes on Iran, and early Wednesday it reimposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports. In a Fox News interview, President Trump sharply escalated his rhetoric, warning 'next week comes the power plants... next week comes the bridges' and saying US strikes would continue until he decides 'it's enough' unless Iran returns to talks. It was the fourth straight day of cross-border fire; Brent crude ticked up about 1.2% to roughly $85.72 a barrel, near a one-month high.
key_points: Iran fired missiles and drones at US military bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan
Tehran claims it destroyed the US Fifth Fleet's command centre — unverified
Trump: 'Next week comes the power plants... the bridges' unless Iran negotiates
US reimposes its naval blockade of Iran's ports; fourth straight day of fire

depth.background: The US–Iran war has now run for more than four months, and a ceasefire signed in June has collapsed back into open fighting centred on the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow sea lane that carries about a fifth of the world's oil. Until now, most of the fire aimed at the US came from Iran's regional allies, like Yemen's Houthis. What is new is Iran hitting American forces directly, inside friendly Gulf countries: Bahrain hosts the headquarters of the US Navy's Fifth Fleet (the fleet that patrols the Gulf), Kuwait hosts the Ali Al Salem airbase, and Jordan hosts the Azraq airbase. Those host states had tried to stay on the sidelines.

sources.name: BBC World (Trump threatens to bomb bridges and power plants unless Iran resumes talks)
Al Jazeera (Iran launches attacks across region as US strikes continue)
ANI (Trump threatens strikes on Iranian power plants, bridges 'next week')
ABC News (Iran live updates: US carries out latest strikes, resumes naval blockade)

thread.previously: We reported the US dropping its 20% Hormuz toll but keeping a naval blockade of Iran's ships, a chemical tanker hit off Oman, and Yemen's Houthis striking Saudi Arabia's Abha airport as the war widened across the Gulf.
thread.changed: Iran has now struck US bases directly — in Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan — claiming (unverified) to have destroyed the Fifth Fleet's command centre, and Trump has threatened to bomb Iran's power plants and bridges 'next week,' pushing the war onto US allies and toward civilian infrastructure.

stories.title: Files on India's biggest nuclear plant, Kudankulam, surface on the dark web after a contractor is hacked — including alleged blueprints
stories.what_happened: A ransomware and extortion group calling itself 'World Leaks' posted a large cache of files on the dark web that it says relates to Kudankulam, including purported blueprints of parts of the facility and supplier details. The group claims the files are part of a much larger set of roughly 858,000 files taken from Reliance Group, one of the plant's contractors, and says it has published more than 19,000 of them. Reliance Group, controlled by industrialist Anil Ambani, told Reuters there was a 'partial breach' of its data on a server hosted by third-party Indian data-centre provider Yotta, and that the government has been informed, but did not say exactly what was taken.
key_points: Ransomware gang 'World Leaks' dumped 19,000+ files on the dark web
Data allegedly stolen from contractor Anil Ambani's Reliance Group
Files reportedly include blueprints of parts of the Kudankulam plant
CERT-In is investigating; a nuclear expert calls the risk 'serious'

depth.background: The Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant (KKNPP) in Tamil Nadu is India's largest, built with Russian help, and a flagship of the country's atomic-energy programme. It has been a cyber target before: in 2019 malware linked to a North Korean hacking group was found on the plant's administrative network. Nuclear facilities rely on a web of outside contractors and suppliers for construction and parts — and those third parties are often the weakest link in security.

stories.title: Japan just moved crypto into the same rulebook as stocks and bonds — clearing the path for Bitcoin ETFs and a much lower tax
stories.what_happened: On July 15 Japan's upper house of parliament, the House of Councillors, gave final approval to a law that reclassifies cryptocurrencies as 'financial products' and shifts their oversight from the Payment Services Act to the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (FIEA) — the same core law that governs stocks and bonds. The change takes effect in 2027. Japan's Financial Services Agency will now treat crypto as a regulated investment: with insider-trading bans, disclosure requirements, stronger investor protection, and bigger penalties for unregistered operators. Lawmakers separately backed a plan to cut the top tax on crypto gains from as high as 55% to a flat 20%, starting 2028.
key_points: Parliament reclassifies crypto as a regulated 'financial product'
Opens the door to spot Bitcoin ETFs for ordinary investors
Top tax on crypto gains set to fall from up to 55% to a flat 20%
New insider-trading bans and disclosure rules; takes effect 2027

depth.background: For years Japan treated crypto mainly as a 'means of payment' under its Payment Services Act — a law written to police money transfers, not investments. That left Japanese crypto traders facing some of the world's harshest taxes (gains treated as 'miscellaneous income' at rates up to 55%) and gave regulators no clean legal route to approve mainstream investment products. Japan was one of the earliest crypto hubs and the scene of huge exchange hacks (Mt. Gox, Coincheck), so it has long wrestled with how to supervise the asset.

stories.title: India's car market just had its best-ever start to a year: a record 12.74 lakh vehicles shipped in Q1, up ~26%
stories.what_happened: Industry body SIAM (Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers) said passenger-vehicle dispatches hit a record 12,73,811 units (about 12.74 lakh) in the first quarter of FY27 (April-June 2026), up 25.9% from 10,11,884 a year earlier. SIAM said passenger vehicles, commercial vehicles and three-wheelers all logged their highest-ever first-quarter sales; commercial-vehicle dispatches rose 18.3% to about 2.65 lakh units. SIAM President Shailesh Chandra credited lower GST rates, softer financing costs, a low base a year ago, and a wave of new model launches.
key_points: Passenger-vehicle dispatches hit a record 12,73,811 units in Q1 FY27
Up 25.9% from a year ago; cars, trucks and three-wheelers all set Q1 records
SIAM credits GST cuts, cheaper loans, new models and a low base
A rare upbeat signal amid high inflation and Gulf oil-price risk

depth.background: Passenger vehicles — cars, SUVs and vans — are one of the clearest gauges of how confident Indian households feel about spending, because most are bought on loans. India's car market cooled through parts of 2024-25 as high prices and costly borrowing bit. The government responded by cutting GST (the Goods and Services Tax, India's nationwide sales tax) on many vehicles, and the RBI (India's central bank) lowered interest rates to make car loans cheaper. One caveat to read the numbers correctly: these are 'dispatches' — vehicles shipped from factories to dealers — not final retail sales to buyers.

stories.title: India's E20 petrol backlash boils over: drivers say the mandatory 20% ethanol blend cuts mileage and harms older engines
stories.source: Al Jazeera (India's ethanol rush prompts anger among vehicle owners, questions for gov't)
stories.what_happened: The rollout has triggered mounting public anger, now spilling into politics and the courts. Owners of older cars and two-wheelers complain of falling mileage and problems like rough idling, hard starts and clogged filters, arguing their engines were never built for so much ethanol. Protesters gathered at Delhi's Jantar Mantar on July 5 under the slogan 'Humari Gaadi, Humara Adhikaar' ('Our vehicle, our right'), demanding a choice of fuel. Opposition leader Arvind Kejriwal escalated it, writing on July 8 to 29 automakers — reading aloud from Maruti and Toyota owner's manuals that advise E10 only — and asking them to guarantee in writing that they will compensate customers if mileage drops more than 10%; Maruti and Toyota did not immediately respond. On the other side, Nagpur's cyber police filed a case on July 15 against social-media users over alleged fake and defamatory posts targeting road transport minister Nitin Gadkari, a leading champion of ethanol, showing how politically charged the fight has become.
key_points: Since April 1, only E20 (20% ethanol) petrol is sold at India's ~90,000 pumps
Owners of pre-2023 cars and bikes report lower mileage and engine trouble
Kejriwal wrote to 29 carmakers demanding compensation for any mileage drop
Government pushes back — Nagpur police file an FIR over 'fake' posts on the policy

depth.background: Ethanol is alcohol made from crops such as sugarcane and grain; blending it into petrol lets a country burn less imported oil, supports farmers, and can modestly cut some emissions. India imports about 85% of its crude oil, so every litre of homegrown ethanol saves precious foreign currency — which is why New Delhi has pushed 'blending' hard for years, raising the ethanol share step by step. From April 1, 2026, the government made E20 — petrol with 20% ethanol — the only grade sold at the country's roughly 90,000 fuel stations. The catch: vehicles built before about April 2023, when E20-ready models began selling, were designed for petrol with only 5-10% ethanol.

sources.name: Al Jazeera (India's ethanol rush prompts anger among vehicle owners, questions for gov't)
Reuters via Yahoo (Backlash on ethanol-blend fuel intensifies in India, puts carmakers in the dock)
Outlook Business (E20 Fuel Row: Kejriwal Writes to 29 Auto Firms Demanding Clarity on Engine Damage, Compensation)
LiveMint (FIR filed over alleged fake social media campaign targeting Nitin Gadkari on E20 fuel)

stories.title: Tata wants to build ships in Kerala: the group behind Tata Steel and Jaguar seeks approval for a ₹10,000 crore ($1bn) shipyard — its first-ever move into shipbuilding
stories.what_happened: At the Kerala Samudra Mission 2026 seminar in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala Chief Minister V.D. Satheesan said the Tata Group has sought the state's approval for a ₹10,000 crore (about $1 billion) shipbuilding investment. He said the state would provide the land and is likely to clear the proposal within a month. Satheesan also revealed that Singapore's Changi Airport — one of the world's top-rated airports — is interested in aviation-related investment in Kerala. The shipbuilding plan would mark Tata's first entry into the sector.
key_points: Tata seeks state approval for a ₹10,000 crore ($1bn) Kerala shipyard
It would be Tata's first-ever entry into shipbuilding
Kerala says it will provide land and may approve within a month
Part of Kerala's 'Mission Samudra' port-led economy push

depth.background: India is a shipping giant that barely builds its own ships. It relies on the sea for most of its trade and imports most of its oil, yet Indian shipyards make under 1% of the world's ships, while China, South Korea and Japan together build roughly 90%. New Delhi has spent years trying to change that with subsidies and a 'Maritime India' plan, wanting home-built ships for trade, the navy, and jobs. Tata, India's largest conglomerate, already spans steel, cars (it owns Jaguar Land Rover), software, power and consumer goods — but has never built ships.

stories.title: India's Air India crash probe enters its final stage: investigators finish the cockpit-voice transcript and a 'psychological autopsy,' with the draft report due in October
stories.source: Reuters via Yahoo Finance (Indian investigators conducted psychological autopsy in Air India crash probe, court filing says)
stories.what_happened: In a court filing and to India's Supreme Court, the AAIB said its investigation has moved into its final stages. It has completed a transcript of the cockpit voice recorder (the 'black box' that captures cockpit audio) and carried out a 'psychological autopsy' — a reconstruction of the crew's mental state and history — and has interviewed pilots, other crew who had flown with them, technical staff, air-traffic controllers and weather officials. Still pending are an analysis of data pulled in late May from an engine-monitoring unit and a review of organisational factors; roughly six more weeks of analysis are expected. A draft final report is anticipated around October and will be shared with participating countries, including the US National Transportation Safety Board, for comment before public release. The AAIB also told the court the cockpit recordings themselves cannot be shared publicly; separately, the captain's father has sued over what he says was an implication his son deliberately cut the fuel.
key_points: Probe into the June 2025 Ahmedabad Boeing 787 crash (260 dead) moves to final analysis
Investigators have completed a cockpit-voice-recorder transcript and a 'psychological autopsy' of the crew
Draft final report expected around October; to be shared with the US NTSB before release
India's AAIB told the Supreme Court the cockpit audio itself cannot be made public

depth.background: On June 12, 2025, an Air India Boeing 787 Dreamliner (flight AI171) bound for London Gatwick crashed seconds after taking off from Ahmedabad, killing 260 people — one of the deadliest air disasters in India's history. The Aircraft Accident Investigation Bureau (AAIB) is the independent government body that investigates such crashes. A preliminary report last year noted that the engines' fuel-control switches moved to the 'cutoff' position just after takeoff — a detail that triggered intense but unproven public speculation about the crew, which the captain's family firmly rejects. Under global aviation rules, no cause is official until the final report is published.

sources.name: Reuters via Yahoo Finance (Indian investigators conducted psychological autopsy in Air India crash probe, court filing says)
Reuters via TradingView (Air India crash probe enters final stages after cockpit recorder, psychology reviews)
Oman Observer (Air India B787 crash probe enters final stages)

stories.title: Stripe and a private-equity giant make a surprise $53bn-plus bid for PayPal — a payments pioneer under siege
stories.source: Reuters (Exclusive: Stripe, Advent offer to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion, sources say)
stories.what_happened: According to a Reuters exclusive confirmed by Bloomberg and CNBC, Stripe and Advent have jointly offered to buy PayPal at a valuation above $53 billion — $60.50 per share, roughly a 28% premium to where PayPal closed on Tuesday. The offer is said to be backed by about $50 billion of committed financing from banks, and the two buyers would take equal 50-50 ownership and keep the company whole rather than break it up. Crucially, this is an approach, not a done deal: the suitors first reached out in early April, PayPal has not yet formally responded, and they are trying to push talks forward in the coming weeks. PayPal's stock jumped around 15% after the news leaked, as traders bet a bidding process could follow.
key_points: Stripe and Advent International offer to buy PayPal at a $53bn-plus valuation
Bid is $60.50 a share — about a 28% premium over PayPal's Tuesday close
Backed by ~$50bn of committed bank financing; the two would own it 50-50
It's an unsolicited approach — PayPal hasn't responded, and no deal is agreed yet

depth.background: PayPal is one of the internet's original payment companies — spun out of the eBay era, it lets people and businesses send money and pay online, and for years it was the default 'pay' button on the web. But newer rivals have crowded in: Stripe (a fast-growing, still-private company whose software lets businesses accept card payments) and Apple Pay, plus 'buy now, pay later' apps, have eaten into PayPal's growth, and its share price has lagged. Advent International is one of the world's big private-equity firms — an investor that buys whole companies, usually with borrowed money, aiming to improve them and sell later at a profit. A 'takeover bid' is simply an offer by one party to buy control of another company by purchasing its shares.

sources.name: Reuters (Exclusive: Stripe, Advent offer to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion, sources say)
CNBC (Stripe, Advent offer to buy PayPal for more than $53 billion: Reuters)
Bloomberg (Stripe, Advent Offer to Buy PayPal for $53 Billion, Reuters Says)

stories.title: China's economy grows just 4.3% — its slowest pace since 2022 — as a slump in investment and cautious shoppers drag on the world's No. 2 economy
stories.what_happened: On July 15, 2026, China's statistics bureau reported that the economy grew 4.3% in the second quarter (April-June) compared with a year earlier. That undershot economists' forecasts of about 4.5% and marked a steep cooling from the 5% growth of the first quarter — the slowest quarterly reading since the lockdown-hit final months of 2022. Growth for the first half of the year came in at 4.7%. The weakness was concentrated in investment, which slid as factories and local governments held back on new projects, while retail spending stayed subdued. One bright spot: exports jumped 27% in June (and 17.6% across the first half), lifted by booming global demand for Chinese electric vehicles and AI-related electronics — without that export surge, the slowdown would have been worse.
key_points: April-June GDP grew 4.3% from a year earlier — weakest since late 2022
Below the 4.5% forecast and down sharply from 5% in the first quarter
Investment slumped and consumers kept their wallets shut
A 27% June export jump, powered by AI chips and EVs, softened the blow

depth.background: GDP (gross domestic product) is the total value of everything a country produces in a set period — the single broadest gauge of how fast an economy is growing. China is the world's second-largest economy and, for decades, its main growth engine: when China booms it buys the world's oil, metals, machinery and luxury goods, and when it slows the whole planet feels it. But China's old playbook — pouring money into property and infrastructure — has broken down. A years-long housing crash has left developers buried in debt, households nervous about spending, and local governments short of cash. Beijing has leaned harder on exports and high-tech manufacturing (electric vehicles, solar panels, AI-linked electronics) to fill the gap, and set itself a full-year growth target of 4.5-5% — its least ambitious goal in decades.

stories.title: The company that makes the machines that make every advanced AI chip just raised its outlook — again: ASML lifts its 2026 forecast on 'extremely strong' orders
stories.source: CNBC (ASML rises 5% after hiking sales forecast for second time this year on strong AI chip demand)
stories.what_happened: On July 15 ASML reported second-quarter net revenue of €9.33 billion (about $10.9 billion), above the €8.8 billion analysts expected, and net profit of €2.92 billion, also a beat. More importantly, it raised its full-year 2026 revenue forecast to €43–45 billion, up roughly 16% at the midpoint from its earlier €36–40 billion — the second time it has lifted guidance this year. Chief executive Christophe Fouquet pointed to 'extremely strong' order intake driven by AI-chip demand, with customers such as TSMC, Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron racing to add capacity. In response, ASML said it will expand both its advanced EUV and its older DUV machine capacity by about 30%. The shares rose around 5%.
key_points: ASML raises 2026 revenue outlook to €43–45bn, up from €36–40bn
Second forecast raise this year; Q2 sales of €9.33bn beat estimates
CEO cites 'extremely strong' orders as chipmakers race to build AI capacity
Shares rose ~5%; ASML will expand its machine-making capacity by 30%

depth.background: ASML is a Dutch company most people have never heard of, yet it sits at the very bottom of the entire AI boom. It is effectively the only maker in the world of the most advanced chipmaking machines — 'extreme ultraviolet' (EUV) lithography tools, room-sized systems costing hundreds of millions of dollars each that use light to print circuits just a few atoms wide onto silicon. Every leading-edge chip — the Nvidia processors training AI, the chips inside your phone — is ultimately printed on an ASML machine. Because it has no real competitor at the cutting edge, its order book is the single truest gauge of whether the chip industry genuinely believes AI demand is real, or just hype.

sources.name: CNBC (ASML rises 5% after hiking sales forecast for second time this year on strong AI chip demand)
Reuters via Zawya (ASML raises 2026 forecast, expands capacity on AI chip demand)

stories.title: Ann Widdecombe, a well-known British politician, is killed in a 'targeted attack' — and counter-terror police take over the case
stories.what_happened: UK police say Widdecombe was killed in what they are calling a 'targeted attack' — meaning she was deliberately singled out, not harmed at random. A 28-year-old man is in custody and has been re-arrested on suspicion of the commission, preparation or instigation of acts of terrorism, and counter-terrorism officers took over the investigation after receiving 'new information'. Police say they have not formally declared it a terrorist incident but are still working to establish a motive, and have indicated they are examining a possible far-left (leftwing) political motivation.
key_points: Ann Widdecombe, ex-UK MP and Reform party figure, killed in a 'targeted attack'
Counter-terrorism police take the lead; a 28-year-old suspect re-arrested on terror suspicion
Not yet declared terrorism; police probing a possible far-left motive
Her party, Reform UK, demands 'full security' for all lawmakers

depth.background: Ann Widdecombe is a familiar figure in British public life — a former Conservative Member of Parliament who later became a leading voice on the pro-Brexit right and a member of Nigel Farage's Reform UK party, as well as a long-time television personality. Her killing lands in a decade already scarred by the murders of two sitting MPs — Jo Cox in 2016 and Sir David Amess in 2021 — which turned the safety of politicians into a raw national issue.

stories.title: China's economy grows just 4.3% — its slowest pace since 2022 and its first missed target since Covid, as weak demand at home offsets a booming export machine
stories.source: Reuters (China's Q2 economic growth cools to 3-1/2-year low as imbalances worsen)
stories.what_happened: Official data released July 15 showed China's economy expanded 4.3% in the April–June quarter from a year earlier, slowing from roughly 5% at the start of 2026 and marking its weakest quarterly growth in more than three years. Crucially, it came in below Beijing's own full-year target range of 4.5–5% — described as the least ambitious goal in decades yet still missed — the first time China has fallen short since the Covid era. The shortfall was driven by the domestic side: fixed-asset investment (spending on things like factories, machinery and construction) slumped and consumer demand stayed soft, even as exports surged. Higher oil prices from the Iran war added to the drag. Economists said the numbers strengthen the case for fresh stimulus.
key_points: April–June GDP grew 4.3% from a year earlier, down from ~5% at the start of the year
That undershoots Beijing's own 4.5–5% full-year target — its first miss since the pandemic
Slumping investment and soft consumer spending, not exports, dragged growth down
The weak read is fuelling calls for fresh government stimulus

depth.background: China is the world's second-largest economy and its biggest manufacturer, so how fast it grows sets the pace for global demand — for everything from iron ore and copper to the ships and factories that move world trade. Each year Beijing sets an official GDP growth target; hitting it is treated as a point of national credibility, and the government has almost always met it. GDP ('gross domestic product') is simply the total value of everything a country produces in a period, and China reports it quarterly. For two years its story has been lopsided: a powerful export sector — lately supercharged by the global AI hardware boom — masking a weak home economy weighed down by a property slump and cautious consumers.

sources.name: Reuters (China's Q2 economic growth cools to 3-1/2-year low as imbalances worsen)
CNBC (China posts slowest quarterly growth since 2022 as investment slumps, fanning stimulus calls)
AP News (China's economy slows to 4.3% annual pace of growth in April-June)
BBC (China economic growth falls sharply, missing target)

stories.title: France legalises assisted dying: after 14 years of debate, the National Assembly passes a law letting terminally ill adults take lethal medication
stories.what_happened: In the early hours of July 15, 2026, France's National Assembly gave final approval to the assisted-dying bill by 291 votes to 241. The law lets adults (18+) who are French citizens or legal residents, and who have a serious, incurable, life-threatening illness in an advanced or terminal stage with suffering that cannot be relieved, request lethal medication. Patients must have the mental capacity to make the choice and must confirm it after a reflection period of at least two days; they generally self-administer the drug, with a doctor or nurse assisting only if they are physically unable. Psychological suffering alone does not qualify, and people with severe psychiatric disorders or neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's are excluded.
key_points: France's lower house passed the bill 291–241, ending a 14-year fight
Incurable, advanced-stage adults can get lethal medication under strict rules
The Senate rejected it, but the National Assembly has the final say
It still needs the Constitutional Council's sign-off to take effect

depth.background: Whether the state should let a doctor help a dying person end their life is one of the oldest, most divisive questions in medical ethics — pitting personal autonomy and mercy against fears that the vulnerable, elderly or disabled could be pushed toward death. France has argued over it for more than a decade across successive governments, with reformers pointing to neighbours who already allow it and opponents (including much of the Catholic church and some doctors' groups) warning of a slippery slope. The bill had already cleared the National Assembly in earlier readings but kept hitting resistance in the Senate, France's upper house.

stories.title: The fastest-spreading Ebola outbreak ever is raging in DR Congo — 2,000+ cases and 754 dead, and there's no vaccine that works on this strain
stories.what_happened: The World Health Organization (WHO) declared this outbreak a Public Health Emergency of International Concern — its highest global alarm — on May 17, 2026. As of mid-July, the DRC had recorded 2,011 confirmed cases and 754 deaths across five provinces (Ituri, Haut-Uele, North Kivu, South Kivu and Tshopo), with imported cases reaching Uganda's capital, Kampala. The outbreak crossed 1,000 confirmed cases in just 40 days — a 2018 DRC outbreak took about 235 days to reach the same point — making it the third-largest Ebola epidemic on record and the fastest-growing yet. Because the Bundibugyo strain has no proven vaccine, responders are falling back on the classic tools: isolating the sick, tracing everyone they contacted, and conducting safe burials.
key_points: DR Congo reports 2,011 cases and 754 deaths as of mid-July
Hit 1,000 cases in 40 days — the 2018 outbreak took 235
Caused by the Bundibugyo strain, which has NO licensed vaccine or cure
Already reached Uganda's capital Kampala; WHO calls it a global emergency

depth.background: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal disease that spreads through contact with the blood and body fluids of an infected person, causing fever, bleeding and organ failure. The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), a vast, poor country in central Africa with a weak health system and ongoing armed conflict in its east, has suffered more Ebola outbreaks than anywhere on Earth — this is its 17th. Crucially, there are several different types ('strains') of Ebola virus, and the two vaccines the world has stockpiled were built to fight the most common one, the Zaire strain. This outbreak is caused by a different, rarer strain — Bundibugyo — for which no vaccine or drug has ever been licensed.

stories.title: After 23 years, America is leaving Iraq: Washington and Baghdad say every US troop will be gone by September 30
stories.what_happened: Standing beside Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi at the White House on Tuesday, President Trump said all remaining US forces will leave Iraq by September 30, declaring 'we don't think we need the military there anymore' and pointing to Iraq's growing ties with American oil companies. Al-Zaidi framed it as 'US forces will be out of Iraq' by Sept 30 'while US companies will be inside Iraq' — a swap of soldiers for business. The Pentagon confirmed it, saying the move simply reaffirms the 2024 agreement to end the anti-ISIS mission now that the group is largely defeated.
key_points: US and Iraq agree: all American forces out by Sept 30, 2026
Ends a military presence that began with the 2003 invasion
Trump: 'we don't think we need the military there anymore'
Comes as the US-Iran war puts US bases in Iraq in the crossfire

depth.background: The United States invaded Iraq in March 2003 with a 'shock and awe' bombing campaign that toppled dictator Saddam Hussein, and American troops have been in the country in some form ever since — first fighting a long insurgency, later helping Iraq crush the Islamic State (ISIS), the extremist group that seized huge parts of Iraq and Syria around 2014.
