As ISIS was beaten back, the mission shrank to a small training-and-advising role of roughly 2,500 troops, and in 2024 Washington and Baghdad already signed a deal to wind that mission down. This new announcement sets a hard finish line for a 23-year chapter.

stories.title: 15 Indian tourists killed as a boat capsizes off Vietnam's Phu Quoc — Modi condoles, Hanoi orders a probe
stories.source: NDTV (Vietnam boat tragedy: PM Modi condoles deaths of Indian tourists)
stories.what_happened: A tourist boat carrying Indian visitors capsized in waters near Phu Quoc, killing at least 15 people — 13 men and 2 women, according to Indian reporting. A survivor recounted that the vessel 'flipped in three minutes,' describing the captain jumping into the water before he did. The remains of victims from Tamil Nadu and Kerala are being repatriated. Vietnamese authorities have ordered a probe, and the Phu Quoc administration has rejected allegations that the rescue was delayed.
key_points: At least 15 Indian tourists — 13 men, 2 women — died when their boat capsized.
A survivor says the boat 'flipped in three minutes' near Phu Quoc island.
PM Modi condoled the deaths; Vietnam ordered an investigation.
Families in Tamil Nadu and Kerala grieve as bodies are flown home.

depth.background: Phu Quoc is Vietnam's largest island, a southern resort destination that has become increasingly popular with Indian tourists for holidays and group trips, with boat excursions a common activity. The victims in this incident were largely from Tamil Nadu and Kerala.

sources.name: NDTV (Vietnam boat tragedy: PM Modi condoles deaths of Indian tourists)
The Times of India (Vietnam boat survivor: 'Captain jumped, so did I')

stories.title: New York becomes the first US state to hit pause on the AI data-center boom: Gov. Hochul signs a one-year freeze on giant new server farms
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday, New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed an executive order barring construction of new large-scale data centers that draw 50 megawatts of power or more — as much as a small town uses — for up to one year. The order pauses state permitting for such projects and directs regulators to draw up new standards on energy demand, water use and environmental impact. A separate, tougher bill in the legislature (the Responsible Data Center Development Act) would freeze projects above 20 megawatts; Hochul has said she will 'further review' it before deciding whether to sign.
key_points: Executive order freezes new 'hyperscale' data centers (50+ megawatts) for up to a year
First US state to slam the brakes on the AI building spree
Trigger: soaring electricity bills and anger over power and water use
Regulators told to write new rules on energy, water and environmental impact

depth.background: Training and running AI models needs enormous banks of computers — 'data centers' — that guzzle electricity and water to stay cool. Since the AI boom took off, tech giants have raced to build ever-bigger ones ('hyperscale' campuses), and the extra demand has started pushing up power bills and straining local grids across the US. New York, like many states, had been courting these projects for the jobs and investment — until the backlash grew louder than the sales pitch.

stories.title: Supreme Court flags Patna HC order that pressing a woman's chest is 'not attempt to rape'
stories.what_happened: During that ongoing hearing, senior advocates Shobha Gupta and H.S. Phoolka told a three-judge Supreme Court bench — led by Chief Justice Surya Kant, with Justices Joymalya Bagchi and V. Mohana — that a Patna High Court had recently passed a strikingly similar order. In a 2008 case from a photo studio in Amarpur, Bihar, the Patna HC held that confining a woman, pressing her chest and trying to remove her salwar amounted only to 'outraging her modesty' under Section 354, not attempt to rape, because there was no penetration or clear overt act pointing to rape. The CJI said the bench would 'pass a detailed order dealing with the Patna High Court decision.'
key_points: Patna HC: pressing chest, pulling at salwar = 'outraging modesty', not attempt to rape
Top court's CJI-led bench says it will pass a detailed order on the ruling
Echoes a 2025 Allahabad HC order the SC already set aside
At stake: the legal line between molestation and attempted rape

depth.background: In India, sexual-assault crimes are graded, and the grade decides the punishment. 'Outraging the modesty of a woman' (Section 354 of the old penal code) is a lesser offence carrying a few years in jail; 'attempt to rape' is treated almost as seriously as rape itself and can mean many years to life. So how a court labels an act — molestation versus attempted rape — massively changes the sentence. In March 2025 the Allahabad High Court sparked national outrage by ruling that grabbing a minor girl's breasts and trying to drag her under a culvert was not 'attempt to rape'; the Supreme Court took the matter up on its own (a 'suo motu' case) and struck that reasoning down.

stories.title: India moves to ban paraquat, a deadly weedkiller with no antidote
stories.what_happened: The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare published a draft order — the 'Banning of Paraquat Dichloride Order, 2026' — in the Gazette of India on July 13, proposing to prohibit the herbicide's import, manufacture, sale, transport, distribution and use under the Insecticides Act, 1968. Crucially, this is a draft, not a finalised ban: the government has opened a 30-day window for farmers, industry and the public to file objections, with a deadline of August 13, before it takes a final decision. The stated grounds are the chemical's harm to human health, its long record of poisoning incidents, and the absence of any antidote.
key_points: Draft order proposes banning import, manufacture, sale and use of paraquat
Cited reasons: no antidote, repeated poisonings, human-health harm
Already banned in 70+ countries; widely used across Indian crops
Not final yet — public objections open until August 13

depth.background: Paraquat dichloride is a fast-acting herbicide (weedkiller) sold in India as a 24% liquid and sprayed across tea, coffee, cotton, paddy, sugarcane, maize, rubber, grapes and wheat. It is prized by farmers because it kills weeds quickly, but it is one of the most dangerous farm chemicals in use: swallowing even a small amount is frequently fatal, and there is no known antidote — no medicine that reverses the poisoning. Because of this, more than 70 countries have already banned or severely restricted it, and Indian activists and health groups have pushed for a ban for years, pointing to deaths from accidental exposure, self-harm and poisoning.

stories.title: Warren Buffett cuts off the Gates Foundation — leaving the world's biggest charity out of his annual gift after Bill Gates's Epstein ties resurfaced
stories.what_happened: In his 2026 annual round of giving, Buffett quietly left the Gates Foundation off the list for the first time, instead directing about $6 billion of Berkshire stock to four foundations tied to himself and his three children. The omission came just weeks after Gates was questioned by the US Congress over his Epstein connections. Buffett framed the change as wanting to give more to his children's foundations rather than a punishment, but he also called Gates's dealings with Epstein 'distasteful' while adding that 'people make mistakes.' Gates denies any knowledge of Epstein's crimes and has not been accused of wrongdoing.
key_points: Buffett left the Gates Foundation out of his 2026 annual donation
He instead sent ~$6bn to four foundations run by his own family
The foundation had received $47bn+ from him since 2006
Move lands weeks after Gates faced Congress over Epstein links

depth.background: Warren Buffett, 95, is the billionaire investor who built Berkshire Hathaway, a giant holding company, and long ranked among the world's richest people. In 2006 he made what he called an 'irrevocable pledge' — a public, binding promise — to give away nearly all his Berkshire stock during his lifetime, and the single biggest recipient was the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, the world's largest private charity, which funds global health, vaccines and poverty programmes. Over the years that pledge sent the foundation more than $47 billion in shares. Separately, Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates has faced years of scrutiny over past meetings with Jeffrey Epstein, the disgraced financier and convicted sex offender who died in 2019.

stories.title: A 500% tariff wall: Trump backs a Russia sanctions bill that could punish India for buying Russian oil
stories.source: ANI (Trump backs Graham's Russia sanctions bill, setting up fresh tariff threat for India)
stories.what_happened: President Trump publicly backed the bipartisan Graham-Blumenthal bill that would authorise secondary tariffs of up to 500% on goods from countries that keep importing Russian oil, gas and uranium. India imports more than 30% of its crude from Russia, making it - alongside China - a primary target. The bill has reportedly won the support of about 85 of 100 senators, past the two-thirds needed to override a presidential veto. India already faces a separate 25% US 'punitive' duty tied to its Russia links, and a US Treasury waiver that had let India buy Russian crude without triggering sanctions lapsed on June 17, 2026, leaving its purchases in a legal grey zone. Economists estimate a full 500% tariff could shave up to 0.5% off India's GDP, hitting pharmaceuticals, textiles and IT services hardest.
key_points: Trump backs a Senate bill authorising up to 500% tariffs on buyers of Russian energy
India - buying 30%+ of its oil from Russia - is a prime target
About 85 of 100 senators back it - enough to override a veto
A 500% tariff is effectively a trade wall; economists see up to 0.5% off India's GDP

depth.background: When Russia invaded Ukraine, Western countries tried to squeeze its oil income with sanctions. India (and China) kept buying discounted Russian crude - which helped hold down global prices but drew US frustration. Now US senators Lindsey Graham and Richard Blumenthal have written a bill (the 'Sanctioning Russia Act') that would let the president hit any country still buying Russian energy with 'secondary tariffs' of up to 500%. 'Secondary' means the penalty falls not on Russia, but on Russia's customers - and with the White House now backing it, India is squarely in the crosshairs.

sources.name: ANI (Trump backs Graham's Russia sanctions bill, setting up fresh tariff threat for India)
Yahoo News (Trump greenlights Russia sanctions bill that could raise India's tariffs 500%)
Open Magazine (Donald Trump backs Russia sanctions bill targeting India oil trade)

stories.title: India imports most of its oil: Hormuz 'flip-flops' whipsaw Brent near a 5% jump, and India's refiners are on edge
stories.source: Livemint (Brent crude surge, import bill, India refiners, inflation and growth)
stories.what_happened: Brent crude jumped nearly 5% on Tuesday (up about 3.8% intraday to roughly $85.92, its highest since mid-June, on top of a ~9.6% surge the day before), taking the week's gain past 10%, as the on-again-off-again US moves on Hormuz kept traders guessing. India's refiners - the companies that turn crude into petrol, diesel and jet fuel - are on edge because they must buy that pricier crude in dollars, squeezing their margins even though physical supply has not actually been cut off yet. For now Indian pump prices held steady (petrol around Rs 102/litre in Delhi), but the risk is that a sustained spike eventually forces those costs through to consumers.
key_points: Brent spiked nearly 5% intraday to a one-month high (~$86); the week's gain topped 10%
India imports ~85% of its crude - a costlier barrel hits the whole economy
Even with supplies secure for now, a bigger oil bill threatens inflation and growth
Trump's on-off Hormuz 'toll' and blockade keep prices lurching

depth.background: India buys about 85% of the crude oil it uses from abroad, most of it shipped through or near the Strait of Hormuz. So the price of Brent - the global benchmark grade of crude - flows straight into India's fuel prices, its import bill and, eventually, the cost of almost everything. This week the US-Iran war has made that price lurch: President Trump has repeatedly announced, then softened, plans to blockade Iran and to charge a 'toll' on ships crossing Hormuz, sending oil up and down with each reversal - the 'flip-flops' of the headline.

sources.name: Livemint (Brent crude surge, import bill, India refiners, inflation and growth)
Al Jazeera (Oil hits 1-month high as US-Iran fighting clouds Strait of Hormuz outlook)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Brent spiking to ~$87 (a one-month high, up ~4.8%) as the US moved toward a Hormuz blockade, then easing back toward the low-$80s after Trump dropped his disputed 20% ship-transit toll.
thread.changed: The 'flip-flops' continue: Brent whipsawed nearly 5% again to ~$86 and the week's gain topped 10%, with the focus now on India's refiners - squeezed by dollar-priced crude even though physical supply has not been cut and pump prices are, so far, unchanged.

stories.title: The Gulf war hits home: India summons Iran's envoy after an Indian sailor is killed in a tanker attack in the Strait of Hormuz
stories.source: The Hindu (MEA summons Iranian deputy envoy after Indian national killed in ship attack)
stories.what_happened: Two commercial tankers - reported as the MT Al Bahiyah and MT Mombasa - were attacked while transiting the Strait of Hormuz, killing one Indian seafarer and injuring around 10 others; 30 of the 46 crew aboard the two vessels were Indian nationals. India's Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) summoned Iran's deputy chief of mission, diplomat Mohammad Javad Hosseini, to formally lodge its protest over the attack and the death - a rebuke aimed at Tehran amid Iran's claimed strikes on Hormuz shipping. The ministry urged all sides to stop attacking commercial ships and civilian infrastructure and to take immediate steps to de-escalate the conflict.
key_points: An Indian seafarer killed, ~10 injured in strikes on two Hormuz tankers
30 of the 46 crew aboard the two ships were Indian nationals
India summons Iran's deputy envoy, protests, urges de-escalation
Indians crew a huge share of the world's tankers - the war reaches home

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz - a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman that carries about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil - has become the front line of this year's US-Iran war. Over recent days the fighting moved from threats to direct hits on commercial tankers and their crews. India has no soldiers in this fight, but it has people: Indians are among the largest pools of merchant-navy crew on the planet, so the world's oil tankers are heavily staffed by Indian sailors.

sources.name: The Hindu (MEA summons Iranian deputy envoy after Indian national killed in ship attack)
Bloomberg (India summons Iran envoy after seafarer dies in ship attacks)
India.com (India summons Iran's deputy chief over death of Indian sailor)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported, inside the broader Hormuz story, that India had summoned Iran's deputy envoy after one Indian sailor was killed, with 30 Indian crew aboard the two tankers hit in the strait.
thread.changed: The diplomatic protest is now formalised - India named the summoned Iranian diplomat (Mohammad Javad Hosseini) and pressed for an end to attacks on commercial shipping, as the human toll (one killed, ~10 injured of 30 Indian crew) sharpens Delhi's balancing act between Iran, the Gulf and the US.

stories.title: Toll off, blockade on: Trump scraps his 20% Hormuz fee but reinstates a naval blockade of Iran's ships — the chokepoint for a fifth of the world's oil
stories.source: AP News (US and Iran reignite war over the Strait of Hormuz)
stories.what_happened: One day after floating it, President Trump reversed the 20% cargo fee — the plan, which the UN's shipping agency had called legally baseless, would have taxed all Hormuz traffic, not just Iran's. But he doubled down on the military side, saying the US is 'reinstating the Iranian blockade,' which he stressed stops only Iran's own ships and its customers from entering or leaving, and that the strait would 'stay open with or without Iran.' The disruption is already severe: US officials said just 22 ships crossed the strait on July 9, against 147 the day before the war began in February. The move followed days of US strikes on Iran and Iranian retaliation across the Gulf.
key_points: Trump drops the 20% 'toll' he'd floated on all Hormuz cargo a day earlier
But he keeps a naval blockade — stopping only Iran's own ships and customers
Strait of Hormuz carries ~20% of the world's seaborne oil; no easy detour
Traffic has collapsed: ~22 ships crossed July 9 vs 147 the day before the war

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman that about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil and much of its natural gas must squeeze through — there is no practical way around it. This year's US–Iran war and a failed June ceasefire have kept the strait on a knife-edge, with tankers hit and shipping disrupted. On July 13, Trump escalated on two fronts at once: he reinstated a blockade on Iran and floated a surprise 20% 'toll' on every ship using the strait, casting the US as its paid 'guardian.'

sources.name: AP News (US and Iran reignite war over the Strait of Hormuz)
NPR (Trump drops Hormuz ship toll but pushes ahead with Iran blockade)
Fortune (Trump takes a page from Iran's playbook on the Strait of Hormuz)
CNBC Daily Open (The Hormuz blockade is back and Trump is tolling)

thread.previously: Yesterday we told you the US naval blockade of Iran's ports was set to go live at 4pm ET as Trump abandoned his 20% Hormuz toll, helping ease Brent off its ~$87 high toward the low-$80s.
thread.changed: Trump has now formally confirmed the reversal — the 20% fee is off — while re-framing the blockade as an 'Iranian blockade' that stops only Iran's ships and customers; officials also quantified how far traffic has collapsed (22 crossings on July 9 vs 147 the day before the war).

stories.title: The chip-war line blurs: US confirms the first (so far tiny) shipments of Nvidia's H200 AI chips to China
stories.source: Reuters (US official says shipments of Nvidia H200 chips to China have begun)
stories.what_happened: Jeffrey Kessler, the US under-secretary of commerce for industry and security, said on July 14 that shipments of Nvidia H200 chips to China and Hong Kong have begun — but described the amount as 'very few' and 'trivial,' declining to name buyers or numbers. The export deal opens the door for ten of China's largest technology firms — including Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance, JD and AI start-up DeepSeek — to buy the chips, with an approved purchase limit initially set around 75,000 units and reportedly rising toward 200,000. Despite the approvals, actual deliveries remain minimal so far.
key_points: A top US trade official confirms H200 chips have started reaching China
But volume is 'trivial' / 'very few' so far, the official stressed
The reversal itself dates to December: Trump OK'd sales for a 25% US cut
Ten Chinese giants — Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance, JD, DeepSeek — are approved buyers

depth.background: For years the US has restricted sales of its most advanced AI chips to China, fearing they could power Chinese military and surveillance systems — a core front in the US–China 'chip war.' Nvidia, the world's most valuable company, makes those chips; the H200 is a powerful (though roughly two-year-old 'Hopper'-generation) processor — a big step up from the stripped-down H20 previously allowed into China, but a generation behind Nvidia's newest Blackwell chips. In December, after a Trump–Xi meeting, the White House reversed course and agreed to license H200 sales to China in exchange for the US government taking a 25% cut of the revenue.

sources.name: Reuters (US official says shipments of Nvidia H200 chips to China have begun)
CNBC (US trade official says 'very few' Nvidia H200 AI chips have been shipped to China)
Bloomberg (Small amount of Nvidia AI chips sold to China via US license)

stories.title: Inflation cools to 3.5% as fuel prices fall — but economists warn the Iran war could reverse it next month
stories.source: CNBC (Here's the inflation breakdown for June 2026)
stories.what_happened: The US Bureau of Labor Statistics said consumer prices fell 0.4% in June from May — the largest monthly decline since April 2020 — pulling annual inflation down to 3.5% from 4.2%, the first drop in the annual rate since January. Falling energy costs did the heavy lifting: the energy index rose 15.7% year-on-year, down sharply from 23.5% in May, as last month's brief US–Iran ceasefire eased fuel prices. But the report is already backward-looking: it does not capture July's renewed oil spike, and economists — including a Dallas Fed research paper on the war's inflation impact — warned that a serious re-escalation could revive inflation and even raise the odds of interest-rate hikes. Brent crude had just jumped above $86, a one-month high, after Trump reinstated the Hormuz blockade.
key_points: US consumer prices rose 3.5% in the year to June, down from 4.2% in May
Prices FELL 0.4% on the month — the biggest monthly drop since April 2020
Cheaper energy, from June's brief US–Iran ceasefire, drove the fall
The catch: June's data predates July's fresh oil spike — inflation could re-ignite

depth.background: Inflation is how fast the prices households pay are rising, tracked by the Consumer Price Index (CPI). The US Federal Reserve (the central bank) watches it closely because its main job is to keep inflation low — and its chief tool is the interest rate, which it raises to cool a hot economy and cuts to support a weak one. Energy is one of the most volatile parts of the basket, so a swing in oil prices can move the whole number.

sources.name: CNBC (Here's the inflation breakdown for June 2026)
CBS News (Inflation eased more than expected in June as gas prices fell)
Fox Business (June CPI: inflation eased following recent surge driven by Iran war)
Dallas Fed (working paper: The Impact of the 2026 Iran War on U.S. Inflation)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported June CPI cooling to 3.5% with 'core' flat at 2.6% — softer than the feared 'head-fake' — sending yields and the VIX down and jumping Fed-hold odds to ~86%.
thread.changed: The forward risk is now the story: the same energy drop that cooled June came from a ceasefire that has since collapsed, and the Fed's own Dallas-branch researchers and other economists warn July's oil spike could revive inflation and even raise the odds of rate hikes.

stories.title: Meta sued by 26 ex-staff who say its AI software picked disabled and sick workers for layoffs
stories.source: Reuters via US News (Meta Used AI to Target Workers With Medical Conditions for Layoffs, Lawsuit Claims)
stories.what_happened: Twenty-six former Meta employees filed a lawsuit in federal court in Oakland, California, alleging the company used AI-powered software that disproportionately selected people with disabilities or who took medical leave for its mass layoffs. The suit says Meta ranked staff on factors like productivity and 'AI token usage' (how much they used the company's AI tools), which it argues penalised those who missed work for medical or caregiving reasons. The plaintiffs - filing anonymously and notified in May that their jobs end starting July 22 - say Meta violated anti-discrimination and medical-leave laws and failed to test its AI for bias under the new California and NYC rules. They are seeking a court order to pause the layoffs while they pursue claims in private arbitration. Meta says the claims lack merit: 'Workforce management and organizational decisions were and are made by people, not AI.'
key_points: 26 former Meta employees sue, alleging AI-driven layoffs targeted disabled and medical-leave staff
Suit says Meta ranked workers on productivity and 'AI token usage', penalising those who took leave
Claims Meta failed to test its AI for bias, breaching new California and NYC laws
Meta says decisions were 'made by people, not AI'; the layoffs are set to complete July 22

depth.background: Companies are increasingly using AI software to help rank and evaluate employees. US and state laws bar discrimination against workers who have disabilities, take medical leave, or are pregnant. In May, Meta laid off about 10% of its global workforce - nearly 8,000 people - and signalled more cuts to come. New laws in California and New York City now require employers to test automated hiring-and-firing tools for bias, making this a live legal frontier.

sources.name: Reuters via US News (Meta Used AI to Target Workers With Medical Conditions for Layoffs, Lawsuit Claims)
Reuters via Yahoo Finance (Meta used AI to target workers with medical conditions for layoffs, lawsuit claims)
Business Today (Meta sued by 26 ex-employees over alleged AI-driven layoff bias)

stories.title: IBM loses a quarter of its value (~$70bn) in a day after warning AI spending is eating its software business
stories.source: The Guardian (IBM loses a quarter of its value as shares plunge and profits falter)
stories.what_happened: IBM released disappointing preliminary second-quarter results and its shares collapsed more than 25% in a single session, on track to erase about $70 billion from a market value of roughly $273 billion. Revenue was $17.2 billion — up just 1% from a year earlier and short of the ~$17.9 billion analysts expected — with adjusted earnings of $2.93 a share versus the $3.02 forecast. CEO Arvind Krishna said that in the last weeks of June, clients abruptly shifted budgets toward servers, storage and memory — racing to lock in 'supply-constrained' AI hardware before prices rose — and away from the software IBM sells, admitting the company had 'faltered' in keeping pace. The rout spread: Microsoft, ServiceNow, Salesforce and Intuit fell between 3% and 5%.
key_points: IBM shares plunged more than 25% on July 14 — about $70bn wiped out
Q2 revenue came in at $17.2bn, up just 1% and below the ~$17.9bn expected
CEO: clients diverted budgets to scarce AI hardware, leaving IBM's software behind
The shock dragged Microsoft, Salesforce, ServiceNow and Intuit down 3–5%

depth.background: IBM is a 114-year-old American technology giant — one of the industry's original blue-chip names — that in recent decades pivoted from hardware toward software, consulting and cloud services. A 'profit warning' is when a company tells investors, ahead of its formal results, that earnings will fall short of expectations; the signal is so negative it often triggers an immediate sell-off. IBM had been seen as a steadier, cheaper way to play the tech boom — this warning shattered that image.

sources.name: The Guardian (IBM loses a quarter of its value as shares plunge and profits falter)
Yahoo Finance (IBM stock plummets more than 25% on Q2 earnings warning)
Yahoo Finance (IBM warns AI boom squeezing software budgets; shares sink in sector rout)

thread.previously: Yesterday we flagged IBM's ~24% plunge on disappointing results as a reminder that even on an up-market day, a weak earnings report is punished hard.
thread.changed: The scale and cause are now confirmed: the drop is more than 25% (~$70bn erased), and CEO Arvind Krishna pinned it on clients suddenly diverting budgets from IBM's software to supply-constrained AI hardware — a shift that also dragged Microsoft, Salesforce and ServiceNow 3–5%.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal in years goes live: the India–UK free-trade pact (CETA) takes effect today, targeting ~$100bn in two-way trade by 2030
stories.source: Livemint (India–UK CETA to boost bilateral trade to $100 billion by 2030; engineering, textiles among biggest gainers)
stories.what_happened: Having cleared ratification on both sides, CETA — and an accompanying Double Contribution Convention (DCC) on social security — formally enters into force today, July 15, 2026. From day one the UK eliminates import duties on about 99% of Indian tariff lines (product categories), wiping out tariffs as high as ~70% on processed foods, ~18% on engineering goods and auto parts, ~16% on leather and footwear, and ~12% on textiles and clothing. India, in return, phases down its duties on British goods like Scotch whisky, cars and machinery. Officials and industry estimate the deal could nearly double two-way trade from about $58bn in 2025–26 toward $100–120bn by 2030.
key_points: India–UK CETA comes into force today, July 15, 2026
UK scraps duties on ~99% of Indian tariff lines from day one
Textiles, leather, engineering goods and processed foods are the big winners
Two-way trade projected to roughly double (~$58bn → ~$120bn) by 2030

depth.background: A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a pact between countries to cut or scrap tariffs — the taxes governments charge on imported goods — so their businesses can sell to each other more cheaply. India and the UK signed the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA) on July 24, 2025, after three years and 14 rounds of talks; it is India's most comprehensive deal with a G7 (major-economy) nation. It first had to clear ratification — formal legal sign-off — in both countries before it could take effect.

sources.name: Livemint (India–UK CETA to boost bilateral trade to $100 billion by 2030; engineering, textiles among biggest gainers)
Business Standard (India–UK CETA explained: what's changing in tariffs, duties from July 15?)

thread.previously: Yesterday we flagged that the India–UK CETA — called one of India's 'most ambitious' FTAs — was set to come into force on July 15, with the UK removing or reducing tariffs on 98.8% of lines covering ~99.5% of trade value.
thread.changed: It's now live: the pact and its Double Contribution Convention on social security formally took effect today, turning a scheduled promise into real, day-one tariff cuts for Indian exporters.

stories.title: Sensex falls 561 points as surging oil and the West Asia flare-up drag banks and autos
stories.source: The Hindu (Sensex declines by 561 points on surging oil, West Asia flare-up; banks, auto shares major drag)
stories.what_happened: India's benchmark indices closed sharply lower on July 14. The BSE Sensex fell 561 points (−0.72%) to 77,055 and the NSE Nifty slid 159 points (−0.66%) to 24,052, dropping below the 24,100 mark. The trigger was a jump in oil: Brent crude, the global benchmark, rose about 4% to the mid-$80s a barrel as tensions in West Asia (the Middle East) escalated. State-owned (PSU) banks, autos and real-estate stocks were the biggest losers, while pharma stocks bucked the trend. A rupee sliding below 96 to the dollar and fresh selling by foreign investors added to the gloom.
key_points: Sensex ended down 561 points (−0.72%) at 77,055; Nifty −159 at 24,052
Brent crude jumped ~4% to the mid-$80s on West Asia tensions
PSU banks, autos and realty were the biggest drags; pharma held up
A rupee slipping below 96 and foreign selling deepened the fall

depth.background: India imports roughly 85% of the crude oil it uses, so its economy and markets are unusually sensitive to the oil price. When a Middle East conflict threatens supply and pushes crude higher, India's import bill balloons, its currency weakens, and its companies — especially those that burn a lot of fuel or lend to a slowing economy — come under pressure. That is the backdrop to today's fall.

sources.name: The Hindu (Sensex declines by 561 points on surging oil, West Asia flare-up; banks, auto shares major drag)
Deccan Herald (Markets slump on surging oil, West Asia flare-up; Sensex drops 561 points)

stories.title: T. rex 'Gus' sells for $50.1m — the priciest dinosaur ever auctioned
stories.what_happened: At Sotheby's in New York on Tuesday, a Tyrannosaurus rex skeleton nicknamed 'Gus' sold for $50,130,000 after a roughly 10-minute bidding war among seven bidders, going to an undisclosed buyer. The roughly 67-million-year-old skeleton was found in Harding County, South Dakota, and named after the late rancher Gary 'Gus' Licking, who owned the land. At 38 feet long and 12.5 feet tall with a 54-inch skull, it is one of the largest T. rexes ever found — 183 bone elements, about 61% complete by bone count and 75–80% by mass, including rarely found parts like the wishbone, a complete pelvis and both feet. The pre-sale estimate had been $20–30 million.
key_points: Sotheby's New York: $50.13 million, a world record for a fossil
A 10-minute bidding war among seven buyers; a mystery winner
One of the largest, most complete T. rex skeletons found
Scientists worry rare fossils vanish into private hands

depth.background: Fossils have increasingly become luxury collectibles, auctioned alongside art and rare watches to the ultra-wealthy — and each record sale pulls prices higher. The previous benchmark was a nearly complete stegosaurus, 'Apex', which sold for almost $45 million at the same auction house in 2024. That trend worries many palaeontologists, because a specimen bought privately can disappear from science if the owner won't allow study or public display.

stories.title: Indian-origin NASA astronaut Anil Menon blasts off on his first mission to the ISS
stories.what_happened: Menon lifted off on 14 July 2026 aboard a Russian Soyuz MS-29 spacecraft from the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan, alongside Roscosmos cosmonauts Pyotr Dubrov and Anna Kikina. He is bound for the International Space Station on an eight-month stay, with return to Earth expected around April 2027. His research includes testing AI- and augmented-reality-guided ultrasound and water-purification systems that can produce IV medical fluids from recycled water.
key_points: Indian-origin NASA astronaut Anil Menon reached space for the first time
He launched aboard Russia's Soyuz MS-29 from Baikonur, Kazakhstan
An eight-month ISS mission, returning to Earth around April 2027
His father hails from Ottapalam in Kerala's Palakkad district

depth.background: Anil Menon, born and raised in Minneapolis, is of Indo-Ukrainian descent — his father is from Ottapalam in Kerala's Palakkad district, his mother from Ukraine. A physician and mechanical engineer, he served as a US Space Force flight surgeon and pilot, helped SpaceX build its medical team, and was selected as a NASA astronaut in 2021. This is his first spaceflight.

thread.previously: Yesterday we flagged Menon's launch as a one-line notable — an Indian-origin NASA astronaut heading to the ISS aboard a Russian Soyuz, a rare feel-good India-in-the-world moment.
thread.changed: Today we decode who he is — his Kerala–Ukraine roots and career as a doctor and Space Force flight surgeon — and the full eight-month mission, now confirmed underway.

stories.title: A chemical tanker burns off Oman: the Stolt Magnesium is hit by an external blast near Hormuz as Gulf shipping comes under sustained attack
stories.source: Reuters (Chemical tanker Stolt Magnesium hit by explosion off Omani coast, manager says)
stories.what_happened: The Stolt Magnesium caught fire after what its manager described as the 'explosion of an unidentified external device' while sailing in the Arabian Sea off Oman's coast, roughly 40 nautical miles northeast of Qalhat. The blast, at about 12:40 a.m. local time, started a fire in the engine room; all seafarers were reported safe and accounted for and began fighting the blaze. The UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) - the Royal Navy body that tracks regional shipping - logged an incident at that location. It follows other strikes in the same waters: UAE-flagged supertankers Mombasa B and Al Bahyah were hit by Iranian cruise missiles transiting Hormuz, damaging the ships and killing one crew member.
key_points: Norwegian-managed chemical tanker Stolt Magnesium hit by an external device off Oman
Blast sparked an engine-room fire ~40 nautical miles NE of Qalhat; all crew safe
One of a string of Gulf shipping attacks during the US-Iran Hormuz war
Keeps Brent near $85-86, a one-month high, on the shipping-risk premium

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman through which about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil must pass, with no easy detour. This year's US-Iran war has turned the surrounding waters into a shooting gallery: over the past week tankers have been struck, crew killed, and traffic has thinned as owners avoid the risk. The Stolt Magnesium is a chemical tanker (a ship built to carry liquid chemicals rather than crude oil) managed by Norway's Stolt-Nielsen.

sources.name: Reuters (Chemical tanker Stolt Magnesium hit by explosion off Omani coast, manager says)
South China Morning Post (Norwegian chemical tanker hit by projectile off Oman amid US-Iran tensions)
TradeWinds (Stolt chemical tanker struck by 'projectile' off Oman)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported Iran claiming it hit two oil supertankers in the Gulf, and India summoning Iran's deputy envoy after an Indian sailor was killed aboard one of the tankers, as the US naval blockade of Iran's ports went live.
thread.changed: The attacks spread to chemical shipping: a Norwegian-managed chemical tanker, the Stolt Magnesium, was hit by an external blast off Oman near Hormuz and caught fire (crew safe), keeping Brent pinned near a one-month high.

stories.title: India bans imports of goods made with forced labour — a first, and a move aimed at heading off US tariffs
stories.source: The Hindu (India bans the import of goods made using forced labour, even as U.S. investigation is pending)
stories.what_happened: India amended its Foreign Trade Policy to formally prohibit the import of goods made using forced labour, aligning its rules with ILO standards for the first time. Under the change, the DGFT can launch an inquiry and then order a ban on specific products or sources found to involve forced labour, giving the government a concrete legal tool it previously lacked. The step lands while Washington is in the process of releasing its final decision on tariffs it plans to impose on countries it says have not done enough to stop the trade in forced-labour goods — making India's timing look deliberate.
key_points: India amends its trade policy to prohibit imports of goods produced using forced labour
Aligns the country with international labour (ILO) standards for the first time
The DGFT can now order specific bans after an inquiry finds forced labour
Timing is pointed: the US is about to decide on tariffs over forced-labour goods

depth.background: 'Forced labour' means work people are made to do against their will — through coercion, debt bondage, trafficking or threats — and it taints supply chains for goods like cotton, electronics and minerals worldwide. The International Labour Organization (ILO), a UN body, sets the global standards against it. India regulates what can be imported through its Foreign Trade Policy, enforced by the DGFT (Directorate General of Foreign Trade, the arm of the commerce ministry that writes import-export rules). Until now India had no explicit power to block goods simply because they were made with forced labour. This move arrives as the United States finalises a decision on tariffs against trading partners it judges to have done too little to keep such goods out.

sources.name: The Hindu (India bans the import of goods made using forced labour, even as U.S. investigation is pending)
Livemint (Explained: Why India has banned imports of goods made with forced labour)
Reuters (India to ban import of goods made using forced labour amid US probe)

stories.title: Ukraine opens a new front at sea: drone strikes choke Russian shipping in the Sea of Azov
stories.source: Al Jazeera (Russia readies to reroute exports from Sea of Azov after Ukrainian attacks)
stories.what_happened: Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces say they have struck scores of Russian 'shadow fleet' and cargo vessels in the Sea of Azov - reports cite 15 hit in one overnight raid and roughly 100-116 vessels over about nine days, including tankers, dry-cargo ships and tugs. Russia reportedly halted shipping through the Don-Azov canal and stopped traffic through the Kerch Strait after the attacks, and is preparing to reroute exports. Kyiv frames the campaign as part of a wider effort to isolate Crimea and hit the energy and export revenue that funds Russia's war. Russia has confirmed some strikes, but the full tally is Ukraine's claim and hard to verify independently.
key_points: Ukraine says it has struck 100+ Russian vessels in the Sea of Azov in about nine days
Russia reportedly halted shipping through the Don-Azov canal and Kerch Strait
The Azov handles ~25% of Russia's wheat exports - a global grain-price risk
Part of a campaign to isolate Crimea and squeeze Russia's war economy

depth.background: The Sea of Azov is a small, shallow sea in Russia's south, connected to the Black Sea by the narrow Kerch Strait beside occupied Crimea. Russia has used it as a sheltered route for oil, grain and military cargo, largely out of reach of Ukraine's earlier weapons. Over the war Ukraine has increasingly relied on cheap naval and aerial drones to strike far behind the front line. Russia is the world's largest wheat exporter, and a big share of that grain sails from Azov ports.

sources.name: Al Jazeera (Russia readies to reroute exports from Sea of Azov after Ukrainian attacks)
France 24 (Kyiv claims drone attacks on 11 Russian tankers in Sea of Azov)
The Kyiv Independent (Russia halts shipping through Don-Azov canal after Ukrainian strike on vessels, Reuters reports)

stories.title: India's direct tax collections up 16% to Rs 6.51 lakh crore early in FY27
stories.what_happened: The Income Tax Department reported that net direct tax collection (what the government keeps after paying out refunds) rose 16.4% from a year earlier to over Rs 6.51 lakh crore as of July 13, early in FY27. The standout was corporate tax, up about 22% to Rs 2.40 lakh crore, with non-corporate tax — mostly individuals — also growing healthily. On a gross basis, before refunds, collections were up around 16% to roughly Rs 7.73 lakh crore. The government has budgeted Rs 26.97 lakh crore of direct taxes for the full year, nearly 15% more than FY26.
key_points: Net direct tax collection up 16.4% year-on-year so far in FY27
Corporate tax the engine — up 22% to Rs 2.40 lakh crore
Gross collections (before refunds) up ~16% to Rs 7.73 lakh crore
Full-year target is Rs 26.97 lakh crore, ~15% above last year

depth.background: The government funds itself mainly through taxes, split into two buckets. 'Direct taxes' are levied straight on income and profits — corporate tax paid by companies and income tax paid by individuals — and you cannot pass them on to someone else. 'Indirect taxes' like GST sit on spending. Direct-tax numbers are watched closely because they move with how much companies are earning and how much people are being paid, making them a real-time read on the economy's health. India's financial year 'FY27' runs from April 2026 to March 2027.

stories.title: China expels Politburo member Ma Xingrui in a widening purge
stories.what_happened: The Communist Party expelled Ma Xingrui and referred him for prosecution after an investigation opened in April. Authorities accuse him of taking bribes, rigging official appointments, illegally accepting gifts, helping relatives buy property below market price, and 'trading power and money for sex.' He is the third member of the current Politburo to fall since 2025 — following the expulsion of top general He Weidong and an investigation into Zhang Youxia, the military's most senior officer — a striking concentration of purges at the very top of the system.
key_points: Ma Xingrui expelled from the Communist Party over graft and 'sex' charges
Third member of the ~24-strong Politburo brought down since 2025
A rocket scientist who rose to run restive Xinjiang
Signal that Xi's anti-corruption drive still reaches the very top

depth.background: China is a one-party state run by the Communist Party, and at its apex sits the Politburo — roughly two dozen of the country's most powerful officials who help set national policy. Leader Xi Jinping has waged a sweeping 'anti-corruption' campaign since 2012 that has removed hundreds of thousands of officials; supporters call it a clean-up, critics say it doubles as a tool to purge rivals and enforce loyalty. Ma Xingrui was a rocket-scientist-turned-politician: he ran China's main state aerospace and missile maker, later governed the wealthy province of Guangdong, and most recently served as Party chief of Xinjiang, the tightly-controlled far-western region.

stories.title: First clinical hint that Ozempic/Wegovy may slow biological aging
stories.what_happened: A team at UC San Diego ran a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial — the gold-standard design — in 108 adults with HIV-associated fat changes, giving about half weekly semaglutide and half a dummy shot for 32 weeks. On one epigenetic clock (called DunedinPACE), the pace of biological aging slowed by roughly 9% in the treated group; a second measure linked to age-related disease and death risk also improved, and telomere-length markers rose in nearly half of participants. The researchers, publishing in a peer-reviewed journal, called it the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging.
key_points: Small trial: ~9% slower aging on an epigenetic 'clock'
108 adults with HIV-related fat changes, 32 weeks vs placebo
'Biological age' isn't your birthday age — it's cellular wear
Early, narrow evidence — not proof the drug extends life

depth.background: Semaglutide — sold as Ozempic for diabetes and Wegovy for weight loss — belongs to a class of drugs called GLP-1 receptor agonists, which mimic a gut hormone to curb appetite and steady blood sugar. As tens of millions began taking them, researchers noticed benefits beyond weight, from the heart to the kidneys, and started asking a bigger question: could these drugs affect aging itself? To test that, scientists use 'epigenetic clocks' — lab tests that read chemical marks on DNA to estimate how fast a body is actually wearing down, regardless of age in years.

thread.previously: Yesterday we flagged this as a one-line notable — early research hinting the blockbuster weight-loss drugs might slow biological aging, a potentially huge story for health and pharma.
thread.changed: The actual trial is now out: a small UC San Diego randomized study found semaglutide slowed an epigenetic aging marker by about 9% over 32 weeks — the first human clinical hint, but in just 108 people with HIV-related fat changes, so far from proof.

stories.title: Sonam Wangchuk urged to end 17-day hunger strike over exam-paper leaks
stories.what_happened: Wangchuk has been on hunger strike at a protest site in central New Delhi since June 28, reaching his 17th day as his health deteriorates — reports say he has lost about 8.5 kg. He is fasting in solidarity with the founder of a Gen Z-led outfit, the 'Cockroach Janta Party', who is staging a sit-in demanding that Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan resign over exam-paper leaks in May that affected millions of students. Opposition leaders, along with the protest's own organisers, have urged Wangchuk to end the fast as concern grows for his condition, but he has so far refused.
key_points: Activist fasting since June 28; now 17th day, health worsening
Demand: education minister resign over May exam-paper leaks
In solidarity with a Gen Z-led youth party's sit-in
Opposition leaders and organisers press him to call it off

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a well-known engineer-turned-education-reformer from Ladakh whose life inspired the lead character in the hit Bollywood film 3 Idiots. He has repeatedly used peaceful protest and hunger strikes to draw attention to public causes. His current fast targets one of India's most sensitive nerves: exam integrity. Millions of students sit high-stakes national exams each year for university seats and government jobs, so a leaked question paper can wipe out years of preparation and hand an unfair edge to those who cheat — making paper leaks a recurring flashpoint for the country's exam-dependent youth.

stories.title: India's imports from China hit a record ~$80bn in the first half of 2026
stories.what_happened: New official trade data show India imported roughly $80 billion of goods from China in the first half of 2026 — the highest ever for that period — while exports to China rose about 37%, but from a far smaller base. Figures for April–June alone put imports near $38 billion, up around 28% on last year. The surge was led by electronics, machinery, industrial chemicals, solar equipment and components.
key_points: Imports from China reach a record ~$80bn in H1 2026
Exports to China rose ~37% but from a much smaller base
The trade gap keeps widening — India buys far more than it sells
Electronics, machinery, solar and chemicals led the import surge

depth.background: India and China are giant neighbours and rivals — their armies clashed on the Himalayan border in 2020, and relations have been frosty since. Yet India's factories, phone makers, solar plants and drug makers lean heavily on Chinese parts and materials. For years India has run a large trade deficit with China (it imports far more than it exports), and that gap has kept growing.

stories.title: India's wholesale inflation jumps to 9.87% in June, driven by mineral oils, food and metals
stories.source: The Hindu (WPI inflation rises to 9.87% in June; minerals, food items see price spike)
stories.what_happened: Ministry of Commerce data showed WPI inflation climbed to 9.87% in June from 9.68% in May, pushing wholesale prices to the edge of double digits. The rise was led by 'mineral oils' (petroleum products), food articles and basic metals. By group, fuel-and-power inflation ran hot at 27.41%, primary articles at 7.0%, and manufactured products at 7.48%; the WPI food index rose to 6.14% from 4.49% in May.
key_points: Wholesale inflation (WPI) rose to 9.87% in June, from 9.68% in May
Fuel and power prices up a steep 27.41% year-on-year
Mineral oils, food articles and basic metals led the rise
Wholesale prices often flow through to shop prices weeks later

depth.background: India tracks two different inflation gauges. Retail inflation (the CPI) measures what households pay at the shop. Wholesale inflation — the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) — measures the bulk, factory-gate prices businesses charge one another before goods reach the consumer. WPI is dominated by fuel, raw materials and manufactured goods, so it swings sharply with global oil and metal prices, and it often moves before retail prices do.

sources.name: The Hindu (WPI inflation rises to 9.87% in June; minerals, food items see price spike)
Business Standard (India's wholesale inflation rises to 9.87% in June on high food prices)

stories.title: Rupee breaches 96 to the dollar as crude climbs and the RBI's forex boost fades
stories.source: Business Recorder (Indian rupee may drift towards 96 on twin drag from oil and U.S. yields)
stories.what_happened: The rupee weakened past the 96-per-dollar mark, approaching record lows, after settling around 95.6 the previous session. The immediate triggers: a renewed round of US strikes on Iran reignited fears over oil supply and pushed Brent crude to its highest in more than a month (around $85 a barrel), while hawkish comments from a US Federal Reserve official lifted US Treasury yields — both drawing money toward the dollar and away from the rupee. The RBI had recently pulled in dollars via a special deposit drive to support the currency, but that boost is fading against the twin pressure. Traders expect the RBI to intervene to slow the slide rather than let the rupee hit a lifetime low immediately.
key_points: Rupee slid past 96 per US dollar, near record lows
A renewed US–Iran flare-up pushed oil to one-month highs (~$85)
Rising US bond yields pulled global money toward the dollar
RBI expected to intervene, but a weak rupee makes India's oil bill costlier

depth.background: The rupee's value against the US dollar matters enormously to India because it prices the country's imports — above all oil, which India buys in dollars and imports about 85% of. When the rupee falls (more rupees needed to buy one dollar), every barrel of oil costs more in rupee terms. Two forces usually push the rupee down: a rising oil price (India must buy more dollars to pay its import bill) and rising US interest rates/yields (which pull global money toward the dollar). Right now both are happening at once.

sources.name: Business Recorder (Indian rupee may drift towards 96 on twin drag from oil and U.S. yields)
The Logical Indian (Rupee slides to 95.95 against US dollar as oil prices surge amid Middle East tensions)

stories.title: New bids from Fairfax and Emirates NBD revive the stalled IDBI Bank privatisation
stories.what_happened: The stalled sale has been revived after Canada's Fairfax Financial Holdings and Dubai's Emirates NBD submitted revised financial bids for the controlling stake. Earlier bids in March fell short of the government's reserve (minimum) price, so authorities cut that price by around 20% to draw fresh interest. Officials say the revised bids are now being evaluated, with the sale potentially completed within a month.
key_points: Fresh bids from Fairfax (Canada) and Emirates NBD (Dubai) revive the sale
The government and LIC are selling a 60.7% controlling stake
Earlier March bids fell below the reserve price and stalled
The reserve price was cut ~20% to attract buyers

depth.background: IDBI Bank is a mid-sized Indian lender that the government has tried to privatise — sell to private owners — for years, as part of a broader plan to exit businesses the state need not run. The Centre owns about 45.5% and state-owned insurer LIC about 49.2%; together they aim to sell a 60.7% controlling stake with management control. The process has repeatedly stalled, most recently in March 2026 when bids came in below the government's minimum price.

stories.title: China's exports jump 27% in June as the AI boom drives chip demand - the other side of the AI trade
stories.source: AP News (China's June exports surge 27% from a year earlier as AI boom drives strong demand)
stories.what_happened: Customs data showed China's exports rose 27% from a year earlier in June - the fastest pace since 2021 and far above the ~18% economists expected, up from 19.4% in May. A customs vice-minister, Wang Jun, said AI is driving robust trade in the sector; Chinese companies exported 32 billion integrated circuits in the month. China's trade surplus (exports minus imports) widened to $125.6 billion from $105.4 billion. Exports to Southeast Asia jumped nearly 35%, and to the EU and Latin America more than 18% and 28% respectively. Imports rose 36% (partly on costlier goods amid the Iran war), and car exports topped one million vehicles in a single month for the first time.
key_points: China's June exports surged 27% year-on-year - fastest since 2021, well above the ~18% forecast
Chinese firms shipped 32 billion integrated circuits (chips) in June on AI demand
Trade surplus widened to $125.6bn; car exports topped 1 million in a month for the first time
Exports to Southeast Asia +35%, EU +18%, Latin America +28%

depth.background: China is the world's largest exporter, so its monthly trade data is a closely watched read on global demand. For two years an AI boom has driven surging demand for semiconductors (the chips that power computers and AI systems) and the hardware around them, and China both makes and re-exports large volumes of this gear - so a global scramble for AI hardware shows up directly in its export numbers. This print landed the same week IBM's shares crashed about 24% on a warning that AI-hardware spending is cannibalising its software sales - two sides of the same AI-spending wave.

sources.name: AP News (China's June exports surge 27% from a year earlier as AI boom drives strong demand)
AJOT (China's exports ride AI boom as domestic economy struggles)
China-Global South (China Exports Surge as AI Chip Demand Powers Trade)

stories.title: Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones - the war widens onto Saudi soil
stories.source: AP News via The Washington Post (Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones)
stories.what_happened: The Iran-aligned Houthis said they launched missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha International Airport on Monday, calling it retaliation for airstrikes they blamed on Saudi Arabia that hit Sanaa International Airport earlier that day. No casualties were reported; the Saudi-led coalition's spokesman, Maj. Gen. Turki al-Malki, said air defences dealt with ballistic missiles fired toward the kingdom's south. A Houthi military spokesman, Brig. Gen. Yahya Saree, warned airlines to avoid Saudi airspace 'until the blockade on Sanaa International Airport is lifted.' The UN Security Council held an emergency meeting, with a senior UN official warning the region 'cannot afford another cycle of escalation.'
key_points: Houthis fire missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha International Airport
Framed as retaliation for strikes they blame on Saudi Arabia at Sanaa airport
No casualties reported; Saudi air defences intercepted ballistic missiles
Houthis warn airlines off Saudi airspace; UN Security Council meets in emergency

depth.background: The Houthis are an armed movement that controls much of northern Yemen, including the capital Sanaa; they are aligned with Iran and have fought a Saudi-led coalition since it intervened in Yemen's civil war in 2015. Over those years they have periodically fired missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia. This strike is the sharpest such escalation seen since that coalition campaign years ago, and it plugs directly into the wider US-Iran war now raging across the Gulf.

sources.name: AP News via The Washington Post (Yemen's Houthis strike Saudi Arabia's Abha airport with missiles and drones)
Al Jazeera (Houthis: Saudi airport targeted in retaliation for strikes on Sanaa airport)
Business Standard (Houthis strike Saudi's Abha airport with missiles in sharp escalation)

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported a Houthi leader threatening a 'siege' on Saudi Arabia after strikes on Yemen's Sanaa airport, as the US-Iran war widened across the Gulf.
thread.changed: The Houthis turned the threat into action - firing missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha airport (intercepted, no casualties) and warning airlines off Saudi airspace, the sharpest escalation onto Saudi soil in years.

stories.title: Hungary's parliament votes to oust Orban's ally from the presidency
stories.what_happened: Parliament passed a constitutional amendment to remove President Sulyok, with 139 votes in favour and just 6 against, a move that would immediately end his term and clear the way to elect a replacement. Magyar framed it as 'an important step to eliminate Orban's economic and political mafia.' There is a catch: for the amendment to take legal effect it must be signed by Sulyok himself — and analysts and diplomats expect him to refuse, which could trigger a constitutional standoff.
key_points: MPs pass a constitutional change to remove President Tamas Sulyok
Lopsided vote: 139 in favour, only 6 against
New PM Peter Magyar is dismantling Orban's 16-year machine
Catch: Sulyok may refuse to sign, risking a constitutional crisis

depth.background: For 16 years Hungary was run by Viktor Orban, a nationalist who clashed repeatedly with the European Union over democracy, migration and the rule of law and cultivated warm ties with Russia. In April 2026 voters turned him out, handing a landslide to Peter Magyar and his pro-European, centre-right Tisza party, which now holds a two-thirds parliamentary majority — enough to rewrite the constitution. Tamas Sulyok, the president, is a largely ceremonial head of state but an Orban appointee, and one of the last levers of the old order still in place.

stories.title: War-risk hits the pump: Brent jumps ~10% to a one-month high as the Hormuz crisis flares, while AI chip stocks slide
stories.source: AP News (Oil prices jump following the latest fighting in the Middle East, while AI stocks sink)
stories.what_happened: After a weekend of US–Iran strikes and duelling claims over who controls the Strait of Hormuz, Brent crude — the international benchmark — surged about 9.6% to $83.30 a barrel, its highest in a month, with the gains accelerating immediately after President Trump said he was reinstating a blockade on Iranian oil tankers. As oil climbed, AI-linked shares slid: Nvidia — the most valuable US stock — fell 3.5% and was the single heaviest drag on the S&P 500, while memory-chip maker Micron dropped 4.4% (trimming a stellar 243% gain for the year). The S&P 500 fell 60 points to 7,515, the Dow dropped 138 to 52,499, and the Nasdaq sank 408 to 25,873.
key_points: Brent crude climbed ~9.6% to $83.30 — a one-month high — on July 13
Gains accelerated right after Trump reinstated the Hormuz blockade
AI winners led stocks lower: Nvidia −3.5%, Micron −4.4%
S&P 500, Dow and Nasdaq all fell as the two forces collided

depth.background: About a fifth of the world's seaborne oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz, so when fighting there disrupts tanker traffic, the price of crude — the raw material for petrol, diesel and jet fuel — jumps worldwide. At the same time, the market's biggest recent winners have been AI-linked chip stocks like Nvidia, which have soared for two years; because they have risen so far, they also fall fastest when fear returns. On July 13, both forces hit at once.

sources.name: AP News (Oil prices jump following the latest fighting in the Middle East, while AI stocks sink)
The Washington Post (Oil prices jump following the latest fighting in the Middle East, while AI stocks sink)
BNN Bloomberg (Oil prices jump and world shares are mixed as US and Iran carry out airstrikes)

stories.title: HCLTech beats estimates on financial-services strength and enters the data-centre business
stories.what_happened: HCLTech reported first-quarter (FY27) net profit up 20.3% to ₹4,624 crore on revenue of ₹34,579 crore, beating analyst estimates; dollar revenue was $3.65 billion, up 3% year-on-year. Growth was led by financial services, manufacturing and technology. It logged record first-quarter net new deal bookings of $2.4 billion and said it will invest ₹3,500 crore to enter the AI data-centre business, scalable to 50 megawatts. It kept its full-year revenue-growth guidance.
key_points: Q1 profit rose ~20% to ₹4,624 crore, beating estimates
Record first-quarter deal bookings of $2.4 billion
Entering the AI data-centre business with a ₹3,500-crore plan
Financial-services demand led the growth

depth.background: HCLTech is India's third-largest IT-services company, after TCS and Infosys, serving global banks, manufacturers and tech firms. India's IT giants are all scrambling to prove they can profit from the AI boom rather than be disrupted by it. 'Data centres' — warehouses of powerful computers that run AI and cloud services — are where much of that AI spending is going, and until now Indian IT firms mostly wrote software rather than owning this physical infrastructure.

thread.previously: In yesterday's markets roundup we flagged that IT was shining — HCL Tech Q1 profit up ~20% to about ₹4,626 crore with a ₹12 dividend — as earnings cushioned a jittery market.
thread.changed: Today we decode the full result: the estimate beat, record $2.4 billion first-quarter bookings, retained FY27 guidance, and a new ₹3,500-crore push into the AI data-centre business.

stories.title: A five-month-high trade gap: India's goods deficit widens to $30.4bn on a costlier oil bill and softer exports
stories.source: Reuters via Bloomberg (India trade deficit widens as Hormuz risks cloud outlook)
stories.what_happened: Government data showed India's merchandise (goods) trade deficit widened to about $30.4 billion in June - a five-month high, up from $28.2bn in May and far above the ~$19bn gap a year earlier, and bigger than the ~$26.5bn economists had expected. Imports jumped roughly 31% year-on-year to about $70.8 billion (a pricier oil bill, plus restocking of fertilisers, electronics and farm goods), while exports grew a slower 15.5% to about $40.4 billion. In short, India's purchases from the world grew much faster than its sales to it.
key_points: June goods trade deficit widened to a five-month high of ~$30.4bn
Imports rose ~31% to ~$70.8bn; exports rose 15.5% to ~$40.4bn
A bigger oil bill plus soft global demand drove the gap
A wider deficit pressures the rupee and adds to inflation

depth.background: A trade deficit is the gap when a country buys more goods from abroad (imports) than it sells (exports). India usually runs one, mainly because it imports huge amounts of crude oil and gold. To pay for those imports, Indians must sell rupees to buy dollars - so a wider goods gap tends to weaken the rupee. The Hormuz war has made oil dearer just as global demand for Indian exports has softened, widening that gap.

sources.name: Reuters via Bloomberg (India trade deficit widens as Hormuz risks cloud outlook)
Business Standard (India's trade deficit widens to five-month high of $30.43 billion in June)
ANI (India's trade deficit may stay elevated amid Middle East risks, oil prices)

thread.previously: On Monday we noted, inside India's market wrap, that the June trade deficit had widened to a five-month high of $30.43bn even as exports grew 15.5% - feeding the record-low rupee.
thread.changed: The focus now is on the deficit itself and its drivers - imports up ~31% on a dearer oil bill against softer 15.5% export growth - and how Hormuz-driven oil keeps the gap, and the pressure on the rupee, elevated.

stories.title: Volkswagen warns of up to 50,000 more job cuts to survive
stories.what_happened: In an internal memo, CEO Oliver Blume told staff that Volkswagen may need to cut about 50,000 more jobs on top of the roughly 50,000 already agreed — confirming for the first time it is weighing up to 100,000 reductions. Blume said the group carries a roughly 20% cost disadvantage versus comparable rivals and that, as of today, it cannot confirm a competitive future for four German plants — Emden, Hanover, Zwickau and Neckarsulm — in the 2030s. He said he prefers 'intelligent solutions' (like building Chinese-designed VWs in Europe, or defence work at idle sites) over outright factory closures.
key_points: CEO memo: another ~50,000 cuts on top of 50,000 already agreed
That's up to 100,000 jobs — near a fifth of Europe's biggest carmaker
Cause: Chinese EV rivals, tariffs, a slow and costly EV switch
Four German plants have no confirmed future in the 2030s

depth.background: Volkswagen is Europe's largest carmaker and a pillar of Germany's economy — its brands include VW, Audi, Porsche, Skoda and Seat, and the auto industry supports millions of German jobs directly and through suppliers. For decades VW's cash cow was China, but the world's biggest car market has flipped fast to electric vehicles (EVs) built by cheaper, quicker local champions such as BYD. VW has been slow and expensive making the same shift, and its profits have slumped under tariff costs and falling sales.
